**(b)** Convert to standard form, identify the **IBFS**, and discuss whether the Big-M or Two-Phase method is preferred here. *(7 Marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a) — Formulation (7 Marks)

**Decision Variables:** $x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4$ in ₹ Crores.

**Objective Function:** *(1 Mark)*

$$
\text{Maximize } Z = 12 x_1 + 15 x_2 + 10 x_3 + 8 x_4
$$

**Constraints** *(6 Marks)*

*Budget:*
$$
x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4 \leq 100
$$

*Healthcare bounds:*
$$
20 \leq x_1 \leq 40
$$

*Education bounds:*
$$
25 \leq x_2 \leq 50
$$

*Infrastructure lower bound:*
$$
x_3 \geq 15
$$

*Agriculture bounds:*
$$
10 \leq x_4 \leq 30
$$

*Education/Healthcare ratio:*
$$
x_2 \leq 2 x_1
$$

*Healthcare + Education ≥ 50% of 100:*
$$
x_1 + x_2 \geq 50
$$

*Non-negativity:* implicit in the bounds.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Standard Form & IBFS (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Standard Form Conversion** *(3 Marks)*
The two-sided bounds like $20 \leq x_1 \leq 40$ are split into two constraints each. After adding slacks ($s_i$), surpluses ($e_i$), and artificials ($A_i$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + x_4 + s_1 &= 100 \\
x_1 - s_2 + A_1 &= 20 \\
-x_1 + s_3 &= -40 \;\;\text{(after rearrangement)} \\
x_2 - s_4 + A_2 &= 25 \\
-x_2 + s_5 &= -50 \\
x_3 - s_6 + A_3 &= 15 \\
x_4 - s_7 + A_4 &= 10 \\
-x_4 + s_8 &= -30 \\
x_2 - 2x_1 - s_9 + A_5 &= 0 \\
x_1 + x_2 - s_{10} + A_6 &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: IBFS Identification** *(2 Marks)*
Because the LP has **multiple $\geq$ constraints**, the IBFS is not at the origin. Setting non-basic variables = 0, the basic variables are:
- $s_1 = 100, \; s_3 = 40, \; s_5 = 50, \; s_8 = 30$
- $A_1 = 20, \; A_2 = 25, \; A_3 = 15, \; A_4 = 10, \; A_5 = 0, \; A_6 = 50$

**Step 3: Method Preference** *(2 Marks)*
The problem has **6 artificial variables** ($A_1$ through $A_6$) in the IBFS. With such a large number of artificials, the **Big-M method** is computationally heavy because the value of $M$ must be kept symbolically very large, leading to numerical instability. The **Two-Phase Method** is preferred because:
- Phase-I minimizes the sum of artificials ($\text{Min } W = \sum A_i$), driving them out one by one.
- Phase-II then optimizes the original objective starting from a feasible basis.
- It avoids the symbolic large-$M$ arithmetic and is **numerically stable** — recommended by KTU for problems with many $\geq$ constraints.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> Students frequently lose **2 to 3 marks** in investment LP problems for the following reasons:
> 1. **Percentage-to-Decimal Mistake:** Writing the objective as $Z = 12x_1 + 15x_2 + \ldots$ when $c_j = 12$ is the return "per Crore" and the capital unit is also in Crores — this is fine, but if the problem says "12% return" and the unit is rupees, you must write $0.12 x_1$. **Always re-read the problem statement for units.**
> 2. **Reversing the Inequality:** "At least 25%" means $x_j \geq 0.25B$, **not** $\leq$. Inverting this single constraint makes the entire feasible region wrong.
> 3. **Forgetting Non-Negativity:** The default assumption in LP is $x_j \geq 0$. Writing it explicitly earns you 0.5 marks, but its absence in the answer key can be penalized.
> 4. **Skipping the Big-M Penalty:** When you add artificial variables, you **must** subtract $M \cdot A_i$ from the objective (for a max problem). Forgetting this is a **2-mark deduction** as per KTU rubrics.
> 5. **Not Verifying the Solution:** Always plug the optimal $x_j^*$ back into every constraint to confirm feasibility. A solution that violates a constraint cannot be optimal, even if the Simplex produced it (due to arithmetic error).

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Linear Programming** is the standard tool for formulating investment allocation problems with a single linear objective and linear constraints.
- The three building blocks of any investment LP are: **(i) decision variables** (amounts $x_j$), **(ii) objective function** ($Z = \sum c_j x_j$), and **(iii) constraints** (capital, bounds, sectoral relations).
- "**At least $k$%**" translates to $x_j \geq 0.01k \cdot B$; "**At most $k$%**" translates to $x_j \leq 0.01k \cdot B$.
- The **average risk constraint** is non-linear in its raw form $\frac{\sum r_j x_j}{\sum x_j} \leq R$, but is linearized by multiplying both sides by the positive denominator $\sum x_j$ to obtain $\sum (r_j - R) x_j \leq 0$.
- For maximization problems with one or more "$\geq$" constraints, the **Big-M Method** or **Two-Phase Method** is mandatory; the latter is preferred when the number of artificials is large.
- The optimal solution to an investment LP gives both the **optimal portfolio** ($x_j^*$) and the **maximum return** ($Z^*$); **shadow prices** (dual variables) further reveal the marginal value of relaxing each binding constraint.
- **Always** convert percentages to decimals before plugging into the objective; **always** verify the optimal solution satisfies every constraint; **always** report units in the final answer (₹ per year, Crores, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production Planning and Inventory Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Production Planning and Inventory Control # MODULE 1 — PRODUCTION PLANNING AND INVENTORY CONTROL ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Production Planning — Formal Definition > [!NOTE] > **Production Planning** is the managerial function of deciding *what to produce*, *in what quantity*, and *when to produce* it, such that the aggregate demand over a planning horizon is satisfied at minimum total cost (set-up, production, holding, and shortage costs), subject to capacity, workforce, and inventory constraints. In the language of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEITT415)**, production planning forms the bridge between **demand forecasting** and **shop-floor scheduling**. It operates at three hierarchical levels: | Level | Time Horizon | Decision Variables | |:------|:-------------|:-------------------| | **Aggregate Production Planning (APP)** | 3 – 18 months | Production rates, workforce size, subcontracting, backorders | | **Master Production Schedule (MPS)** | Weeks to months | Quantity and timing of finished goods | | **Material Requirements Planning (MRP)** | Days to weeks | Component-level lot sizing and order release | ### 1.2 Inventory Control — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inventory Control** is the scientific technique of determining the **optimal order quantity**, the **re-order point**, and the **safety stock** for an item so that the sum of ordering, holding, shortage, and purchase costs is **minimised** while maintaining a desired service level. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy **Production Planning = Grocery Shopping for a Family 🍚** Imagine you are feeding a family of four for a month. You don't buy rice *every day* (too many trips → "ordering cost") and you don't buy 365 kg at once (it spoils and occupies the kitchen → "holding cost"). You find a **sweet spot** — buy a fixed quantity every fortnight, keep a small buffer for surprise guests (safety stock), and accept that once in a while the rice may finish a day early (shortage/backorder). The *mathematics of inventory control* is exactly this trade-off, but solved rigorously with calculus. ### 1.4 Core Metrics & Constants - **D** = Annual demand (units/year) - **S** (or **C₀**) = Ordering (set-up) cost per order (₹/order) - **H** (or **C_h**) = Holding/carrying cost per unit per year (₹/unit/year) - **P** = Production rate (units/year) - **d** = Demand rate (units/year) - **C** = Unit purchase cost (₹/unit) - **TC** = Total relevant cost (₹/year) - **Q** = Order/Production lot size (units) - **N** = Number of orders per year = D/Q - **T** = Cycle time = Q/D (years) - **EOQ** = Economic Order Quantity (Wilson's formula) - **EPQ** = Economic Production Quantity (Production Lot Size) - **LT** = Lead time - **ROP** = Re-Order Point > [!IMPORTANT] > **Holding cost H** is **NOT** the price of the item. It is the *opportunity cost + storage + insurance + obsolescence + deterioration*, typically expressed as **i × C**, where **i** is the carrying-charge fraction (commonly 0.20 – 0.30 of unit cost per year). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Classification of Inventory Models The classical taxonomy used in KTU examinations is the **Harris–Wilson classification** based on two binary questions: 1. **Is demand known and constant, or probabilistic?** 2. **Is replenishment instantaneous, or gradual (finite rate)?** 3. **Are shortages permitted, or not?** 4. **Is the unit price constant, or quantity-discounted?** This produces a **2 × 2 × 2 × 2 matrix**, of which the six most frequently examined models are: | Model # | Demand | Replenishment | Shortages | Discount | |:-------:|:------:|:-------------:|:---------:|:--------:| | M-1 | Deterministic, Constant | Instantaneous | Not allowed | None | | M-2 | Deterministic, Constant | Instantaneous | Allowed (back-ordered) | None | | M-3 | Deterministic, Constant | Finite rate (P > d) | Not allowed | None | | M-4 | Deterministic, Constant | Finite rate (P > d) | Allowed (back-ordered) | None | | M-5 | Deterministic, Constant | Instantaneous | Not allowed | All-Units Discount | | M-6 | Deterministic, Constant | Instantaneous | Not allowed | Incremental Discount | ### 2.2 Assumptions of the Classical EOQ Model For a fair analysis, the **classical model** assumes: - Demand rate **D** is **uniform** and **known** with certainty. - Lead time is **constant** and **known**. - Order quantity **Q** is received **instantaneously** (infinite production rate). - Order cost **S** and holding cost **H** are **constant** per order / per unit per year. - Purchase price **C** is **independent** of order size. - **No shortages** are permitted. - Planning horizon is **infinite** (or long enough to repeat many cycles). ### 2.3 The Cost Trade-off — Why a Minimum Exists Annual ordering cost and holding cost move in **opposite directions** as Q varies: - As **Q ↑**, the number of orders per year **D/Q ↓**, so **ordering cost ↓**. - As **Q ↑**, average inventory **Q/2 ↑**, so **holding cost ↑**. The **total cost** curve is **U-shaped**. The minimum lies where the two component curves **intersect**, i.e., where $$\text{Annual Ordering Cost} = \text{Annual Holding Cost}$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Model | Optimal Quantity Q* | Total Cost TC(Q*) | Max Inventory I_max | |:-:|:------|:-------------------|:------------------|:-------------------| | 1 | **Basic EOQ (instantaneous, no shortage)** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$ | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$ | $I_{max} = Q^*$ | | 2 | **EOQ with planned shortages** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot\dfrac{H+S}{S}}$ | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}\cdot\sqrt{\dfrac{S}{H+S}}$ | $I_{max} = Q^*\dfrac{H}{H+S}$ | | 3 | **Production Lot Size (EPQ), no shortage** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P-d}}$ | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH\left(1-\dfrac{d}{P}\right)}$ | $I_{max} = Q^*\left(1-\dfrac{d}{P}\right)$ | | 4 | **EPQ with planned shortages** | $Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}\cdot\dfrac{P}{P-d}\cdot\dfrac{H+S}{S}}$ | $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH\left(1-\dfrac{d}{P}\right)\dfrac{S}{H+S}}$ | $I_{max} = Q^*\left(1-\dfrac{d}{P}\right)\dfrac{H}{H+S}$ | | 5 | **Re-Order Point (no safety stock)** | — | — | $ROP = d \times LT$ | | 6 | **Re-Order Point (with safety stock)** | — | — | $ROP = d \times LT + SS$ | ### 2.5 Quantity Discount Models — Decision Logic In practice, suppliers offer **price breaks**. Two discount structures exist: **A. All-Units Quantity Discount** - If you order $\geq Q_i$ units, the unit price for **the entire batch** drops to $C_i$. - The decision procedure is the standard KTU algorithm: 1. Compute $Q_i^* = \sqrt{2DS / (i \cdot C_i)}$ for each price level $i$. 2. If $Q_i^*$ falls in the price bracket of $C_i$, it is a **candidate**. 3. If $Q_i^*$ falls in a *lower* bracket, the candidate for that level is the **break-point quantity** $Q_b$ (the minimum quantity to enjoy that price). 4. Among all candidates, choose the one with the **lowest total cost** $TC_i = D \cdot C_i + S \cdot D/Q + (H_i \cdot Q)/2$, where $H_i = i \cdot C_i$. **B. Incremental Discount** - The discount applies **only to the units beyond the break-point**, not the whole batch. - More common in real procurement; less frequent in KTU questions. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Manufacturing**: deciding lot sizes in a job-shop (reduces set-up cost while keeping WIP low). - **Retail & E-commerce**: Amazon's warehouse replenishment uses EPQ with stochastic demand. - **Healthcare**: blood-bank inventory uses EOQ with perishable items. - **Defence Logistics**: EOQ with quantity discounts governs ammunition procurement. - **Civil Engineering**: cement and steel inventory at construction sites. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Classical EOQ (Wilson's Formula) We define the **Total Relevant Cost** per year as the sum of three components (purchase cost is *independent* of Q in the basic model, so it is often dropped from the optimisation): $$\begin{aligned} TC(Q) &= \underbrace{\text{Ordering Cost}}_{S \cdot (D/Q)} \;+\; \underbrace{\text{Holding Cost}}_{(H \cdot Q)/2} \\ &= \frac{SD}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2} \end{aligned}$$ We minimise TC(Q) with respect to Q using calculus. Differentiate w.r.t. Q: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= -\frac{SD}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0 \end{aligned}$$ Solving for Q: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{H}{2} &= \frac{SD}{Q^{2}} \\ Q^{2} &= \frac{2SD}{H} \\ Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}} \end{aligned}$$ To **verify it is a minimum**, take the second derivative: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d^{2}\,TC}{dQ^{2}} &= \frac{2SD}{Q^{3}} > 0 \end{aligned}$$ Since the second derivative is positive, $Q^*$ is indeed a global minimum (the cost curve is convex). **Substitute** $Q^*$ back into TC(Q): $$\begin{aligned} TC^{*} &= \frac{SD}{\sqrt{2SD/H}} + \frac{H}{2}\sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H}} \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{SDH}}{\sqrt{2}} + \frac{\sqrt{SDH}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ &= \frac{2\sqrt{SDH}}{\sqrt{2}} = \sqrt{2SDH} \end{aligned}$$ **Number of orders per year** and **Time between orders**: $$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \sqrt{\frac{DH}{2S}} \qquad ; \qquad T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \sqrt{\frac{2S}{DH}}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — EOQ with Planned Shortages (Back-Orders) When shortages are permitted, inventory is allowed to fall below zero. Define: - $Q$ = order quantity - $S_b$ = shortage cost per unit per year (sometimes written as $C_s$ or $K$) - $I_{max}$ = maximum inventory just after replenishment - $Q - I_{max}$ = maximum back-order level Inventory builds up from back-order to $I_{max}$ over time $t_1 = I_{max}/D$, then depletes from $I_{max}$ to zero over time $t_2 = I_{max}/D$ (symmetric if holding and shortage costs are the only differing factors, but here they differ). A more careful derivation: in the shortage model, holding cost applies for time $t_1$ and shortage cost applies for time $t_2$ where $t_1 + t_2 = Q/D$. The average inventory and average back-order are: $$\text{Average Inventory} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} \cdot \frac{t_1}{T} \quad ; \quad \text{Average Back-order} = \frac{(Q - I_{max})}{2} \cdot \frac{t_2}{T}$$ A cleaner, KTU-standard derivation uses a *virtual* inventory that goes negative. Holding cost is charged on the **positive area** and shortage cost on the **negative area**. The total cost becomes: $$\begin{aligned} TC(Q, I_{max}) &= \underbrace{\frac{SD}{Q}}_{\text{Ordering}} + \underbrace{\frac{H \cdot I_{max}^{2}}{2Q}}_{\text{Holding}} + \underbrace{\frac{S_b(Q - I_{max})^{2}}{2Q}}_{\text{Shortage}} \end{aligned}$$ (Here $S_b$ is written as $S$ in the KTU Formula Sheet to mirror standard notation; the symbol is *shortage cost*, distinct from *ordering cost* which we now write as $C_o$.) Apply $\partial TC / \partial I_{max} = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{\partial TC}{\partial I_{max}} &= \frac{H \cdot I_{max}}{Q} - \frac{S_b(Q - I_{max})}{Q} = 0 \\ H \cdot I_{max} &= S_b(Q - I_{max}) \\ I_{max} &= \frac{S_b \cdot Q}{H + S_b} \end{aligned}$$ Substitute into TC and apply $\partial TC / \partial Q = 0$: $$\begin{aligned} TC &= \frac{SD}{Q} + \frac{H}{2Q}\left(\frac{S_b Q}{H+S_b}\right)^{2} + \frac{S_b}{2Q}\left(Q - \frac{S_b Q}{H+S_b}\right)^{2} \\ &= \frac{SD}{Q} + \frac{H S_b^{2}Q}{2(H+S_b)^{2}} + \frac{S_b H^{2}Q}{2(H+S_b)^{2}} \\ &= \frac{SD}{Q} + \frac{H S_b Q}{2(H+S_b)} \end{aligned}$$ Differentiate w.r.t. Q and set to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= -\frac{SD}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H S_b}{2(H+S_b)} = 0 \\ Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2SD(H+S_b)}{H S_b}} = \sqrt{\frac{2SD}{H} \cdot \frac{H+S_b}{S_b}} \end{aligned}$$ The total cost at the optimum is: $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2SDH \cdot \frac{S_b}{H+S_b}}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Economic Production Quantity (EPQ) — Finite Production Rate The shop produces at finite rate **P** (units/year) and consumes at rate **d** (units/year). During production, inventory builds up at rate $(P - d)$. After production stops, inventory depletes at rate $d$. - Time of one cycle: $T = Q/d$. - Production time: $t_p = Q/P$. - Maximum inventory: $I_{max} = (P - d) \cdot t_p = Q(1 - d/P)$. Average inventory over the cycle (only positive part counted): $$\bar{I} = \frac{I_{max}}{2} = \frac{Q}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right)$$ Total annual cost: $$TC(Q) = \frac{C_o D}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right)$$ Differentiate w.r.t. Q and set to zero: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d\,TC}{dQ} &= -\frac{C_o D}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2}\left(1-\frac{d}{P}\right) = 0 \\ Q^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 C_o D}{H \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{P}\right)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_o D}{H} \cdot \frac{P}{P-d}} \end{aligned}$$ Total minimum cost: $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2 C_o D H \left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > When $P \to \infty$ (instantaneous replenishment), the factor $P/(P-d) \to 1$ and EPQ collapses to **classical EOQ** — a beautiful sanity check. ### 3.4 Worked Example — Classical EOQ > A factory uses **24,000 units/year** of a component. The cost of placing one order is **₹150** and the holding cost is **₹6 per unit per year**. Find (a) EOQ, (b) optimal number of orders, (c) time between orders, (d) total minimum cost. Given: $D = 24{,}000$ units/yr, $S = 150$ ₹/order, $H = 6$ ₹/unit/yr. **(a) EOQ:** $$Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150}{6}} = \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000} = 1095.45 \text{ units}$$ **(b) Number of orders per year:** $$N^{*} = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{24{,}000}{1095.45} = 21.91 \approx 22 \text{ orders/yr}$$ **(c) Cycle time:** $$T^{*} = \frac{Q^{*}}{D} = \frac{1095.45}{24{,}000} = 0.04564 \text{ yr} \approx 16.67 \text{ days}$$ **(d) Total minimum cost:** $$TC^{*} = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 24{,}000 \times 150 \times 6} = \sqrt{43{,}200{,}000} \approx 6572.67 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ ### 3.5 Worked Example — EOQ with Quantity Discount > A retailer needs **2,000 units/year**. Ordering cost is **₹400/order**, holding cost = **20% of unit cost**. The supplier's price schedule is: > > | Quantity Bracket | Unit Price (₹) | > |:----------------:|:--------------:| > | 1 – 499 | 50 | > | 500 – 999 | 48 | > | 1,000 and above | 46 | **Step 1 — Holding cost at each level:** $H_1 = 0.20 \times 50 = 10$; $H_2 = 0.20 \times 48 = 9.6$; $H_3 = 0.20 \times 46 = 9.2$. **Step 2 — EOQ at each level (ignoring quantity restrictions):** $$\begin{aligned} Q_1^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2000 \times 400}{10}} = \sqrt{160{,}000} = 400 \text{ units} \quad \checkmark \text{ (in bracket 1)}\\ Q_2^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2000 \times 400}{9.6}} = \sqrt{166{,}667} = 408.25 \text{ units} \quad \times \text{ (NOT in bracket 2)}\\ Q_3^{*} &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 2000 \times 400}{9.2}} = \sqrt{173{,}913} = 416.99 \text{ units} \quad \times \text{ (NOT in bracket 3)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Adjusted candidates:** - Level 1: $Q = 400$ ✓ - Level 2: must order $\geq 500$ → use break-point $Q = 500$ - Level 3: must order $\geq 1{,}000$ → use break-point $Q = 1{,}000$ **Step 4 — Compute total cost for each candidate:** $$\begin{aligned} TC_i &= D \cdot C_i + \frac{S \cdot D}{Q} + \frac{H_i \cdot Q}{2}\\[4pt] TC_1 &= 2000(50) + \frac{400 \times 2000}{400} + \frac{10 \times 400}{2} = 100{,}000 + 2{,}000 + 2{,}000 = 104{,}000 \\[4pt] TC_2 &= 2000(48) + \frac{400 \times 2000}{500} + \frac{9.6 \times 500}{2} = 96{,}000 + 1{,}600 + 2{,}400 = 100{,}000 \\[4pt] TC_3 &= 2000(46) + \frac{400 \times 2000}{1000} + \frac{9.2 \times 1000}{2} = 92{,}000 + 800 + 4{,}600 = 97{,}400 \end{aligned}$$ **Decision:** The minimum total cost is **₹97,400** at $Q^* = 1{,}000$ units. Hence the retailer should order **1,000 units at a time** at ₹46 per unit. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Inventory Level vs Time — Basic EOQ Model (Instantaneous Replenishment, No Shortage) The inventory starts at $Q$ after a fresh order, decreases linearly at rate $D$ (units per year) to zero, then jumps back to $Q$ on the next order. The cycle repeats with period $T = Q/D$. ```mermaid graph LR nodeA["Cycle 1 Begins
Inventory = Q"] --> nodeB["Inventory depletes
at rate D per year"] nodeB --> nodeC["Inventory reaches 0
at time T1 = Q/D"] nodeC --> nodeD["New order placed
Instantaneous receipt"] nodeD --> nodeE["Inventory jumps to Q
Cycle 2 begins"] nodeE --> nodeF["Inventory depletes
at rate D per year"] nodeF --> nodeG["Inventory reaches 0
at time T2 = 2Q/D"] G_END["Steady state
Repeats every Q/D years"] nodeG --> G_END G_END -.-> nodeA classDef peak fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef zero fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 class nodeA,nodeE peak class nodeC,nodeG zero ``` ### 4.2 Inventory Level vs Time — EPQ Model (Finite Production, No Shortage) Here, inventory ramps up at rate $(P - d)$ during the production run $[0, t_p]$, reaches $I_{max} = Q(1 - d/P)$, and then falls at rate $d$ during the non-production phase. ```mermaid graph LR stepA["Cycle start
Inventory = 0"] --> stepB["Production begins
Net build-up at rate P minus d"] stepB --> stepC["Production stops
at time t sub p = Q / P
Peak inventory Imax reached"] stepC --> stepD["Inventory depletes
at rate d"] stepD --> stepE["Inventory reaches 0
at time T = Q / d
Cycle ends"] stepE --> stepF["Next cycle
Production resumes"] classDef build fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000 classDef peak fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef deplete fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef reset fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 class stepA,stepF reset class stepB build class stepC peak class stepD deplete ``` ### 4.3 Inventory Level vs Time — EOQ with Backorders Now the inventory is allowed to drop **below zero**. The cycle has four phases: (i) backorder clearance, (ii) positive inventory build-up, (iii) positive inventory depletion, (iv) backorder accumulation. The area under the positive axis is charged holding cost, the negative area is charged shortage cost. ```mermaid graph LR p1["Phase 1
Backorder clearance
Inventory rises from negative to 0"] --> p2["Phase 2
Positive build-up
Inventory rises to Imax"] p2 --> p3["Phase 3
Positive depletion
Inventory falls from Imax to 0"] p3 --> p4["Phase 4
Backorder accumulation
Inventory falls to negative Q minus Imax"] p4 --> p1 classDef pos fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef neg fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 classDef peak fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef trough fill:#FF6347,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff class p1,p3 pos class p4 neg class p2 peak ``` ### 4.4 Functional Architecture of an Inventory Control Module ```mermaid graph TD A[Demand Data Input] --> B[Forecasting Module] B --> C[Aggregate Plan] C --> D[Master Production Schedule] D --> E[Lot Sizing Engine] E --> F{Discount Applicable?} F -->|Yes| G[Quantity Discount Solver] F -->|No| H[EOQ / EPQ Solver] G --> I[Order Release] H --> I[Order Release] I --> J[Shop Floor Production] J --> K[Inventory Update] K --> L{Reorder Point
Reached?} L -->|Yes| M[Trigger New Order] L -->|No| N[Continue Monitoring] M --> I classDef input fill:#E6F2FF,stroke:#003366,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#8B6914,color:#000 classDef process fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#006400,color:#000 classDef control fill:#FFCCCB,stroke:#8B0000,color:#000 class A,B,C,D input class F,L decision class E,G,H,I,J,K process class M,N control ``` ### 4.5 ABC Analysis — Inventory Classification ```mermaid graph LR A[Total Inventory Items] --> B[Classify by
Annual Rupee Usage] B --> C[Class A
10 to 20 percent of items
70 to 80 percent of value] B --> D[Class B
20 to 30 percent of items
15 to 25 percent of value] B --> E[Class C
50 to 70 percent of items
5 to 10 percent of value] C --> F[Strict control
Tight EOQ
Frequent review] D --> G[Moderate control
Standard EOQ
Periodic review] E --> H[Loose control
Bulk ordering
Infrequent review] classDef aClass fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#8B0000,color:#fff classDef bClass fill:#FFD93D,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000 classDef cClass fill:#6BCB77,stroke:#006400,color:#fff classDef ctrl fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,color:#000 class C,F aClass class D,G bClass class E,H cClass class A,B ctrl ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define EOQ. State the assumptions of the classical Economic Order Quantity model.** > **Model Answer:** > **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)** is the fixed order quantity $Q^*$ that minimises the total annual inventory cost (ordering + holding) for a given constant demand. Mathematically, > $$Q^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2DS}{H}}$$ > where $D$ = annual demand, $S$ = ordering cost per order, $H$ = holding cost per unit per year. > > **Assumptions:** > 1. Demand is uniform and known. > 2. Lead time is constant. > 3. Replenishment is instantaneous. > 4. Order cost $S$ and holding cost $H$ are constant. > 5. No quantity discounts. > 6. No shortages are permitted. > 7. Infinite planning horizon. **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between EOQ and EPQ. When is the EPQ model preferred?** > **Model Answer:** > > | Feature | EOQ | EPQ | > |:-------|:----|:----| > | Replenishment | Instantaneous | Finite rate $P$ | > | Build-up phase | None | Inventory rises at rate $P - d$ | > | Formula | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ | $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/[H(1-d/P)]}$ | > | When $P \to \infty$ | Both are equivalent | > > **EPQ is preferred** when the production facility produces the item *in-house* at a finite rate (e.g., a bakery producing bread), rather than purchasing it from an outside vendor (e.g., ordering readymade bread from a wholesaler). --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** > A manufacturing company requires **6,000 units/year** of a raw material. The ordering cost is **₹200 per order** and the holding cost is **₹4 per unit per year**. > > **(a)** Determine the **Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)**, the number of orders per year, the time between consecutive orders, and the total annual cost. > > **(b)** If the supplier offers a quantity discount of **₹2 per unit** for orders of 1,000 units or more, evaluate whether the company should accept the discount. **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** Given: $D = 6{,}000$ units/yr, $S = 200$ ₹/order, $H = 4$ ₹/unit/yr. **EOQ:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2DS}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 200}{4}} = \sqrt{600{,}000} \approx 774.6 \text{ units}$$ **Number of orders per year:** $$N^* = \frac{D}{Q^*} = \frac{6{,}000}{774.6} = 7.746 \approx 8 \text{ orders/yr}$$ **Time between orders:** $$T^* = \frac{Q^*}{D} = \frac{774.6}{6{,}000} = 0.1291 \text{ yr} \approx 47.14 \text{ days}$$ **Total annual cost (ordering + holding):** $$TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH} = \sqrt{2 \times 6{,}000 \times 200 \times 4} = \sqrt{9{,}600{,}000} \approx 3{,}098.39 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** With the discount, the company must order $\geq 1{,}000$ units. The relevant unit cost reduction lowers *holding cost* (if H is proportional to price) and *purchase cost*. Assuming purchase cost $C = ₹10$/unit (not given explicitly; we treat the discount's effect on total cost only): **Total annual cost at $Q = 1{,}000$ with the new effective price (₹8/unit instead of ₹10):** $$\begin{aligned} TC_{discount} &= D \cdot C_{new} + \frac{SD}{Q} + \frac{HQ}{2} \\ &= 6{,}000(8) + \frac{200 \times 6{,}000}{1{,}000} + \frac{4 \times 1{,}000}{2} \\ &= 48{,}000 + 1{,}200 + 2{,}000 = 51{,}200 \text{ ₹/yr} \end{aligned}$$ **Without discount at EOQ:** $$TC_{no\,discount} = 6{,}000(10) + 3{,}098.39 = 63{,}098.39 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ **Savings:** $$63{,}098.39 - 51{,}200 = 11{,}898.39 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ **Decision:** The company should accept the discount and order **1,000 units per cycle** to save ₹11,898.39 per year. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students commonly forget to **re-evaluate total cost at the break-point** quantity when discounts are offered. They simply compare the EOQ with the discounted EOQ and conclude that the discount is unfavourable — losing 4–5 marks. Always compute the **actual TC at the break-point** and compare it against the **TC at the un-discounted EOQ**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] — ALTERNATIVE** > A factory produces a component at the rate of **20,000 units/year** and consumes it at the rate of **8,000 units/year**. The set-up cost per production run is **₹1,500** and the holding cost is **₹20 per unit per year**. > > **(a)** Determine the **Economic Production Quantity (EPQ)**, the maximum inventory level, and the total minimum cost. > > **(b)** If shortages are now permitted at a shortage cost of **₹30 per unit per year**, find the new optimal lot size, the maximum inventory, and the maximum back-order level. **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks:** Given: $P = 20{,}000$ u/yr, $d = 8{,}000$ u/yr, $C_o = 1{,}500$ ₹/run, $H = 20$ ₹/u/yr. **Economic Production Quantity:** $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_o D}{H \left(1 - \dfrac{d}{P}\right)}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1{,}500 \times 8{,}000}{20 \times \left(1 - \dfrac{8{,}000}{20{,}000}\right)}}$$ $$= \sqrt{\frac{24{,}000{,}000}{20 \times 0.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{24{,}000{,}000}{12}} = \sqrt{2{,}000{,}000} \approx 1{,}414.21 \text{ units}$$ **Maximum inventory:** $$I_{max} = Q^* \left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right) = 1{,}414.21 \times 0.6 = 848.53 \text{ units}$$ **Total minimum cost:** $$TC^* = \sqrt{2 C_o D H \left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right)} = \sqrt{2 \times 1{,}500 \times 8{,}000 \times 20 \times 0.6} = \sqrt{288{,}000{,}000} \approx 16{,}970.56 \text{ ₹/yr}$$ **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks:** With shortages, the new formula (using the **KTU notation** where shortage cost is also denoted $S$ for symmetry, we rename it $S_b = 30$): $$Q^* = \sqrt{\frac{2 C_o D}{H} \cdot \frac{P}{P - d} \cdot \frac{H + S_b}{S_b}}$$ $$\begin{aligned} Q^* &= \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1{,}500 \times 8{,}000}{20} \times \frac{20{,}000}{20{,}000 - 8{,}000} \times \frac{20 + 30}{30}} \\ &= \sqrt{1{,}200{,}000 \times 1.6667 \times 1.6667} \\ &= \sqrt{3{,}333{,}333} \approx 1{,}825.74 \text{ units} \end{aligned}$$ **Maximum inventory:** $$I_{max} = Q^* \left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right) \cdot \frac{H}{H + S_b} = 1{,}825.74 \times 0.6 \times \frac{20}{50} = 438.18 \text{ units}$$ **Maximum back-order level:** $$B_{max} = Q^* \left(1 - \frac{d}{P}\right) \cdot \frac{S_b}{H + S_b} = 1{,}825.74 \times 0.6 \times \frac{30}{50} = 657.27 \text{ units}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** In the EPQ-with-shortage model, students **forget the factor** $P/(P-d)$ and use the basic-EOQ-with-shortage formula, losing 4 marks. Remember: the production-rate factor multiplies the entire expression inside the square root, not just the first term. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Production Planning & Inventory Control** > > - ☐ **EOQ** (instantaneous, no shortage): $Q^* = \sqrt{2DS/H}$; $TC^* = \sqrt{2DSH}$. > - ☐ **EOQ with backorders**: $Q^* = \sqrt{(2DS/H) \cdot (H+S_b)/S_b}$; the shortage factor is **always > 1**, so $Q^*$ *increases* when shortages are allowed. > - ☐ **EPQ** (finite production, no shortage): $Q^* = \sqrt{(2DS/H) \cdot P/(P-d)}$; reduces to EOQ as $P \to \infty$. > - ☐ **EPQ with backorders**: combine both factors inside the root. > - ☐ **Holding cost** $H$ is typically $i \cdot C$ (carrying-charge × unit price). > - ☐ **Quantity discount procedure**: compute EOQ for each price level → check if it falls in its own bracket → if not, use the break-point → compare total costs (including purchase cost). > - ☐ **Re-order point**: $ROP = d \times LT + SS$. > - ☐ **Production planning hierarchy**: APP → MPS → MRP (rolling horizon, top-down). > - ☐ **Sanity check 1**: at optimum, ordering cost = holding cost. > - ☐ **Sanity check 2**: total cost curve is convex; second derivative is positive. > - ☐ **Symbol convention** in KTU papers: $S$ may denote *order cost* in the basic model but *shortage cost* in the back-order model — read the question carefully. > - ☐ **Unit consistency**: if $D$ is yearly, $T^*$ will be in years; convert to days by multiplying by 365. > - ☐ **ABC analysis** is a *classification* tool, not an optimisation tool — it tells you *where* to apply tight control, not *how much* to order. > - ☐ **Two discount types**: All-Units Discount (entire batch gets new price) vs Incremental Discount (only units beyond break-point get new price).

topics.title: Workforce Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Workforce Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Workforce Planning (Manpower Planning)** is a systematic Operations Research technique used to determine and forecast the **optimal number, composition, and skill-mix of human resources** an organization needs — both in the **present** and **future** — to meet its operational and strategic objectives. It minimizes the total cost of recruitment, training, layoff, salary, and retaining excess or deficit manpower, while satisfying demand constraints.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PEITT415 – Operations Research, Module 1), workforce planning is treated as a **stochastic forecasting and decision-making problem**, solved using one or a combination of the following OR tools:

1. **Markov Chain Analysis** – to forecast the internal flow of employees (promotion, demotion, transfer, attrition).
2. **Dynamic Programming** – to determine an optimal recruitment / layoff policy over multiple time periods (a **staircase / multi-stage decision model**).
3. **Linear Programming / Goal Programming** – to allocate manpower across departments against demand.

The two pillar sub-models the examiner expects students to master are:
- **Markov-chain-based forecasting (supply side).**
- **Dynamic-programming-based optimal recruitment (cost side).**

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — A Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bus Conductor & Driver" Analogy**
>
> Imagine a KSRTC bus depot that needs to know how many drivers it will have next year. Every year some drivers **retire (leave the system)**, some **get promoted from conductor to driver**, some **resign**, and some **are transferred out**. A fraction **stay** in the same grade.
>
> The depot manager asks: *"Given today's workforce, the rate at which drivers leave, get promoted, and stay — how many new drivers must I recruit next year to keep the fleet running without shortage or wasteful surplus?"*
>
> **Workforce planning is the mathematical translation of that question.** It replaces guesswork with:
> - **Probabilistic flows** (Markov chain → "expected number of employees moving between grades"),
> - **Cost-minimising recruitment schedules** (Dynamic Programming → "when should I hire vs. retrain, given the cost of doing so over years?").

This dual nature — **prediction (Markov)** and **optimisation (DP)** — is what makes workforce planning a flagship OR application in human-resource management, defence manpower planning, banking, and large-scale industrial staffing.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard notations used throughout KTU board answers:
>
> - **n_i(t)** = Number of employees in grade *i* at time *t* (a **state vector element**).
> - **P = [p_ij]** = **Transition Probability Matrix** — probability that an employee in grade *i* at time *t* moves to grade *j* at time *t+1*.
> - **R(t)** = **Recruitment vector** at time *t* (number of new employees added to each grade).
> - **π_j** = **Steady-state (equilibrium) proportion** of employees in grade *j* in the long run.
> - **g_k(t)** = Demand (required number) of grade *k* employees at time *t*.
> - **C_R, C_L, C_T, C_S** = Costs of **Recruitment, Layoff, Training, Salary** respectively.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Pillars of Workforce Planning

### Pillar 1 — Markov Chain Forecasting (Supply Side)

The organisation is modelled as a **finite, discrete-time Markov chain** where each **grade (or job category)** is a **state**. An employee currently in grade *i* has a fixed probability *p_ij* of being in grade *j* next period.

**Assumptions made in the KTU model:**
1. Transition probabilities are **stationary** (do not change with time *t*).
2. The number of employees is large enough that **expected-value analysis** is acceptable.
3. New recruits enter at the **lowest grade** (or the grade for which recruitment vector is defined).
4. The organisation is a **closed system** apart from controlled recruitment/layoff.

#### 2.1.1 The Forecast Equation (Fundamental Identity)

The expected workforce vector at time *t+1* is:

$$\mathbf{n}(t+1) \;=\; \mathbf{n}(t)\,\mathbf{P} \;+\; \mathbf{R}(t)$$

- **First term** $\mathbf{n}(t)\mathbf{P}$ = expected number of employees moving internally (transitions).
- **Second term** $\mathbf{R}(t)$ = new recruits added to each grade.

> [!NOTE]
> If the organisation also has **voluntary wastage** (resignations) and **retirements**, the equation is augmented with a **wasage vector W(t)** subtracted from supply.

#### 2.1.2 Steady-State (Long-Run) Workforce

If $\mathbf{R}(t) = \mathbf{0}$ (no recruitment) and $\mathbf{P}$ is regular, the chain reaches equilibrium:

$$\pi_j \;=\; \lim_{t \to \infty} \Pr\{\text{employee is in grade } j\}$$

$$\boldsymbol{\pi} \mathbf{P} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\pi}, \qquad \sum_j \pi_j \;=\; 1$$

The **expected long-run grade-size** for a starting strength of $N$ employees is:

$$n_j(\infty) \;=\; N \cdot \pi_j$$

#### 2.1.3 Promotions, Transfers, and Wastage — Direct Read-Off

The expected number of **promotions from grade *i* to grade *j*** in period *t* is:

$$E[\text{promotions } i \to j] \;=\; n_i(t) \cdot p_{ij}$$

Similarly, **wastage in grade *i*** is the probability of leaving the system from grade *i*:

$$p_{i,\text{out}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \sum_{j} p_{ij}$$

---

### Pillar 2 — Dynamic Programming for Recruitment (Cost Side)

This is the **staircase (or multi-stage) recruitment model**. The decision-maker must decide, in each of *N* time periods, how many employees to **recruit** or **lay off**, balancing:

- **Recruitment cost** $C_R$ per new employee,
- **Layoff cost** $C_L$ per separated employee,
- **Salary cost** $C_S$ per retained employee,
- **Penalty / shortage cost** $C_P$ if workforce falls below demand.

#### 2.1.4 Bellman's Recursive Equation

$$f_n(s) \;=\; \min_{0 \le x_n \le s} \Big[\, C_R \cdot \max(0, x_n - s) \;+\; C_L \cdot \max(0, s - x_n) \;+\; C_S \cdot x_n \;+\; f_{n-1}(x_n) \,\Big]$$

- $f_n(s)$ = minimum cost from period *n* back to period *1* when the current workforce is *s*.
- $x_n$ = workforce size chosen for the next period.
- $f_0(x_1)$ = terminal cost (often 0 if the organisation closes, or a final salvage value).

The optimal policy is obtained by **backward recursion**.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **every formula KTU examiners expect** for the workforce planning sub-topic. Memorise the *Forecast Equation*, *Steady-state equation*, and *Bellman recursion* — these three alone carry ~10 of the 14 marks in a typical Module-1 Part-B question.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Forecast Equation (vector form) | $\mathbf{n}(t+1) = \mathbf{n}(t)\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{R}(t)$ | n = grade-size vector, P = transition matrix, R = recruitment vector |
| 2 | Scalar forecast (grade *k*) | $n_k(t+1) = \sum_{i} n_i(t) p_{ik} + r_k(t)$ | sum over all feeding grades |
| 3 | Steady-state equation | $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ | $\pi$ = row vector of long-run proportions |
| 4 | Long-run grade size | $n_j(\infty) = N \cdot \pi_j$ | $N$ = total long-run workforce |
| 5 | Expected promotions $i \to j$ | $E_{ij}(t) = n_i(t) \cdot p_{ij}$ | direct multiplication |
| 6 | Wastage probability of grade *i* | $p_{i,\text{out}} = 1 - \sum_{j} p_{ij}$ | complement of row sum |
| 7 | Expected wastage in grade *i* | $E[W_i(t)] = n_i(t) \cdot p_{i,\text{out}}$ | |
| 8 | Bellman Recursion (DP) | $f_n(s) = \min_{x_n} [C_R(x_n-s)^+ + C_L(s-x_n)^+ + C_S x_n + f_{n-1}(x_n)]$ | $f_n$ = min cost from period *n* |
| 9 | Steady-state solution | $\pi_j = \dfrac{\sum_i n_i(0) p_{ij}^*}{\sum_j \sum_i n_i(0) p_{ij}^*}$ | where $p_{ij}^*$ is limiting prob. |
| 10 | Expected value of wastage over T periods | $E[W_{\text{total}}] = \sum_{t=1}^{T} \mathbf{n}(t) \cdot \mathbf{p}_{\text{out}}$ | row-vector dot product |

> **Units convention:** *n_i(t)* is dimensionless (count of persons); *p_ij* is dimensionless (probability); *C_R, C_L, C_S* are in monetary units (₹, $, etc.) **per person per period**.

---

## 2.3 Why Workforce Planning Matters in Industry

- **Defence / Military:** Officer-grade forecasting (cadet → captain → major …).
- **Banking:** Probationary officer → branch manager grade transitions.
- **IT Services:** Junior developer → senior developer → architect promotion flows.
- **Public sector (KTU placement relevance):** Engineering college staff planning, faculty promotions under AICTE/UGC norms.
- **Healthcare:** Resident doctor → specialist → consultant tracking.

The common thread is that **people flow between grades stochastically**, and **OR gives the only rigorous, defendable way to forecast the requirement** and **minimise the cost of getting it wrong** (over-staffing drains salary budget; under-staffing drains productivity).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Forecast Equation

**Starting principle:** An employee in grade *i* at time *t* moves to grade *j* at time *t+1* with probability *p_ij* (Markov property).

**Step 1 — Expected count in grade *j* from internal movement alone:**

For a single employee in grade *i*, the probability of being in grade *j* next period is $p_{ij}$. For $n_i(t)$ independent employees in grade *i*:

$$\text{Expected arrivals to } j \text{ from } i \;=\; n_i(t) \cdot p_{ij}$$

**Step 2 — Sum over all feeding grades *i*:**

$$E[\text{arrivals to } j \text{ (no recruitment)}] \;=\; \sum_{i} n_i(t) \cdot p_{ij}$$

**Step 3 — Add new recruits directly placed in grade *j*:**

$$n_j(t+1) \;=\; \sum_{i} n_i(t)\,p_{ij} \;+\; r_j(t)$$

**Step 4 — Write in matrix form by collecting over all grades:**

$$\mathbf{n}(t+1) \;=\; \mathbf{n}(t)\,\mathbf{P} \;+\; \mathbf{R}(t)$$

**Step 5 — Justification of linearity:** Because expectation is linear, the matrix form is a strict, exact identity on expected values (not a heuristic).

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Steady-State Equation

**Step 1 — As $t \to \infty$** the system (without recruitment) reaches an equilibrium distribution $\pi_j$.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium condition:** the proportions do not change after one more transition.

$$\pi_j \;=\; \sum_{i} \pi_i \, p_{ij}, \qquad \forall j$$

**Step 3 — In matrix form:**

$$\boldsymbol{\pi} \mathbf{P} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\pi}, \qquad \sum_j \pi_j \;=\; 1$$

**Step 4 — Solving:** This is a homogeneous linear system augmented with the normalisation condition. Solve by Gaussian elimination on $(\mathbf{P}^T - \mathbf{I})\boldsymbol{\pi}^T = 0$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 1 — Markov Forecasting (KTU-Style, 14-Mark Format)

> **Problem.** A university has a faculty in three grades: **Lecturer (L)**, **Assistant Professor (AP)**, **Professor (P)**. The transition probability matrix (per year) and current strengths are:

$$\mathbf{P} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & 0.3 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.7 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.0 & 0.9 \end{bmatrix} \quad \text{(rows = L, AP, P; columns = L, AP, P)}$$

**Current strengths:** $\mathbf{n}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 100 & 80 & 50 \end{bmatrix}$ (L, AP, P).
**No recruitment is allowed** (R(t) = 0).

**Required:** (a) Forecast the strength in each grade after 1, 2, 3 years. (b) Find the steady-state distribution. (c) Compute the expected number of promotions AP→P in year 1 and L→AP in year 2.

### Solution — Part (a): One-Year Forecast

$$\mathbf{n}(1) \;=\; \mathbf{n}(0)\,\mathbf{P}$$

Step-by-step scalar computation:

$$n_L(1) \;=\; 100(0.6) + 80(0) + 50(0) \;=\; 60$$

$$n_{AP}(1) \;=\; 100(0.3) + 80(0.7) + 50(0) \;=\; 30 + 56 + 0 \;=\; 86$$

$$n_P(1) \;=\; 100(0.0) + 80(0.2) + 50(0.9) \;=\; 0 + 16 + 45 \;=\; 61$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n}(1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 60 & 86 & 61 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Total check:** $60 + 86 + 61 = 207$ (matches $100+80+50=230$ minus expected wastage $23$ in year 1). ✓

### Solution — Part (a continued): Two-Year Forecast

$$\mathbf{n}(2) \;=\; \mathbf{n}(1)\,\mathbf{P}$$

$$n_L(2) \;=\; 60(0.6) + 86(0) + 61(0) \;=\; 36$$

$$n_{AP}(2) \;=\; 60(0.3) + 86(0.7) + 61(0) \;=\; 18 + 60.2 + 0 \;=\; 78.2$$

$$n_P(2) \;=\; 60(0.0) + 86(0.2) + 61(0.9) \;=\; 0 + 17.2 + 54.9 \;=\; 72.1$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n}(2) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 36.0 & 78.2 & 72.1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

### Solution — Part (a final): Three-Year Forecast

$$\mathbf{n}(3) \;=\; \mathbf{n}(2)\,\mathbf{P}$$

$$n_L(3) \;=\; 36(0.6) + 78.2(0) + 72.1(0) \;=\; 21.6$$

$$n_{AP}(3) \;=\; 36(0.3) + 78.2(0.7) + 72.1(0) \;=\; 10.8 + 54.74 + 0 \;=\; 65.54$$

$$n_P(3) \;=\; 36(0.0) + 78.2(0.2) + 72.1(0.9) \;=\; 0 + 15.64 + 64.89 \;=\; 80.53$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n}(3) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 21.60 & 65.54 & 80.53 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Observation:** Lecturer grade is **decaying**, Assistant Professor is **falling after a peak**, and Professor grade is **monotonically growing** — the classic "pyramid inversion" symptom of an ageing faculty with no recruitment.

### Solution — Part (b): Steady-State Distribution

We need $\pi$ such that $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$ and $\pi_L + \pi_{AP} + \pi_P = 1$.

**Equations from $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$:**

$$\pi_L \;=\; 0.6\,\pi_L \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.4\,\pi_L \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \pi_L \;=\; 0$$

$$\pi_{AP} \;=\; 0.3\,\pi_L + 0.7\,\pi_{AP} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.3\,\pi_{AP} \;=\; 0.3\,\pi_L \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \pi_{AP} \;=\; 0$$

$$\pi_P \;=\; 0.2\,\pi_{AP} + 0.9\,\pi_P \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.1\,\pi_P \;=\; 0.2\,\pi_{AP} \;=\; 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \pi_P \;=\; 0$$

Using the normalisation $\pi_L + \pi_{AP} + \pi_P = 1$:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\pi} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Interpretation:** In the long run, **100% of the faculty will be Professors** because there is no recruitment, and the chain is **absorbing** at the top grade. This is exactly the trend visible in Part (a) forecasts.

### Solution — Part (c): Expected Promotions

**AP→P promotions in year 1:**

$$E[\text{AP} \to \text{P in year 1}] \;=\; n_{AP}(0) \cdot p_{AP \to P} \;=\; 80 \times 0.2 \;=\; \mathbf{16 \text{ promotions}}$$

**L→AP promotions in year 2:**

The number of Lecturers in year 1 is $n_L(1) = 60$. Each has probability 0.3 of being promoted to AP during year 2.

$$E[\text{L} \to \text{AP in year 2}] \;=\; n_L(1) \cdot p_{L \to AP} \;=\; 60 \times 0.3 \;=\; \mathbf{18 \text{ promotions}}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 2 — Dynamic Programming for Recruitment

> **Problem.** A company forecasts its **required workforce** for the next 4 years: $g_1 = 50, g_2 = 70, g_3 = 80, g_4 = 60$. Current workforce $s = 60$. Costs: $C_R = 200$ (recruit), $C_L = 250$ (lay off), $C_S = 100$ (salary/period). No shortage penalty is given.

> **Required:** Find the minimum-cost recruitment/layoff schedule using dynamic programming.

### Solution Setup

**Stage variable** $n$ = year (4, 3, 2, 1, going backwards).
**State variable** $s_n$ = workforce at the *start* of year *n*.
**Decision variable** $x_n$ = workforce at the *end* of year *n* (next year's start).

**Period cost:**

$$c_n(s_n, x_n) \;=\; 200\,(x_n - s_n)^+ \;+\; 250\,(s_n - x_n)^+ \;+\; 100\,x_n$$

(We assume salary is paid on the **end-of-year** workforce.)

### Recursion (Backward DP)

We work **from year 1 forward**, computing minimum cost from start year to year $k$, given current strength $s$.

**Period 1 (initial state $s = 60$, choose $x_1$):**

$$f_1(s = 60) \;=\; \min_{x_1} \Big[\,200(x_1-60)^+ + 250(60-x_1)^+ + 100\,x_1\,\Big]$$

For $x_1 \ge 60$ (recruit): cost $= 200(x_1-60) + 100 x_1 = 300 x_1 - 12000$, increasing in $x_1$. Optimal at $x_1 = 60$ (no recruit). Cost = **6000**.
For $x_1 < 60$ (lay off): cost $= 250(60-x_1) + 100 x_1 = 15000 - 150 x_1$, decreasing in $x_1$. Optimal at $x_1 = 0$ → cost = **15000**.

$$\boxed{f_1(60) \;=\; 6000 \quad \text{at } x_1^* = 60}$$

**Period 2 (assume $x_1 = 60$ enters period 2):**

$$f_2(60) \;=\; 6000 \;+\; \min_{x_2} \Big[\,200(x_2-60)^+ + 250(60-x_2)^+ + 100 x_2\,\Big] \;=\; 6000 + 6000 \;=\; \mathbf{12000 \text{ at } x_2^* = 60}$$

**Period 3 (need to ramp up to demand 80):**

If we entered with $x_2 = 60$, choosing $x_3 = 80$:

- Recruit cost: $200 \times 20 = 4000$
- Salary: $100 \times 80 = 8000$
- **Total this period: 12000**
- **Cumulative: 6000 + 6000 + 12000 = 24000**

$$\boxed{f_3(60) \;=\; 24000 \text{ at } x_3^* = 80}$$

(Choosing $x_3 < 60$ would save salary but incur heavy layoff — verify: $x_3 = 50$ → $250 \times 10 + 100 \times 50 = 7500$, cumulative 6000+6000+7500 = 19500. **This is cheaper!**) Let us re-evaluate properly.

**Re-evaluation Period 3:**

- $x_3 = 50$ → cost = $250(60-50) + 100(50) = 2500 + 5000 = 7500$. Cum = $6000+6000+7500 = \mathbf{19500}$.
- $x_3 = 60$ → cost = $0 + 100(60) = 6000$. Cum = $6000+6000+6000 = \mathbf{18000}$.
- $x_3 = 80$ → cost = $200(20) + 100(80) = 4000+8000 = 12000$. Cum = $6000+6000+12000 = \mathbf{24000}$.

**Minimum: 18000 at $x_3 = 60$** (hold workforce, ignore demand spike to save on heavy recruitment).

$$\boxed{f_3(60) \;=\; 18000 \text{ at } x_3^* = 60}$$

**Period 4 (demand 60):**

If we entered with $x_3 = 60$, choosing $x_4 = 60$:

- Cost this period: $0 + 100(60) = 6000$
- **Cumulative: 18000 + 6000 = 24000**

$$\boxed{f_4(60) \;=\; 24000 \text{ at } x_4^* = 60}$$

### Summary Table

| Year | State $s_n$ | Decision $x_n^*$ | Period Cost | Cumulative Cost |
|------|------------|-----------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 60 | 60 | 6000 | 6000 |
| 2 | 60 | 60 | 6000 | 12000 |
| 3 | 60 | 60 | 6000 | 18000 |
| 4 | 60 | 60 | 6000 | **24000** |

> [!NOTE]
> The optimal policy is **"hold the workforce constant at 60 across all four years"**, accepting that demand of 70 and 80 will not be fully met. This is the **mathematically optimal** solution under the given cost structure — a great example of how OR reveals counterintuitive results.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Markov vs. Dynamic Programming

| Aspect | Markov Forecasting | Dynamic Programming |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **What it answers** | "What will the workforce look like?" | "What is the cheapest recruitment plan?" |
| **Type of model** | Probabilistic (stochastic) | Deterministic sequential decision |
| **Core variable** | Transition matrix P | Cost function $C_R, C_L, C_S$ |
| **Time structure** | Steady-state OR finite horizon | Multi-stage horizon |
| **Output** | Forecast of $n_i(t)$ | Optimal $x_t$ (workforce to maintain) |
| **Limitation** | Assumes constant P | Assumes accurate demand forecast $g_t$ |
| **Combined use** | Use Markov to *predict* future need; use DP to *plan* the recruitment that realises it. |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Markov Workforce Flow Diagram (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **functional flow of information** in a Markov-based workforce planning model. It avoids non-physical drawings and instead shows how data moves through the model components.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Current Workforce n of 0] --> B[Apply Transition Matrix P]
    B --> C[Internal Flow n of 0 times P]
    C --> D{Add External Input}
    D -->|Recruitment R of t| E[Combined Forecast n of t+1]
    D -->|Wastage W of t| F[Adjusted Forecast n of t+1 minus W]
    E --> G[Grade wise Forecast Output]
    F --> G
    G --> H{Time Horizon Check}
    H -->|Finite t equals 1 to T| I[Report n of t for t equals 1 to T]
    H -->|Infinite t tends to infinity| J[Solve pi times P equals pi]
    J --> K[Steady State Distribution pi]
    I --> L[Compare with Demand g of t]
    L --> M[Identify Surplus or Deficit]
    M --> N[Feeds into Dynamic Programming Optimiser]
    N --> O[Optimal Recruitment Schedule x of t]
    O --> P[Final Workforce Plan]
```

**How to read this diagram (KTU exam tip):**
- Box **A** is the **input state vector**.
- Box **B–C** is the **Markov transition step** — generates expected internal movement.
- Box **D** is the **decision point** for adding recruitment or subtracting wastage.
- Box **H** branches the workflow: finite-horizon forecast (left) vs. infinite-horizon steady-state analysis (right).
- Box **N → O** is the **handshake** between the two pillars: Markov *forecasts* the supply, DP *optimises* the corrective recruitment.

## 4.2 Three-Grade Markov State Diagram

The following diagram shows the **absorbing state behaviour** of the lecturer→AP→professor example, identifying which grade is the **absorbing state**.

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Lecturer
    Lecturer --> Lecturer: p LL equals 0 point 6 stay
    Lecturer --> AssistantProf: p LAP equals 0 point 3 promote
    Lecturer --> [*]: p Lout equals 0 point 1 retire or resign
    AssistantProf --> AssistantProf: p APAP equals 0 point 7 stay
    AssistantProf --> Professor: p APP equals 0 point 2 promote
    AssistantProf --> [*]: p APout equals 0 point 1 leave
    Professor --> Professor: p PP equals 0 point 9 stay
    Professor --> [*]: p Pout equals 0 point 1 retire
    note right of Professor: Absorbing like behaviour in long run
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Professor grade is not strictly absorbing** (it has 0.1 wastage), but its self-loop probability (0.9) is so high that the **system tends to accumulate there** — hence the all-mass-in-Professor steady state derived in Section 3.3.

## 4.3 Dynamic Programming Staircase Diagram

The classical **staircase / multi-stage** representation used in KTU board answers for DP-based recruitment.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage n equals 4 State s4] --> S2[Stage n equals 3 State s3]
    S2 --> S3[Stage n equals 2 State s2]
    S3 --> S4[Stage n equals 1 State s1]
    S4 --> S5[Terminal f0]
    S1 -.Decision x4.-> S2
    S2 -.Decision x3.-> S3
    S3 -.Decision x2.-> S4
    S4 -.Decision x1.-> S5
```

**Reading guide:** The flowchart proceeds **backward** (right-to-left) when solving by Bellman's recursion. Each horizontal arrow represents a one-stage transition; each diagonal annotation is the **decision variable** $x_n$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define workforce planning. State any two OR techniques used to solve it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Workforce planning is a systematic OR technique to forecast and determine the **optimal number and grade-mix of human resources** an organisation requires over a planning horizon, minimising total cost of recruitment, layoffs, salary, and shortage.  
Two OR techniques commonly used are:
1. **Markov Chain Analysis** — to forecast internal flows of employees between grades using a transition probability matrix.
2. **Dynamic Programming** — to determine the minimum-cost recruitment/layoff schedule over multiple time periods using Bellman's principle of optimality.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Write the steady-state equation for a Markov-chain workforce model. What does it signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The steady-state (equilibrium) equation is:

$$\boldsymbol{\pi} \mathbf{P} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\pi}, \qquad \sum_{j} \pi_j \;=\; 1$$

It signifies that in the **long run** the proportion of employees in each grade remains unchanged from one period to the next. The vector $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ represents the **equilibrium grade-distribution** of the workforce, useful for long-term capacity and budget planning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 1, Q2]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

A small IT firm has employees in three grades — **Trainee (T)**, **Developer (D)**, **Project Lead (L)**. The annual transition matrix is:

$$\mathbf{P} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.3 & 0.0 \\ 0.0 & 0.6 & 0.2 \\ 0.0 & 0.0 & 0.8 \end{bmatrix}$$

Current workforce: $\mathbf{n}(0) = \begin{bmatrix} 40 & 30 & 20 \end{bmatrix}$.

**(a)** Compute the expected workforce distribution in years 1 and 2. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Determine the steady-state distribution. Identify which grade is the "absorbing-like" state and comment on the HR implication. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Forecasts

**Year 1:**

$$n_T(1) \;=\; 40(0.5) + 30(0) + 20(0) \;=\; 20$$

$$n_D(1) \;=\; 40(0.3) + 30(0.6) + 20(0) \;=\; 12 + 18 + 0 \;=\; 30$$

$$n_L(1) \;=\; 40(0) + 30(0.2) + 20(0.8) \;=\; 0 + 6 + 16 \;=\; 22$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n}(1) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 20 & 30 & 22 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Year 2:**

$$n_T(2) \;=\; 20(0.5) + 30(0) + 22(0) \;=\; 10$$

$$n_D(2) \;=\; 20(0.3) + 30(0.6) + 22(0) \;=\; 6 + 18 + 0 \;=\; 24$$

$$n_L(2) \;=\; 20(0) + 30(0.2) + 22(0.8) \;=\; 0 + 6 + 17.6 \;=\; 23.6$$

$$\boxed{\mathbf{n}(2) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 10.0 & 24.0 & 23.6 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Total check Year 1:** $20+30+22 = 72$ (down from 90, wastage = 18). ✓
**Total check Year 2:** $10+24+23.6 = 57.6$ (further wastage = 14.4). ✓

#### Part (b) — Steady State

From $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$:

- $\pi_T = 0.5\pi_T \Rightarrow 0.5\pi_T = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_T = 0$
- $\pi_D = 0.3\pi_T + 0.6\pi_D \Rightarrow 0.4\pi_D = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_D = 0$
- $\pi_L = 0.2\pi_D + 0.8\pi_L \Rightarrow 0.2\pi_L = 0.2\pi_D = 0 \Rightarrow \pi_L = 0$

But $\pi_T + \pi_D + \pi_L = 1$, so we resolve by checking chain structure.

Because $0.5 + 0.3 = 0.8 < 1$ (Trainees leak 20% to wastage), and $0.6 + 0.2 = 0.8 < 1$ (Developers leak 20% to wastage), all mass is eventually **absorbed** by the Project Lead grade (or leaves via wastage from the chain). With no recruitment and the chain being **transient** at T and D, the only persistent mass in the long run is in the L grade. Hence:

$$\boxed{\boldsymbol{\pi} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}}$$

**HR Implication:** In the long run, **all surviving employees end up as Project Leads**, and the firm will eventually have **no Trainees or Developers** unless recruitment is initiated. The HR manager must **recruit continuously** into the Trainee grade to sustain the talent pipeline.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU Valuation):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Students forget to **subtract the wastage probability** ($1 - 0.5 - 0.3 = 0.2$ for T; $1 - 0.6 - 0.2 = 0.2$ for D; $1 - 0.8 = 0.2$ for L). The transition matrix rows must each sum to ≤ 1, not to 1. If they sum to 1 there is no wastage. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> - **Common mistake 2:** Forgetting to verify the **normalisation** $\sum \pi_j = 1$ before declaring the steady-state answer.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Treating the system as ergodic when it is actually **absorbing-like** — this leads to a wrong "uniform" or "cyclic" answer.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2 alternative]**

A manufacturing company's **demand forecast** for the next 4 years is $g_1 = 100, g_2 = 120, g_3 = 90, g_4 = 110$ employees. Current workforce is **100**. Recruitment cost is ₹300 per person, layoff cost is ₹400 per person, and salary is ₹200 per person per year.

**(a)** Formulate the workforce planning problem as a dynamic programming model. Clearly state the stage, state, decision, and cost functions. **(7 marks)**
**(b)** Solve the DP by backward recursion and determine the optimal recruitment / layoff plan and the minimum total cost. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — DP Formulation

- **Stage $n$** = year (numbered $n = 1, 2, 3, 4$).
- **State $s_n$** = workforce at the **start of year $n$** (given: $s_1 = 100$).
- **Decision $x_n$** = workforce at the **end of year $n$** (= workforce at start of year $n+1$).
- **Recruitment** = $(x_n - s_n)^+$; **Layoff** = $(s_n - x_n)^+$.
- **Cost function for year $n$:**

$$c_n(s_n, x_n) \;=\; 300\,(x_n - s_n)^+ \;+\; 400\,(s_n - x_n)^+ \;+\; 200\,x_n$$

- **Recursive equation (Bellman):**

$$f_n(s_n) \;=\; \min_{x_n \ge 0} \Big[\,c_n(s_n, x_n) \;+\; f_{n+1}(x_n)\,\Big], \qquad f_5(\cdot) \;=\; 0$$

#### Part (b) — Solving by Backward Recursion

We minimise cost while satisfying the constraint that $x_n \ge 0$ (workforce cannot be negative). For simplicity, assume $x_n$ is treated as a continuous variable (discrete version in real practice).

**Year 4 (final period):**

The terminal cost $f_5(x_4) = 0$. So we minimise the period-4 cost alone:

$$f_4(s_4) \;=\; \min_{x_4 \ge 0} \Big[\,300(x_4 - s_4)^+ + 400(s_4 - x_4)^+ + 200 x_4\,\Big]$$

**Trade-off analysis:**

- **Recruit** ($x_4 \ge s_4$): $c_4 = 300(x_4 - s_4) + 200 x_4 = 500 x_4 - 300 s_4$. Increasing in $x_4$. Optimal at boundary $x_4 = s_4$.
- **Lay off** ($x_4 < s_4$): $c_4 = 400(s_4 - x_4) + 200 x_4 = 400 s_4 - 200 x_4$. Decreasing in $x_4$. Optimal at boundary $x_4 = 0$ → cost = $400 s_4$.
- **At $x_4 = s_4$:** cost = $200 s_4$.

Choose the smaller of $\{200 s_4,\; 400 s_4\}$ → **$x_4^* = s_4$** (no change), cost = $200 s_4$.

$$\boxed{f_4(s_4) \;=\; 200 s_4, \qquad x_4^* \;=\; s_4}$$

**Year 3:**

$$f_3(s_3) \;=\; \min_{x_3 \ge 0}\Big[\,c_3(s_3, x_3) + 200 x_3\,\Big]$$

Period-3 cost is $300(x_3 - s_3)^+ + 400(s_3 - x_3)^+ + 200 x_3$. Adding $f_4 = 200 x_3$:

- **If $x_3 \ge s_3$:** total = $300(x_3 - s_3) + 200 x_3 + 200 x_3 = 500 x_3 - 300 s_3 + 200 x_3 = 700 x_3 - 300 s_3$ — wait, re-evaluate.

Period-3 cost only (without $f_4$): $300(x_3-s_3)^+ + 400(s_3-x_3)^+ + 200 x_3$.
With $f_4 = 200 x_3$: total = $300(x_3-s_3)^+ + 400(s_3-x_3)^+ + 400 x_3$.

- **Recruit** ($x_3 \ge s_3$): $300(x_3-s_3) + 400 x_3 = 700 x_3 - 300 s_3$, increasing. Optimal $x_3 = s_3$, cost = $400 s_3$.
- **Lay off** ($x_3 < s_3$): $400(s_3-x_3) + 400 x_3 = 400 s_3$, constant! Any $0 \le x_3 < s_3$ gives cost $400 s_3$.

So $f_3(s_3) = 400 s_3$, and any $x_3 \in [0, s_3]$ is optimal. Choose **$x_3^* = s_3$** (no change) for simplicity.

$$\boxed{f_3(s_3) \;=\; 400 s_3}$$

**Year 2:**

Same structure, $f_3 = 400 x_2$:

Total = $300(x_2-s_2)^+ + 400(s_2-x_2)^+ + 200 x_2 + 400 x_2 = 300(x_2-s_2)^+ + 400(s_2-x_2)^+ + 600 x_2$.

- **Recruit** ($x_2 \ge s_2$): $300(x_2-s_2) + 600 x_2 = 900 x_2 - 300 s_2$, increasing. Optimal $x_2 = s_2$, cost = $600 s_2$.
- **Lay off** ($x_2 < s_2$): $400(s_2-x_2) + 600 x_2 = 400 s_2 + 200 x_2$, decreasing. Optimal $x_2 = 0$, cost = $400 s_2$.

Choose **$\min(600 s_2, 400 s_2) = 400 s_2$** → **$x_2^* = 0$** (lay off everything).

Wait — that would zero out the workforce, which is unrealistic. KTU examiners expect the candidate to **recognise the trade-off and the demand constraint**. In practice, $x_n$ should be constrained to meet minimum demand $g_n$. Let us apply that:

**With demand constraint** $x_n \ge g_n$ for all $n$:

- Year 2: $g_2 = 120$. Need $x_2 \ge 120$. With $s_2 = s_1$ (year 1 didn't change) = 100. So must recruit from 100 to at least 120. Optimal: $x_2 = 120$ (no more). Period-2 cost = $300(20) + 200(120) = 6000 + 24000 = 30000$. $f_2(100) = 30000 + 400(120) = 30000 + 48000 = 78000$.
- Year 3: $g_3 = 90$. Enter with $s_3 = 120$. To meet $x_3 \ge 90$, simply choose $x_3 = 90$ (lay off 30). Period-3 cost = $400(30) + 200(90) = 12000 + 18000 = 30000$. $f_3(120) = 30000 + 200(90) = 30000 + 18000 = 48000$.
- Year 4: $g_4 = 110$. Enter with $s_4 = 90$. Recruit to 110. Period-4 cost = $300(20) + 200(110) = 6000 + 22000 = 28000$. $f_4(90) = 28000$.

**Year 1:** No recruitment, no layoff. $x_1 = 100$. Cost = $200 \times 100 = 20000$.

#### Final Summary Table

| Year | Demand $g_n$ | State $s_n$ | Decision $x_n^*$ | Recruitment | Layoff | Period Cost | Cumulative |
|------|-------------|-------------|------------------|-------------|--------|-------------|------------|
| 1 | 100 | 100 | 100 | 0 | 0 | 20000 | 20000 |
| 2 | 120 | 100 | 120 | 20 | 0 | 30000 | 50000 |
| 3 | 90 | 120 | 90 | 0 | 30 | 30000 | 80000 |
| 4 | 110 | 90 | 110 | 20 | 0 | 28000 | **108000** |

$$\boxed{\text{Minimum Total Cost} \;=\; ₹\,\mathbf{1{,}08{,}000}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (KTU Valuation):**
> - **Common mistake 1:** Forgetting the **demand constraint** $x_n \ge g_n$ — without it the DP collapses to "lay off everything" which is mathematically optimal but practically absurd. KTU expects you to mention and apply the demand constraint. (Loses 2–3 marks.)
> - **Common mistake 2:** Mis-labelling the **period cost components** — students often include salary in BOTH the period cost and the future-state value. Salary is paid **once**, on the end-of-period workforce.
> - **Common mistake 3:** Confusing the direction of recursion (forward vs. backward). The standard form is **backward** (from $n=N$ down to 1).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist — read this 5 minutes before the exam.**

**Core Definitions**
- Workforce planning = **forecast + optimise** the manpower an organisation needs.
- Two OR tools: **Markov Chain** (forecast) + **Dynamic Programming** (optimise).

**Markov Chain Essentials**
- **Forecast equation:** $\mathbf{n}(t+1) = \mathbf{n}(t)\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{R}(t)$.
- **Scalar form:** $n_k(t+1) = \sum_i n_i(t)\,p_{ik} + r_k(t)$.
- **Steady state:** $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$, with $\sum \pi_j = 1$.
- **Row sums of P** can be **< 1** (wastage probability is the complement).
- **Expected promotion $i \to j$** in period $t$ = $n_i(t) \cdot p_{ij}$.

**Dynamic Programming Essentials**
- **Stage** = time period; **State** = current workforce; **Decision** = next-period workforce.
- **Bellman recursion:** $f_n(s) = \min_x [c_n(s,x) + f_{n-1}(x)]$.
- **Three cost components** — recruitment $C_R$, layoff $C_L$, salary $C_S$.
- **Always include the demand constraint** $x_n \ge g_n$ to avoid trivial "lay-off-everything" solutions.
- Solve by **backward recursion** (start from terminal stage $f_0 = 0$).

**Exam-Day Heuristics**
1. If asked to **forecast only** → use Markov, compute $\mathbf{n}(1), \mathbf{n}(2), \dots$ and stop.
2. If asked to **find the long-run workforce** → solve $\boldsymbol{\pi}\mathbf{P} = \boldsymbol{\pi}$.
3. If asked to **find optimal recruitment** → use DP with the standard recursive cost equation.
4. If asked to **compute expected promotions/transfers** → multiply state size by transition probability.
5. Always **state assumptions** (stationary P, large workforce for expectation to hold).
6. Always **normalise** your steady-state vector — partial marks otherwise.
7. Always **show every matrix multiplication step** — examiners reward transparency.

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid**
- ❌ Forgetting wastage (row sums < 1).
- ❌ Forgetting to add the recruitment vector $\mathbf{R}(t)$.
- ❌ Confusing row and column conventions for the transition matrix.
- ❌ Using forward recursion in DP instead of backward.
- ❌ Omitting the demand constraint in DP.
- ❌ Mixing up $\pi_j$ (proportion) with $n_j(\infty)$ (count).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Urban Development Planning: The Simplex Method, LP Model in Equation Form
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Urban Development Planning: The Simplex Method, LP Model in Equation Form # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Simplex Method: A Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Simplex Method (KTU Definition):** > The **Simplex Method** is an iterative, algebraic procedure developed by **George B. Dantzig (1947)** for solving **Linear Programming (LP)** problems. It moves from one **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** of the system to an adjacent BFS along the **edges of the feasible region (convex polytope)** in a systematic manner, terminating at an optimal vertex (corner point). The method operates on a Linear Programming model that has already been **converted into Equation Form (Standard Form)**, which is a strict prerequisite for the algorithmic mechanics (pivoting, basis identification, optimality tests) of the Simplex Method. ## 1.2 The Urban Development Planning Context The **Urban Development Planning (UDP) problem** is a classical, real-world pedagogical LP model used in Taha's *Operations Research: An Introduction* (and adopted in KTU 2024 scheme study materials) to introduce the Simplex Method. It captures the essence of **resource-constrained land allocation** — a city planner must decide how much of a finite tract of land to dedicate to **residential, commercial, industrial, and recreational** zones, subject to zoning regulations and economic constraints. ## 1.3 Intuitive Overview — Plain English Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Real-Estate Treasure Map:** > Imagine you own a **640-acre plot** in a new city. You must divide this land into 4 zones to earn the **highest possible total revenue** while obeying government rules: > - At least **25%** of the *developed* land must be residential (for housing). > - At most **30%** of the *developed* land can be commercial (for malls/offices). > - Recreation (parks) must occupy **at least 80 acres** of the **total** 640. > - At most **70%** of the total land can be developed. > - Commercial area = Industrial area (zoning balance rule). > > The LP is the **"puzzle"**: you have one objective (max revenue), four unknowns (acres for each zone), and five rule-book constraints. The **Simplex Method** is the *step-by-step trial-and-error engine* that tests the corner-points (vertices) of the feasible region, **climbing toward the best solution**. ## 1.4 What is "LP Model in Equation Form"? > [!IMPORTANT] > **LP Model in Equation Form (Standard Form) — KTU Definition:** > A Linear Programming model is said to be in **equation form** when: > 1. All functional constraints are written as **equalities** (using slack / surplus / artificial variables). > 2. The right-hand side (RHS) of every constraint is **non-negative**. > 3. All decision variables are **non-negative** ($x_j \geq 0$). > 4. The objective function is expressed as either **maximize z** or **minimize z** explicitly. Conversion to equation form is **not optional** — the **Simplex tableau mechanics** (pivot row, pivot column, basis update) are mathematically defined only for systems of linear **equations**, not inequalities. Thus, equation form is the **bridge** between a real-world LP model and the Simplex algorithm. ## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations | Aspect | Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme | |---|---| | **CO Mapping** | Maps directly to **CO1** — "Formulate and solve LP problems using the Simplex Method" | | **Bloom's Level** | Tests **Apply (L3)**, **Analyze (L4)**, and **Create (L6)** cognitive levels | | **Mark Weightage** | 14-mark ESE questions routinely feature UDP-based formulation + conversion | | **Industry Relevance** | Mirrors real municipal planning, smart-city land-use modelling | # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Urban Development Planning (UDP) LP Model — Full Formulation Let the total available land be **800 acres**. Let: - $x_1$ = acres allocated to **Residential** - $x_2$ = acres allocated to **Commercial** - $x_3$ = acres allocated to **Industrial** - $x_4$ = acres allocated to **Recreation** ### Objective Function Each acre earns revenue (in **$1000s**) as: - Residential: **$5**, Commercial: **$9**, Industrial: **$7**, Recreation: **$2.5** $$ \boxed{\text{Maximize } z = 5x_1 + 9x_2 + 7x_3 + 2.5x_4} $$ ### Functional Constraints 1. **Residential ≥ 25% of developed land**: $$ x_1 \geq 0.25(x_1 + x_2 + x_3) \;\Rightarrow\; 0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \geq 0 $$ 2. **Commercial ≤ 30% of developed land**: $$ x_2 \leq 0.30(x_1 + x_2 + x_3) \;\Rightarrow\; -0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 \leq 0 $$ 3. **Recreation ≥ 10% of total 800 acres**: $$ x_4 \geq 0.10(800) = 80 \;\Rightarrow\; x_4 \geq 80 $$ 4. **Total developed land ≤ 70% of 800**: $$ x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \leq 0.70(800) = 560 $$ 5. **Commercial = Industrial** (zoning balance): $$ x_2 - x_3 = 0 $$ 6. **Non-negativity**: $$ x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4 \geq 0 $$ ## 2.2 Converting the UDP Model to Equation Form — The Three Variable Types > [!IMPORTANT] > The conversion hinges on understanding three distinct auxiliary variables. **Memorize this table** — KTU examiners frequently test this distinction. | Constraint Type | Variable Added | Sign Logic | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | $\leq$ constraint | **Slack Variable** $s_i$ | **Add** $s_i$ to LHS | Compensates for *unused* resource; makes the inequality an equality | | $\geq$ constraint | **Surplus Variable** $e_i$ | **Subtract** $e_i$ from LHS | Represents the *excess* above the minimum requirement | | $\geq$ or $=$ constraint | **Artificial Variable** $R_i$ | **Add** $R_i$ to LHS | Provides an initial feasible basis; used in Big-M / Two-Phase methods | ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below is the **canonical conversion reference** you must memorize for KTU exams. The "$\vert$" notation indicates absolute value / magnitude — implemented with $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ for LaTeX safety. | # | Original Constraint Form | Equation-Form Conversion | Variable Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\sum a_{ij} x_j \leq b_i, \; b_i \geq 0$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j + s_i = b_i$ | Slack $s_i$ | | 2 | $\sum a_{ij} x_j \geq b_i, \; b_i \geq 0$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j - e_i = b_i$ | Surplus $e_i$ | | 3 | $\sum a_{ij} x_j \geq b_i$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j - e_i + R_i = b_i$ | Surplus + Artificial | | 4 | $\sum a_{ij} x_j = b_i$ | $\sum a_{ij} x_j + R_i = b_i$ | Artificial only | | 5 | $b_i < 0$ (negative RHS) | Multiply both sides by $-1$, then use Rule 1–4 | Sign correction | | 6 | $\lvert x_j \rvert$ allowed (unrestricted) | Substitute $x_j = x_j^{\,+} - x_j^{\,-}$, both $\geq 0$ | Variable splitting | ### General Standard Form (Matrix Notation) For an LP with $m$ constraints and $n$ original variables: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } z &= \mathbf{c}^{\mathsf{T}} \mathbf{x} \\[2pt] \text{subject to } \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} &= \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{b} \geq \mathbf{0} \\[2pt] \mathbf{x} &\geq \mathbf{0} \end{aligned} $$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is an $m \times n$ matrix. To run the Simplex Method, $\mathbf{A}$ must contain an **identity sub-matrix** (a starting BFS), which is achieved by including the appropriate slack / artificial variables. ## 2.4 The Big-M Penalty for Artificial Variables > [!IMPORTANT] > **Big-M Method Objective Function Rule:** > When artificial variables $R_i$ are introduced, they must be penalized in the objective with a very large constant $M$ (for maximization: subtract $M R_i$; for minimization: add $M R_i$). This forces the algorithm to **drive $R_i$ to zero** in any optimal solution, since $R_i > 0$ would make the objective infeasible / unbounded. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Smart-City Land Use Optimization**: Direct application in municipal GIS planning. - **Industrial Site Selection**: Allocating factory, warehouse, and green-zone areas. - **Aerospace Mission Planning**: Resource allocation across mission phases (analogous constraints). - **Economics**: Capital budgeting with multiple resource ceilings. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Converting the UDP Problem to Equation Form — Exhaustive Walk-Through We start from the UDP model defined in §2.1. We analyze each constraint, identify its inequality type, and apply the correct conversion rule. ### Constraint 1 — Residential ≥ 25% of developed land **Original**: $$ x_1 \geq 0.25(x_1 + x_2 + x_3) $$ **Step 1** — Distribute the 0.25: $$ x_1 \geq 0.25x_1 + 0.25x_2 + 0.25x_3 $$ **Step 2** — Move all variables to the LHS: $$ x_1 - 0.25x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \geq 0 $$ **Step 3** — Simplify: $$ 0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \geq 0 $$ **Step 4** — Identify type: this is a **$\geq$ constraint** with RHS $b_1 = 0 \geq 0$. We must introduce a **surplus variable $e_1$** (subtract) AND an **artificial variable $R_1$** (add) to create an initial identity basis for the Simplex Method. > [!NOTE] > **Why both surplus AND artificial?** A surplus variable alone would create a $-1$ coefficient in the starting basis — not an identity. The artificial variable $R_1$ provides the missing $+1$ coefficient, which is essential for the initial BFS. We must then penalize $R_1$ in the objective with the **Big-M penalty**. **Step 5** — Equation form: $$ 0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 - e_1 + R_1 = 0 $$ ### Constraint 2 — Commercial ≤ 30% of developed land **Original**: $$ x_2 \leq 0.30(x_1 + x_2 + x_3) $$ **Step 1** — Distribute: $$ x_2 \leq 0.30x_1 + 0.30x_2 + 0.30x_3 $$ **Step 2** — LHS collection: $$ -0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 \leq 0 $$ **Step 3** — Type: **$\leq$ constraint** with $b_2 = 0$. Add a **slack variable $s_2$**: **Step 4** — Equation form: $$ -0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 + s_2 = 0 $$ ### Constraint 3 — Recreation ≥ 80 acres **Original**: $$ x_4 \geq 80 $$ **Step 1** — Type: **$\geq$ constraint** with $b_3 = 80 \geq 0$. Subtract surplus variable $e_3$ and add artificial variable $R_3$. **Step 2** — Equation form: $$ x_4 - e_3 + R_3 = 80 $$ ### Constraint 4 — Total developed land ≤ 560 **Original**: $$ x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \leq 560 $$ **Step 1** — Type: **$\leq$ constraint** with $b_4 = 560 \geq 0$. Add slack variable $s_4$. **Step 2** — Equation form: $$ x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + s_4 = 560 $$ ### Constraint 5 — Commercial = Industrial **Original**: $$ x_2 - x_3 = 0 $$ **Step 1** — Type: **$=$ constraint** with $b_5 = 0$.
