Add only an artificial variable $R_5$ (no surplus needed, since nothing is "above" or "below" a strict equality). **Step 2** — Equation form: $$ x_2 - x_3 + R_5 = 0 $$ ## 3.2 The Complete UDP Model in Equation Form (with Big-M Objective) We collect all five constraints, the modified objective, and non-negativity: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Maximize } z \;=\; & 5x_1 + 9x_2 + 7x_3 + 2.5x_4 + 0s_2 + 0s_4 - 0e_1 - 0e_3 - M R_1 - M R_3 - M R_5 \\[4pt] \text{Subject to:} \quad & 0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \phantom{{} - e_1 + R_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad} = 0 \\[2pt] & -0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 \phantom{{} - e_1 + R_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad\quad} + s_2 \phantom{{} = 0} = 0 \\[2pt] & x_4 \phantom{{} - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \phantom{{} - e_1} - e_3 + R_3 = 80 \\[2pt] & x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \phantom{{} - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \phantom{{} - e_1} + s_4 = 560 \\[2pt] & x_2 - x_3 \phantom{{} - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \phantom{{} - e_1} + R_5 = 0 \\[2pt] & x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4, \, s_2, s_4, \, e_1, e_3, \, R_1, R_3, R_5 \;\geq\; 0 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > The $\boxed{M}$ symbol denotes an **arbitrarily large positive penalty constant**. KTU examiners accept $M \to \infty$. In computations, any $M$ coefficient dominates all other coefficients in row-reduction. ## 3.3 The Initial Simplex Tableau The Initial Basis consists of the artificial variables $R_1$, $s_2$, $R_3$, $s_4$, $R_5$. The first tableau (before any pivoting) is constructed by writing the objective row as $(z_j - c_j)$ values: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Initial Basis: } & B_0 = \{ R_1, s_2, R_3, s_4, R_5 \} \\[4pt] \text{Initial BFS: } & (x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4, R_1, s_2, R_3, s_4, R_5) = (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 80, 560, 0) \end{aligned} $$ The $(z_j - c_j)$ row for the artificial variables under the Big-M method becomes: $$ (z_j - c_j)_{R_i} = 0 - (-M) = +M $$ So the initial objective row contains a $+M$ penalty coefficient for every column of $R_1, R_3, R_5$, and $0$ elsewhere. The pivot column selection follows the standard rule: **most positive $(z_j - c_j)$** for a maximization problem. ## 3.4 Numerical Sanity Check — Why a Starting Basis Is Mandatory > [!IMPORTANT] > A valid initial **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** requires exactly $m$ basic variables (one per row). In the UDP problem, $m = 5$. The 5 basic variables in our starting tableau are $R_1, s_2, R_3, s_4, R_5$ — providing a **non-singular identity sub-matrix** in the LHS coefficient matrix. This is the *the* condition that lets Simplex pivoting commence. ## 3.5 Conversion Rule Summary (Memorization Card) | Given | $\leq$ with $b \geq 0$ | $\geq$ with $b \geq 0$ | $=$ with $b \geq 0$ | $b < 0$ (any type) | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Action** | Add $s_i$ | Subtract $e_i$ + Add $R_i$ | Add $R_i$ | Multiply constraint by $-1$ first | > [!NOTE] > When a $\leq$ constraint has $b_i < 0$, multiply the entire row by $-1$. This **flips** the inequality direction (e.g., $\leq$ becomes $\geq$), and the standard rule for the *new* direction applies. KTU students frequently forget this sign-flip — see §5.4 examiner warning. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Conversion Pipeline (LP → Equation Form) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Real World LP Problem
e.g. UDP Land Allocation] --> B[Step 1: Identify Decision Variables
x1, x2, x3, x4] B --> C[Step 2: Formulate Objective Function
Max z = 5x1 + 9x2 + 7x3 + 2.5x4] C --> D[Step 3: Identify Constraint Types
le, ge, eq] D --> E[Step 4: Add Auxiliary Variables
Slack s, Surplus e, Artificial R] E --> F[Step 5: Modify Objective Function
Big-M Penalty on R] F --> G[Step 6: Build Initial Simplex Tableau
Identity Submatrix in Basis] G --> H[Step 7: Run Simplex Iterations
Pivot on most positive zj-cj] H --> I[Optimal Solution
BFS at Vertex] I --> J[Step 8: Reverse Engineer to Original Variables
Drop Slack Surplus Artificial] A -.real example.-> K[Urban Development Planning
640 or 800 acres] K -.feeds.-> B style A fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#990000,color:#000000 style I fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 style J fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000000 style G fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 style F fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Variable Type Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR P[Examine Constraint Type] --> Q{Is it
less than or equal?} P --> R{Is it
greater than or equal?} P --> S{Is it
equality?} Q -->|YES| Q1[Add Slack Variable s
coefficient is plus 1] R -->|YES| R1[Subtract Surplus e
plus 1] S -->|YES| S1[Add Artificial R
plus 1] R1 --> R2[ALSO add Artificial R
to form initial identity basis] S1 --> S2[Artificial R only] Q1 --> T[Equation Form Ready
for Simplex Tableau] R2 --> T S2 --> T T --> U{Penalty R in
Objective Function?} U -->|YES, Big-M| V[Subtract MR from Maximize
or Add MR to Minimize] style Q fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000000 style R fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000000 style S fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0a58ca,color:#000000 style T fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#0f5132,color:#000000 style V fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Initial Basis Composition for the UDP Model ```mermaid flowchart TD UDP[UDP Model
5 Functional Constraints] --> R1[Constraint 1: Residential ge 0
Basis Variable R1] UDP --> S2[Constraint 2: Commercial le 0
Basis Variable s2] UDP --> R3[Constraint 3: Recreation ge 80
Basis Variable R3] UDP --> S4[Constraint 4: Developed le 560
Basis Variable s4] UDP --> R5[Constraint 5: Commercial eq Industrial
Basis Variable R5] R1 --> INIT[Initial Basis B0
R1, s2, R3, s4, R5] S2 --> INIT R3 --> INIT S4 --> INIT R5 --> INIT INIT --> PEN[Apply Big-M Penalty
MR1 plus MR3 plus MR5 in z row] PEN --> TAB[Ready for Simplex Method
Initial Tableau Tab0] style UDP fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000000 style INIT fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000000 style TAB fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000000 style PEN fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000000 ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]** `[CO1, L2-Remember]` > **Q1.** Define a *slack variable* in a Linear Programming model. When is it introduced, and what does it represent economically in the context of an urban land-planning problem? ### Model Answer (3 marks): A **slack variable**, denoted $s_i \geq 0$, is added to a **"less-than-or-equal-to" ($\leq$)** constraint to convert it into an equality. It represents the **amount of unused (surplus) resource** available at the optimal point. *In the UDP context:* If Constraint 4 is $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + s_4 = 560$, then $s_4$ denotes the **undeveloped acres of land** at the optimum (i.e., the gap between the 560-acre development cap and the actual developed acreage). The variable $s_i$ is **basic** in the initial tableau and equals $b_i$ when the LP starts. **(3 marks)** --- > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]** `[CO1, L4-Analyze]` > **Q2.** Distinguish between a **slack variable**, a **surplus variable**, and an **artificial variable**. State clearly which constraint types each is associated with. ### Model Answer (3 marks): | Type | Constraint Form | Sign Convention | Example in UDP | |---|---|---|---| | **Slack** $s_i$ | $\leq$ | Added (+ sign) to LHS | Constraint 2 (Commercial $\leq$) and 4 (Developed land $\leq$) | | **Surplus** $e_i$ | $\geq$ | Subtracted ($-$ sign) from LHS | Constraints 1 (Residential $\geq$) and 3 (Recreation $\geq$) | | **Artificial** $R_i$ | $\geq$ or $=$ | Added (+ sign) to LHS to seed initial basis | Constraints 1, 3, and 5 (all $\geq$/$=$) | A **surplus variable** measures *excess* above a minimum target, while an **artificial variable** has no physical meaning — it is a *mathematical device* used solely to create an identity sub-matrix in the initial simplex tableau. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **ESE Instruction Reminder (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Answer *one* full question (either **Question A** OR **Question B**). Each part carries **7 marks**. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Module 1 ESE]` **(a)** Formulate the **Urban Development Planning LP model** for the 640-acre city scenario, given the following data: - Residential revenue: $5,000/acre; Commercial: $9,000/acre; Industrial: $7,000/acre; Recreation: $2,500/acre - Residential must be ≥ 25% of developed land - Commercial must be ≤ 30% of developed land - Recreation must be ≥ 10% of total 640 acres - Total developed land ≤ 70% of 640 acres - Commercial allocation = Industrial allocation **(7 marks)** **(b)** Convert the model from part (a) into **equation form**, identifying the type of each auxiliary variable, and **state the initial basis** along with the modified objective function (Big-M method). **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question A #### Part (a) — Formulation (7 marks) **Step 1 — Decision Variables** (1 mark): Let $x_1$ = acres of residential, $x_2$ = commercial, $x_3$ = industrial, $x_4$ = recreation. All $x_j \geq 0$. **Step 2 — Objective Function** (1 mark): $$ \boxed{\text{Max } z = 5x_1 + 9x_2 + 7x_3 + 2.5x_4 \quad (\text{revenue in } \$1000\text{s})} $$ **Step 3 — Constraint Translations** (5 marks): | # | Original Rule | Algebraic Form | |---|---|---| | 1 | Residential ≥ 25% of developed | $0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 \geq 0$ | | 2 | Commercial ≤ 30% of developed | $-0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 \leq 0$ | | 3 | Recreation ≥ 10% of 640 | $x_4 \geq 64$ | | 4 | Developed ≤ 70% of 640 | $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 \leq 448$ | | 5 | Commercial = Industrial | $x_2 - x_3 = 0$ | --- #### Part (b) — Conversion to Equation Form (7 marks) **Step 1 — Add auxiliary variables** (3 marks): | Constraint | Type | Variable(s) Added | Equation Form | |---|---|---|---| | 1 ($\geq$) | ge | Surplus $e_1$ ($-$) + Artificial $R_1$ ($+$) | $0.75x_1 - 0.25x_2 - 0.25x_3 - e_1 + R_1 = 0$ | | 2 ($\leq$) | le | Slack $s_2$ ($+$) | $-0.30x_1 + 0.70x_2 - 0.30x_3 + s_2 = 0$ | | 3 ($\geq$) | ge | Surplus $e_3$ ($-$) + Artificial $R_3$ ($+$) | $x_4 - e_3 + R_3 = 64$ | | 4 ($\leq$) | le | Slack $s_4$ ($+$) | $x_1 + x_2 + x_3 + s_4 = 448$ | | 5 ($=$) | eq | Artificial $R_5$ ($+$) | $x_2 - x_3 + R_5 = 0$ | **Step 2 — Big-M Objective** (2 marks): $$ \text{Max } z = 5x_1 + 9x_2 + 7x_3 + 2.5x_4 - 0e_1 - 0e_3 + 0s_2 + 0s_4 - MR_1 - MR_3 - MR_5 $$ **Step 3 — Initial Basis** (2 marks): $$ B_0 = \{ R_1, s_2, R_3, s_4, R_5 \}, \quad \text{with initial BFS } = (0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 64, 448, 0) $$ > **[End of Question A Solution — Total 14 marks]** --- ### ✅ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Module 1 ESE — Alternative]` **(a)** Explain, with a generic example, the **three-step procedure to convert any LP model from inequality form to equation form**. Clearly define each auxiliary variable you introduce. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A manufacturing firm produces two products $P_1$ and $P_2$ using two raw materials $R_1$ and $R_2$. The LP model is: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Max } z &= 4x_1 + 3x_2 \\ \text{s.t. } \quad 2x_1 + x_2 &\leq 10 \\ x_1 + 3x_2 &\geq 6 \\ x_2 &\leq 4 \\ x_1, x_2 &\geq 0 \end{aligned} $$ Convert this model to **equation form** using the **Big-M method**, write the modified objective function, and identify the initial basic feasible solution. **(7 marks)** --- ### Model Solution for Question B #### Part (a) — Three-Step Conversion Procedure (7 marks) **Step 1 — Constraint Classification** (2 marks): Scan every functional constraint. For each one, classify it as one of: - "$\leq$" (less-than-or-equal), - "$\geq$" (greater-than-or-equal), - "=" (equality). Ensure every RHS constant $b_i \geq 0$. If $b_i < 0$, multiply the entire row by $-1$ (this flips the inequality direction). **Step 2 — Add the Correct Auxiliary Variable(s)** (3 marks): | Constraint Type | Variable(s) Added | Mathematical Operation | |---|---|---| | $\leq$ | Slack $s_i$ | LHS $+ s_i$ | | $\geq$ | Surplus $e_i$ + Artificial $R_i$ | LHS $- e_i + R_i$ | | $=$ | Artificial $R_i$ | LHS $+ R_i$ | All auxiliary variables are constrained to be $\geq 0$. **Step 3 — Modify the Objective Function (Big-M Penalty)** (2 marks): For every artificial variable $R_i$ introduced, append a penalty term to the objective: - **Maximize**: subtract $M R_i$ from $z$ (with $M$ very large) - **Minimize**: add $M R_i$ to $z$ This ensures the optimizer will drive every $R_i$ to zero in the final solution. --- #### Part (b) — Manufacturing LP Conversion (7 marks) **Step 1 — Identify Constraint Types** (1 mark): - $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$ — "$\leq$" constraint - $x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 6$ — "$\geq$" constraint - $x_2 \leq 4$ — "$\leq$" constraint **Step 2 — Add Auxiliary Variables** (3 marks): | # | Original | Equation Form | Variable Type | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10$ | $2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 10$ | Slack $s_1$ | | 2 | $x_1 + 3x_2 \geq 6$ | $x_1 + 3x_2 - e_2 + R_2 = 6$ | Surplus $e_2$ + Artificial $R_2$ | | 3 | $x_2 \leq 4$ | $x_2 + s_3 = 4$ | Slack $s_3$ | **Step 3 — Modified Objective Function (Big-M)** (1 mark): $$ \boxed{\text{Max } z = 4x_1 + 3x_2 + 0s_1 + 0s_3 - 0e_2 - M R_2} $$ **Step 4 — Initial Basis and BFS** (2 marks): $$ B_0 = \{ s_1, R_2, s_3 \}, \quad \text{Initial BFS } = (x_1, x_2, s_1, e_2, R_2, s_3) = (0, 0, 10, 0, 6, 4) $$ The starting simplex tableau has 3 basic variables (one per constraint), forming an identity sub-matrix in the LHS coefficients. Pivoting can now begin. > **[End of Question B Solution — Total 14 marks]** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Zones — Read Carefully:** > > 1. **Forgetting the Big-M penalty on artificial variables** (–2 to –3 marks). If you add $R_i$ to a constraint but *do not* penalize it in the objective, the algorithm will happily leave $R_i > 0$ at the optimum, which is **mathematically invalid** for the original LP. > > 2. **Mixing up surplus and artificial variable signs** (–1 mark). A $\geq$ constraint needs BOTH a *surplus* (subtracted, $-$) AND an *artificial* (added, $+$). A $\leq$ constraint needs ONLY a *slack* (added, $+$). > > 3. **Not handling negative RHS correctly** (–2 marks). If $b_i < 0$, you MUST multiply the row by $-1$ BEFORE adding the auxiliary variable. Skipping this sign-flip produces a tableau that violates feasibility. > > 4. **Initial basis not forming an identity matrix** (–3 marks). After conversion, the basis columns of the starting BFS must each be a unit vector. If you fail to add artificial variables where needed, the initial tableau is *not* directly solvable. > > 5. **Dropping decision-variable non-negativity in the final answer** (–1 mark). Always state $x_j, s_i, e_i, R_i \geq 0$ explicitly when presenting the equation form. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **Final 60-Second Revision Card — KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1** > > ☐ **Urban Development Planning (UDP)** is the canonical LP example tying Simplex theory to a real land-allocation problem (residential, commercial, industrial, recreation). > > ☐ The **Standard Form (Equation Form)** is the *mandatory prerequisite* for running the Simplex Method — inequalities are converted to equalities via auxiliary variables. > > ☐ **Three Variable Types — Master the Mnemonic "S-S-A":** >    **S**lack $\Rightarrow$ "$\leq$" → add $s_i$ on LHS >    **S**urplus $\Rightarrow$ "$\geq$" → subtract $e_i$ on LHS >    **A**rtificial $\Rightarrow$ "$\geq$" or "=" → add $R_i$ on LHS (and penalize with $M$) > > ☐ **Big-M Rule:** In Maximization, objective becomes $z - \sum M R_i = c x$. In Minimization, $z + \sum M R_i = c x$. With $M$ arbitrarily large. > > ☐ **Initial Basis $B_0$** = set of $m$ variables (slacks + artificials) whose columns form an **identity sub-matrix** in the constraint LHS. The initial BFS sets non-basic variables to 0 and reads off basic variables directly from the RHS. > > ☐ **Negative-RHS Sign-Flip** is non-negotiable. Always inspect $b_i$ before conversion. > > ☐ **Unrestricted variables** $x_j$ (sign-free) must be split as $x_j = x_j^{+} - x_j^{-}$ with both $\geq 0$. > > ☐ **Optimality Test for Max:** Stop when ALL $(z_j - c_j) \leq 0$ in the objective row. > > ☐ **Optimality Test for Min:** Stop when ALL $(z_j - c_j) \geq 0$ in the objective row. > > ☐ **KEEP** the Big-M penalty **visible in your answer sheet** — examiners deduct marks if the modified objective function is not explicitly written.

topics.title: The Simplex Method: Iterative Nature of the Simplex Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** The Simplex Method: Iterative Nature of the Simplex Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Simplex Method (Dantzig, 1947)** is an **iterative, tabular algorithm** for solving Linear Programming Problems (LPPs) in standard form. It systematically moves from one **vertex (corner point / Basic Feasible Solution)** of the convex feasible region to an **adjacent vertex** with a strictly improving (or non-decreasing) objective value, terminating at the **optimal basic feasible solution (OBFS)** when no further improvement is possible.

Mathematically, given the standard form:

$$\text{Maximize } Z = \mathbf{C}^{\top}\mathbf{X} \quad \text{subject to} \quad \mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{b}, \quad \mathbf{X} \geq \mathbf{0}$$

The simplex method repeatedly evaluates the reduced cost row $(Z_j - C_j)$, performs a **pivot operation** (Gauss–Jordan elimination on a chosen pivot element), and updates the basis until the **optimality criterion** is satisfied.

### 1.2 The "Iterative" Nature — What Does It Mean?

The term **iterative** literally means *repeating a defined sequence of operations*. The simplex method is iterative because:

1. It starts at **one corner (BFS)** of the feasible polyhedron.
2. It computes a **direction of improvement** (the entering variable column).
3. It moves to an **adjacent corner** along an edge of the polyhedron.
4. It **re-evaluates** optimality at the new corner.
5. Steps 2–4 are **repeated** until the optimum is reached.

### 1.3 Real-World Analogy — A Hiker on a Mountain in the Dark

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Imagine you are blindfolded on a mountainous terrain and told to reach the highest peak. You cannot see the whole landscape. The simplex method is your strategy:
> - **Standing position** = Current Basic Feasible Solution (BFS).
> - **Feeling the slope locally** = Computing the $Z_j - C_j$ row (reduced costs).
> - **Choosing the steepest ascent** = Selecting the entering variable (most negative $Z_j - C_j$).
> - **Step length** = Limited by the minimum ratio test (you cannot step off the feasible region).
> - **Reaching the summit** = Optimality reached when no upward slope exists.

Every "iteration" is one careful **step from one plateau to a higher adjacent plateau**.

### 1.4 Key Terminology

| Term | Meaning |
|------|---------|
| **Basic Variable (BV)** | A variable corresponding to an identity column in the basis |
| **Non-Basic Variable (NBV)** | A variable currently set to **0** |
| **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** | A solution where NBV = 0 and all BV ≥ 0 |
| **Adjacent BFS** | Two BFSs differ in **exactly one** basic variable (swap of one variable) |
| **Pivot Element** | The intersection of the entering column and leaving row |
| **Optimal BFS** | A BFS satisfying the optimality condition |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Flow of a Single Iteration

Each iteration of the simplex method executes the following **four canonical steps**:

**Step 1 — Optimality Test (Stop Condition Check)**
- If every $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ in a **maximization** problem, the current BFS is **optimal**. Stop.
- If any $Z_j - C_j < 0$, improvement is possible. Proceed to Step 2.

**Step 2 — Selection of Entering Variable**
- Choose the column with the **most negative** $Z_j - C_j$ (steepest improvement per unit).
- Tie-breaking: Smallest subscript index (lexicographic / Bland's rule to avoid cycling).
- If all entries $a_{ij} \leq 0$ in this column, the LP is **unbounded**. Stop.

**Step 3 — Minimum Ratio Test (Leaving Variable Selection)**
- For each row $i$ where $a_{ij} > 0$ (positive pivot column entry), compute:

$$\theta_i = \frac{b_i}{a_{ij}}$$

- The **leaving variable** is the one in the row with the **smallest** non-negative ratio.
- This guarantees the next BFS remains **feasible** (all BV ≥ 0).

**Step 4 — Pivot Operation (Gauss–Jordan Elimination)**
- **Normalize** the pivot row: divide it by the pivot element.
- **Eliminate** the entering variable's coefficient from all other rows (including the $Z$-row).
- A **new BFS** emerges at the adjacent corner.

### 2.2 Why Is the Method Guaranteed to Converge?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fundamental Theorem of the Simplex Method:** *Every LP with a bounded optimum has at most $\binom{n+m}{m}$ basic feasible solutions, where $n$ is the number of original variables and $m$ is the number of constraints (after standardisation). Since the objective strictly improves at each non-degenerate pivot, the algorithm must terminate in **finite iterations**.*

For typical problems, the number of iterations is empirically between $m$ and $3m$, far less than the theoretical worst case.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition | Notes |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------|
| 1 | Standard Form (Max) | $\text{Max } Z = \sum C_j X_j, \sum a_{ij} X_j = b_i, X_j \geq 0$ | Slack/surplus/artificial added |
| 2 | Basic Solution | Set $n$ NBV = 0; solve $m$ equations for $m$ BV | BFS requires all $X_B \geq 0$ |
| 3 | Objective Value | $Z = \mathbf{C}_B \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{b}$ | Computed at each iteration |
| 4 | Reduced Cost (Net Eval.) | $Z_j - C_j = \mathbf{C}_B \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_j - C_j$ | Decision driver for entering var |
| 5 | Optimality (Max) | $Z_j - C_j \geq 0 \; \forall j$ | **STOP** condition |
| 6 | Optimality (Min) | $Z_j - C_j \leq 0 \; \forall j$ | Sign flips for minimisation |
| 7 | Entering Variable (Max) | $\min_j (Z_j - C_j)$ | Most negative value |
| 8 | Minimum Ratio | $\theta_r = \min_{i: a_{ij} > 0} \left(\frac{b_i}{a_{ij}}\right)$ | Leaves if any $a_{ij} \leq 0$ |
| 9 | Unboundedness | All $a_{ij} \leq 0$ in entering column | **STOP** with no finite optimum |
| 10 | Maximum BFS Count | $\binom{n+m}{m}$ | Theoretical worst case |
| 11 | Degeneracy | Any $X_{B_i} = 0$ in a BFS | Risk of cycling |
| 12 | Cycling | Repeated revisit of same BFS | Avoided via Bland's rule |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The simplex method is the **workhorse solver** behind:
- **Production planning** in manufacturing (product mix optimisation).
- **Blending problems** in petroleum refineries and diet planning.
- **Network flow** problems (transportation, assignment).
- **Portfolio optimisation** in finance.
- **Airline crew scheduling** and **logistics routing**.

It is the default LP solver in MATLAB (`linprog`), Python's `scipy.optimize.linprog` (for small/medium problems), Gurobi, CPLEX, and GLPK.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Master Worked Example — Showing the Iterative Process End-to-End

**Problem Statement (KTC 2024 style):**

$$\text{Maximise } Z = 3x_1 + 2x_2$$

subject to:

$$x_1 + x_2 \leq 4, \quad x_1 - x_2 \leq 2, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

#### **Step 0 — Convert to Standard Form**

Introduce slack variables $s_1, s_2 \geq 0$:

$$x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 4 \quad \ldots (1)$$
$$x_1 - x_2 + s_2 = 2 \quad \ldots (2)$$

The initial basis is $\{s_1, s_2\}$ with NBV $\{x_1, x_2\}$.

Initial BFS: $x_1 = 0, \; x_2 = 0, \; s_1 = 4, \; s_2 = 2, \; Z = 0$.

#### **Step 1 — Initial Simplex Tableau**

| Iteration | Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio $\theta$ |
|:---------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:--------------:|
| **0** (Init) | $s_1$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 | $4/1 = 4$ |
| | $s_2$ | 1 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | $2/1 = 2 \;\leftarrow$ |
| | $Z$ row | $-3$ | $-2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | |

**Reading the $Z$-row:** The coefficients are $-C_j$ (negative objective coefficients for NBV). Since $-3 < -2 < 0$, $x_1$ is the most negative — it **enters the basis**.

**Minimum Ratio Test:** $4/1 = 4$, $2/1 = 2$. Minimum is **2** in row of $s_2$, so $s_2$ **leaves**.

**Pivot element:** $a_{21} = 1$ (intersection of $x_1$ column and $s_2$ row).

#### **Step 2 — First Pivot Operation (Gauss–Jordan)**

Pivot element is already 1. The required row operations:

- $R_1 \leftarrow R_1 - 1 \cdot R_2$ (eliminate $x_1$ from row 1)
- $R_2 \leftarrow R_2 / 1$ (normalize pivot row, no change)
- $R_Z \leftarrow R_Z + 3 \cdot R_2$ (eliminate $x_1$ from $Z$-row)

**Row 1 computation:**

$$R_1 = (1-1)x_1 + (1-(-1))x_2 + (1-0)s_1 + (0-1)s_2 = 4 - 2$$
$$\Rightarrow 0 x_1 + 2 x_2 + s_1 - s_2 = 2$$

**$Z$-row computation:**

$$R_Z = (-3+3)x_1 + (-2+3)x_2 + (0+0)s_1 + (0+3)s_2 = 0 + 6$$
$$\Rightarrow 0 x_1 + 1 x_2 + 0 s_1 + 3 s_2 = 6$$

**Resulting Tableau (Iteration 1):**

| Iteration | Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | Ratio $\theta$ |
|:---------:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:--------------:|
| **1** | $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | $-1$ | 2 | $2/2 = 1 \;\leftarrow$ |
| | $x_1$ | 1 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 | $-$ (negative, skip) |
| | $Z$ row | 0 | $1$ | 0 | 3 | 6 | |

Wait — the $Z$-row coefficient for $x_2$ is $+1$, but the optimality condition for a **maximization** problem requires all $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$. Let me re-examine the sign convention.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Used in this Module:** The $Z$-row shows $(Z_j - C_j)$ values **directly** (i.e., the *net evaluation row*). The tableau row reads "$C_j$ - $Z_j$" in the $Z$-row entries. Hence, for maximization, all entries should be $\leq 0$ for optimality. To avoid confusion, **we adopt the convention** where the $Z$-row displays $-C_j$ for NBVs initially, and we look for **most negative** to enter, with **optimality = all $Z$-row entries $\geq 0$** (i.e., $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$).

Under this convention, the $Z$-row is the **net evaluation row** with $Z_j - C_j$ values. Let me re-construct the tableau using this convention clearly.

#### **Clean Re-construction (Convention: $Z_j - C_j$ Row)**

For **Maximization**, place $-C_j$ in the $Z$-row. The **entering column** is the **most negative** entry, and **optimality** is achieved when **all $Z$-row entries are $\geq 0$**.

**Initial Tableau (Iteration 0):**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| $s_1$ | 1 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| $s_2$ | 1 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| $Z_j - C_j$ | $\mathbf{-3}$ | $-2$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |

Most negative = $-3$ → $x_1$ enters. Ratios: $4/1 = 4$, $2/1 = 2$ → $s_2$ leaves. Pivot = 1.

**Pivot operations:** $R_1 \to R_1 - R_2$; $R_2 \to R_2$ (unchanged); $R_Z \to R_Z + 3R_2$.

**After Iteration 1:**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| $s_1$ | 0 | 2 | 1 | $-1$ | 2 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | $-1$ | 0 | 1 | 2 |
| $Z_j - C_j$ | 0 | $\mathbf{-5}$ | 0 | 3 | 6 |

Most negative = $-5$ → $x_2$ enters. Ratios: $2/2 = 1$, $2/(-1)$ (negative, skip) → $s_1$ leaves. Pivot = 2.

**Pivot operations:** $R_1 \to R_1 / 2$; $R_2 \to R_2 + R_1$; $R_Z \to R_Z + 5 R_1$.

**Row 1 (new pivot row):** $0 \cdot x_1 + 1 \cdot x_2 + \frac{1}{2} s_1 - \frac{1}{2} s_2 = 1$.

**Row 2:** $1 \cdot x_1 + (-1 + 1) x_2 + (0 + \frac{1}{2}) s_1 + (1 - \frac{1}{2}) s_2 = 2 + 1 = 3$.

**$Z$-row:** $0 \cdot x_1 + (-5 + 5) x_2 + (0 + \frac{5}{2}) s_1 + (3 - \frac{5}{2}) s_2 = 6 + 5 = 11$.

**After Iteration 2 (Final Optimal Tableau):**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | $1/2$ | $-1/2$ | 1 |
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | $1/2$ | $1/2$ | 3 |
| $Z_j - C_j$ | 0 | 0 | $\mathbf{5/2}$ | $\mathbf{1/2}$ | 11 |

All $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ → **OPTIMAL REACHED**.

#### **Final Solution:**

$$x_1^{*} = 3, \quad x_2^{*} = 1, \quad s_1^{*} = 0, \quad s_2^{*} = 0, \quad Z_{\max} = 11$$

### 3.2 Iteration Summary Table — The Heart of "Iterative Nature"

| Iteration | BFS (Corner Point) | $Z$ Value | Basis (BV) | Status |
|:---------:|:------------------:|:---------:|:----------:|:------:|
| 0 (Initial) | $(0, 0, 4, 2)$ | 0 | $\{s_1, s_2\}$ | Non-optimal |
| 1 | $(2, 0, 2, 0)$ | 6 | $\{s_1, x_1\}$ | Non-optimal |
| 2 (Final) | $(3, 1, 0, 0)$ | 11 | $\{x_1, x_2\}$ | **Optimal** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Observe the **monotonically increasing** $Z$-value: $0 \to 6 \to 11$. This monotonic improvement is the **mathematical signature of the iterative nature** of the simplex method. Each iteration strictly improves the objective (in non-degenerate cases) and corresponds to a **geometric move along an edge of the feasible polyhedron** to an **adjacent vertex**.

### 3.3 Algebraic Underpinning of a Pivot

The simplex tableau is a compact representation of the system:

$$\mathbf{B} \mathbf{X}_B + \mathbf{N} \mathbf{X}_N = \mathbf{b}$$

Multiplying both sides by $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$:

$$\mathbf{X}_B = \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{b} - \mathbf{B}^{-1}\mathbf{N}\mathbf{X}_N$$

Setting $\mathbf{X}_N = \mathbf{0}$ gives the current BFS. The reduced cost row encodes:

$$Z_j - C_j = \mathbf{C}_B \mathbf{B}^{-1} \mathbf{A}_j - C_j$$

A pivot is precisely a **rank-1 update** of $\mathbf{B}^{-1}$ via the **product form of the inverse**:

$$\mathbf{B}_{\text{new}}^{-1} = \mathbf{E}^{-1} \mathbf{B}^{-1}$$

where $\mathbf{E}$ is the elementary elimination matrix built from the pivot column entries (with the pivot element normalised to 1).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart of the Iterative Simplex Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Start: Formulate LPP in Standard Form with Slack Surplus and Artificial Variables"]
    initTab["Construct Initial Simplex Tableau using Initial Basis of Slack or Artificial Variables"]
    optTest["Optimality Test: Check Zj minus Cj row"]
    optQuery{"Are all Zj minus Cj greater than or equal to 0 for Maximisation"}
    stopOpt["STOP Current BFS is Optimal Report Solution"]
    enterVar["Select Entering Variable: Most Negative Coeff in Zj minus Cj Row"]
    unboundQuery{"All entries in Entering Column less than or equal to 0"}
    stopUnb["STOP LP is Unbounded No Finite Optimum"]
    leaveVar["Select Leaving Variable via Minimum Non Negative Ratio Test on RHS divided by Pivot Column"]
    pivotOp["Perform Gauss Jordan Row Operations: Normalise Pivot Row then Eliminate Entering Variable from All Other Rows and the Z Row"]
    updBFS["Update Basis Recompute Z Value Return to Optimality Test"]
    startNode --> initTab
    initTab --> optTest
    optTest --> optQuery
    optQuery -- Yes --> stopOpt
    optQuery -- No --> enterVar
    enterVar --> unboundQuery
    unboundQuery -- Yes --> stopUnb
    unboundQuery -- No --> leaveVar
    leaveVar --> pivotOp
    pivotOp --> updBFS
    updBFS --> optTest
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Simplex Method as an Iterative Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock["INPUT MODULE\nStandard Form LPP\nObjective + Constraints"]
    standardise["STANDARDISATION\nAdd Slack / Surplus / Artificial Variables"]
    tableauBlock["TABLEAU BUILDER\nConstruct Initial Simplex Tableau\nNBV set to zero gives Initial BFS"]
    iterationLoop["ITERATION ENGINE\nFour Step Subroutine\nOptimality Test\nEntering Variable\nLeaving Variable\nGauss Jordan Pivot"]
    decisionBlock{"DECISION GATE\nOptimal or Unbounded or Continue"}
    outputBlock["OUTPUT MODULE\nOptimal Decision Variables\nOptimal Z Value\nStatus Flag"]
    inputBlock --> standardise
    standardise --> tableauBlock
    tableauBlock --> iterationLoop
    iterationLoop --> decisionBlock
    decisionBlock -- Continue Iteration --> iterationLoop
    decisionBlock -- Stop Optimal --> outputBlock
    decisionBlock -- Stop Unbounded --> outputBlock
```

### 4.3 Geometric Interpretation of Iteration Movement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0["BFS 0\nOrigin\nZ equals 0"]
    p1["BFS 1\nTwo comma Zero\nZ equals 6"]
    p2["BFS 2\nThree comma One\nZ equals 11\nOPTIMAL"]
    p0 -- Edge Move 1 --> p1
    p1 -- Edge Move 2 --> p2
```

> [!NOTE]
> The three points $(0,0)$, $(2,0)$, and $(3,1)$ are **adjacent vertices** of the feasible polygon. The simplex method traces the path $(0,0) \to (2,0) \to (3,1)$, never exploring interior points — only corner points (BFSs). This is the geometric essence of the **iterative** simplex method.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define a *Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)* in the context of the simplex method. How is it different from a *Basic Solution*?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A *Basic Solution* of a system $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{b}$ (with $m$ equations, $n$ unknowns, $m \leq n$, $\text{rank}(\mathbf{A}) = m$) is obtained by setting $n - m$ variables (called **non-basic variables**) to zero and solving the resulting $m$ equations for the remaining $m$ variables (called **basic variables**). A *Basic Feasible Solution* is a basic solution in which **all basic variables are non-negative**, i.e., $X_B \geq 0$. A basic solution may have negative values (infeasible); a BFS lies on or inside the feasible region. The simplex method operates **exclusively** on BFSs, traversing from one BFS to an adjacent BFS.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** State the optimality condition for a maximisation LPP in the simplex method. What happens if the optimality condition is satisfied but the slack variables are non-zero?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
For a maximisation LPP, the optimality condition is: **all $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$** in the net evaluation row of the simplex tableau. When this holds, the current BFS is the **optimal basic feasible solution** and the algorithm terminates. If some slack variables are non-zero in the optimal tableau, it means those resources are **surplus (underutilised)** at the optimal product mix — they are not binding constraints. The corresponding constraints are **non-redundant** in the sense that they do not restrict the optimum, but they still appear in the solution.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice: A or B)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question Set: Internal Choice]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Solve the following LPP using the simplex method. Identify the optimal solution and the value of the objective function. **(7 marks)**

$$\text{Maximise } Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$

subject to:

$$3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 18, \quad x_1 \leq 4, \quad x_2 \leq 6, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

**(b)** Explain the *iterative nature* of the simplex method. Discuss what happens in each iteration and how the algorithm moves from one BFS to the next. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — Full Step-by-Step (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Standard Form.** Introduce slack variables $s_1, s_2, s_3 \geq 0$:

$$3x_1 + 2x_2 + s_1 = 18$$
$$x_1 + s_2 = 4$$
$$x_2 + s_3 = 6$$

**Step 2: Initial Tableau.**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | $\theta$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|:--------:|
| $s_1$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 18 | $18/2 = 9$ |
| $s_2$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 | $4/0 = \infty$ (skip) |
| $s_3$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 6 | $6/1 = 6 \;\leftarrow$ |
| $Z-C$ | $-3$ | $\mathbf{-5}$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | |

Most negative = $-5$ → $x_2$ enters. Minimum ratio = $6$ in row of $s_3$ → $s_3$ leaves. Pivot = 1.

**Step 3: Pivot.** $R_3 \to R_3$ (unchanged); $R_1 \to R_1 - 2R_3$; $R_2 \to R_2$; $R_Z \to R_Z + 5R_3$.

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS | $\theta$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|:--------:|
| $s_1$ | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-2$ | 6 | $6/3 = 2 \;\leftarrow$ |
| $s_2$ | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 | $4/1 = 4$ |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 6 | $-$ |
| $Z-C$ | $\mathbf{-3}$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 5 | 30 | |

Most negative = $-3$ → $x_1$ enters. Minimum ratio = $2$ in row of $s_1$ → $s_1$ leaves. Pivot = 3.

**Step 4: Second Pivot.** $R_1 \to R_1/3$; $R_2 \to R_2 - (1/3)R_1$; $R_3 \to R_3$; $R_Z \to R_Z + 3R_1$.

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | $s_3$ | RHS |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| $x_1$ | 1 | 0 | $1/3$ | 0 | $-2/3$ | 2 |
| $s_2$ | 0 | 0 | $-1/3$ | 1 | $2/3$ | 2 |
| $x_2$ | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 6 |
| $Z-C$ | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 3 | 36 |

All $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ → **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 2, \; x_2^* = 6, \; s_1^* = 0, \; s_2^* = 2, \; s_3^* = 0, \; Z_{\max} = 36$.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — Theoretical (7 marks)**

The simplex method is **iterative** because it does not compute the optimum in a single shot; instead, it performs a **sequence of pivot operations**, each transforming one BFS into an **adjacent BFS** with a strictly improved (or equal) objective value.

**What happens in each iteration:**

1. **Optimality Test:** The algorithm checks the $Z_j - C_j$ row. If all entries are non-negative (maximisation), the process halts — the current BFS is optimal. Otherwise, an improvement is possible.

2. **Entering Variable Selection:** The variable with the most negative $Z_j - C_j$ is selected to enter the basis. This represents the steepest unit improvement in $Z$.

3. **Leaving Variable Selection via Minimum Ratio Test:** The variable that limits the extent to which the entering variable can grow (smallest non-negative $b_i / a_{ij}$ ratio) is selected to leave. This guarantees the new solution remains feasible.

4. **Pivot Operation (Gauss–Jordan Elimination):** The pivot element is normalised to 1, and the entering variable is eliminated from all other rows. The result is a new tableau representing the **adjacent BFS** at the new corner of the feasible region.

**Geometric Interpretation:** The feasible region is a convex polyhedron. The simplex method traces a **path along the edges** of this polyhedron from one vertex to another, **always moving uphill** in the direction of increasing $Z$. The number of iterations is bounded above by $\binom{n+m}{m}$, and in practice is much smaller (typically $\mathcal{O}(m)$).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Write down the **step-by-step algorithm** of the simplex method for a maximisation LPP. Clearly explain the role of (i) entering variable, (ii) leaving variable, and (iii) the minimum ratio test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the following LPP using the simplex method: **(7 marks)**

$$\text{Maximise } Z = 4x_1 + 10x_2$$

subject to:

$$2x_1 + x_2 \leq 10, \quad 2x_1 + 5x_2 \leq 20, \quad x_1, x_2 \geq 0$$

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — Algorithm (7 marks)**

**Algorithm: Simplex Method (Maximisation)**

1. **Convert to Standard Form:** Add slack variables to "$\leq$" constraints, surplus + artificial variables to "$\geq$" constraints, and pure artificial variables to "$=$" constraints (use Big-M or Two-Phase).

2. **Construct Initial Tableau:** Coefficients of slack variables form the initial identity basis. Set up the cost row as $-C_j$ for non-basic variables.

3. **Optimality Test:** If all entries in the $Z_j - C_j$ row are $\geq 0$, **STOP** — the current BFS is optimal.

4. **Select Entering Variable:** Choose the column with the **most negative** $Z_j - C_j$ value. This is the variable that will **enter** the basis (it currently equals zero but will become positive in the next BFS).

5. **Select Leaving Variable via Minimum Ratio Test:** For each row $i$ with $a_{ij} > 0$ in the entering column, compute $\theta_i = b_i / a_{ij}$. The row with the **minimum non-negative** $\theta_i$ determines the leaving variable. The minimum ratio test ensures that **no basic variable becomes negative** in the new BFS (preserves feasibility).

6. **Unboundedness Check:** If no $a_{ij} > 0$ exists in the entering column, the LP is **unbounded**. **STOP**.

7. **Pivot Operation:** Divide the pivot row by the pivot element (normalisation). Use elementary row operations to make all other entries in the entering column equal to zero.

8. **Update Basis and Repeat:** The entering and leaving variables swap roles. Return to Step 3.

**Role Summary:**

- **Entering Variable:** Increases from 0; chosen by steepest ascent on the objective.
- **Leaving Variable:** Drops to 0; chosen by the binding constraint (minimum ratio).
- **Minimum Ratio Test:** Prevents infeasibility in the new BFS by bounding the entering variable's growth.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — Full Numerical (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Standard Form.** Add $s_1, s_2$:

$$2x_1 + x_2 + s_1 = 10, \quad 2x_1 + 5x_2 + s_2 = 20$$

**Step 2: Initial Tableau.**

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS | $\theta$ |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|:--------:|
| $s_1$ | 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 10 | $10/1 = 10$ |
| $s_2$ | 2 | 5 | 0 | 1 | 20 | $20/5 = 4 \;\leftarrow$ |
| $Z-C$ | $-4$ | $\mathbf{-10}$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | |

Most negative = $-10$ → $x_2$ enters. Min ratio = 4 in row of $s_2$ → $s_2$ leaves. Pivot = 5.

**Step 3: Pivot.** $R_2 \to R_2 / 5 = (2/5, 1, 0, 1/5 \mid 4)$. Then $R_1 \to R_1 - R_2' = (2 - 2/5, 0, 1, -1/5 \mid 6) = (8/5, 0, 1, -1/5 \mid 6)$. Then $R_Z \to R_Z + 10 R_2' = (0, 0, 0, 2 \mid 40)$.

| Basis | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $s_1$ | $s_2$ | RHS |
|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:---:|
| $s_1$ | $8/5$ | 0 | 1 | $-1/5$ | 6 |
| $x_2$ | $2/5$ | 1 | 0 | $1/5$ | 4 |
| $Z-C$ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 2 | 40 |

All $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ → **OPTIMAL**.

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1^* = 0, \; x_2^* = 4, \; s_1^* = 6, \; s_2^* = 0, \; Z_{\max} = 40$.

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Sign error in $Z$-row:** Students often forget that the $Z$-row entry for non-basic variables is $-C_j$ (negative of objective coefficient). If a student writes $+C_j$ instead, the optimality test and entering variable selection both fail. **Always cross-check** by computing $Z = C_B X_B$ from RHS — it must match the $Z$-row constant.
> 2. **Ratio test on zero or negative pivot entries:** The minimum ratio test must be applied **only** to rows where the entering column entry is **strictly positive** ($a_{ij} > 0$). Using zero or negative entries produces infeasible BFSs.
> 3. **Forgetting to eliminate the entering variable from ALL rows** (including the $Z$-row) after pivoting — a frequent cause of incorrect solutions in the second iteration.
> 4. **Confusing "Basic Variable" with "Non-Zero Variable":** A basic variable can be **zero** in degenerate cases; this is distinct from a non-basic variable.
> 5. **Missing the unboundedness check:** If the entering column has all $a_{ij} \leq 0$, the LP is **unbounded** and the algorithm must STOP. Many students force a pivot and obtain a nonsense solution.
> 6. **Artificial variables in the answer:** When using the Big-M method, artificial variables must be **zero** in the final optimal solution. If they are non-zero, the original LPP is **infeasible**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The simplex method is an **iterative vertex-enumeration algorithm** that moves along edges of the feasible polyhedron.
- Each iteration produces a **new BFS** by swapping one basic variable with one non-basic variable.
- The **objective value strictly improves** (or stays equal) at each non-degenerate pivot: $Z^{(k+1)} \geq Z^{(k)}$.
- The **entering variable** is identified by the most negative $Z_j - C_j$ (maximisation).
- The **leaving variable** is identified by the **minimum ratio test** over positive pivot-column entries only.
- The **maximum number of iterations** is $\binom{n+m}{m}$ (theoretical worst case), but practical cases are $\mathcal{O}(m)$.
- **Optimality condition (Max):** All $Z_j - C_j \geq 0$ in the net evaluation row.
- **Unboundedness:** All entries in the entering column are $\leq 0$ (no positive ratio exists).
- **Infeasibility:** Detected when the Phase-I objective (Big-M) cannot drive all artificial variables to zero.
- **Degeneracy:** A basic variable is zero in a BFS — can cause cycling (preventable with Bland's rule: choose the smallest-index entering/leaving variable on ties).
- The simplex method is **finite** for non-degenerate problems and the algorithm always terminates at a vertex of the feasible region.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Details of the Simplex Algorithm, Artificial Starting Solution, M-Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computational Details of the Simplex Algorithm, Artificial Starting Solution, M-Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Simplex Algorithm**, developed by **George Dantzig in 1947**, is an iterative, algebraic procedure for solving **Linear Programming Problems (LPPs)** by systematically moving from one **Basic Feasible Solution (BFS)** vertex of the feasible region to an adjacent one along an edge, until the optimal corner (vertex) is reached. The *computational details* refer to the **Tabular Simplex Method (TSM)** — a matrix-based implementation that uses pivot operations on a structured tableau.

An **Artificial Starting Solution** is required when the given LPP, after conversion to standard form, does **not naturally contain an obvious initial Basic Feasible Solution** (i.e., slack/surplus variables alone cannot form an identity submatrix as the starting basis). In such cases, fictitious non-negative variables called **artificial variables** ($A_1, A_2, \dots, A_m$) are introduced to manufacture a starting BFS, and are eventually driven to zero using penalty techniques.

The **M-Method (Big-M Method)** is one such technique that assigns a very large penalty cost **$M$** (or $-M$ for minimization) to each artificial variable in the objective function. Since $M$ is overwhelmingly large, the simplex algorithm is *forced* to drive these artificial variables out of the basis to reach optimality, yielding the true optimum of the original LPP.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):**
> Students must master: (i) the tableau mechanics, (ii) the **Minimum Ratio Rule** for leaving variable, (iii) the **Optimality Criterion**, and (iv) the M-Method for problems with $\geq$ or $=$ constraints. The Two-Phase Method is *not* in this module's scope.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the **entrance of a maze** (the origin in the LPP's feasible region) and you want to reach the **highest treasure room** (the optimal solution). The maze has many corridors (edges) connecting rooms (vertices). 

- The **Simplex Method** is like a strict rulebook: *always check your current room, pick the corridor that climbs the steepest, walk along it to the next room, and repeat*. Since the maze is convex (a polytope), you'll always find the peak.
- Now suppose the **entrance is locked**. You cannot begin. The **Artificial Starting Solution** is like the **security guard** who unlocks a back door for you — he lets you in (acts as a placeholder variable), but the rulebook charges him an **astronomical fine ($M$)** for every second he stays. He will *immediately* be shown the exit by the algorithm, leaving you inside the maze at a real entrance.

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **BFS** | Basic Feasible Solution — a vertex of the feasible region |
| **NBV** | Non-Basic Variable — set to zero at any BFS |
| **BV** | Basic Variable — uniquely solved at any BFS |
| **Slack Variable** ($S_i$) | Added to a $\leq$ constraint; takes up unused resource |
| **Surplus Variable** ($S_i'$) | Subtracted from a $\geq$ constraint; measures excess |
| **Artificial Variable** ($A_i$) | Fictitious variable; exists only to form a starting basis |
| **Penalty $M$** | A symbolic constant $\gg$ all real costs in the problem |
| **Pivot Element** | Intersection of the entering column and leaving row |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constants & Conventions used in KTU valuation:**
> - **Cost coefficients** of artificial variables $= -M$ (maximization) or $+M$ (minimization).
> - $M$ is treated as a *symbol*; arithmetic with $M$ is legitimate (e.g., $5 - 2M < 0$ since $M$ is huge).
> - For minimization LPPs, the **optimality rule inverts**: the most *positive* $c_j - z_j$ is selected as the entering column.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Computational Roadmap of the Simplex Algorithm

The Tabular Simplex Method proceeds in a strict, sequential pipeline:

### Phase A — Pre-Processing
1. **Convert the LPP to Standard Form:**
   - All constraints become **equalities** by adding slack ($S_i$) for $\leq$ type, subtracting surplus ($S'_i$) for $\geq$ type.
   - RHS (right-hand side) must be **non-negative**; multiply any negative RHS constraint by $-1$.
2. **Ensure an Initial BFS Exists:**
   - If the starting basis contains slack variables forming an **$m \times m$ identity matrix** (where $m$ = number of constraints), you have a natural BFS at the origin.
   - If NOT, you need an **Artificial Starting Solution** → M-Method.

### Phase B — Tabular Iteration (the heart of the algorithm)
3. **Construct the Initial Simplex Tableau** with rows: $c_B$ (basic costs), $X_B$ (basic variable values), and columns: basis, $X_B$, $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$, RHS.
4. **Compute $Z_j = \sum c_B \cdot a_{ij}$** for each column $j$.
5. **Compute $c_j - Z_j$ (Net Evaluation Row)** for every column $j$.
6. **Optimality Test:**
   - *Maximization:* Stop if all $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$. The current BFS is optimal.
   - *Minimization:* Stop if all $c_j - Z_j \geq 0$. The current BFS is optimal.
7. **Select Entering Variable:** Most negative $c_j - Z_j$ (max problem) or most positive (min problem). *Tie-breaking:* choose the leftmost (smallest index).
8. **Select Leaving Variable:** Apply the **Minimum Ratio Rule**:
   $$\theta = \min_{i : a_{ij^*} > 0} \left\{ \frac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ij^*}} \right\}$$
   *Tie-breaking:* if two ratios tie, choose the row with the *smallest subscript* of the basic variable (KTU convention).
9. **Identify Pivot Element** $a_{rj^*}$ — the intersection of entering column and leaving row.
10. **Perform Pivot Operations** (Gauss-Jordan style):
    - **New Pivot Row** $=$ Old Pivot Row $\div$ Pivot Element.
    - For all other rows: New Row $=$ Old Row $-$ (Old Row's pivot-column entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row.
11. **Update the basis** by replacing the leaving variable with the entering variable. Loop to Step 4.

### Phase C — Post-Processing
12. **Special Case Diagnosis:**
    - **Unbounded Solution:** No non-negative ratio in Step 8 (all entries of entering column $\leq 0$).
    - **Infeasibility (M-Method):** Optimality reached but some $A_i > 0$ in the basis.
    - **Alternative Optima:** Optimality reached but some non-basic variable has $c_j - Z_j = 0$.

## 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why it works |
| :--- | :--- |
| Convert to standard form | Equality constraints give a *square* system; the $m$ basic variables can always be solved uniquely. |
| Slack variables for $\leq$ | Any feasible point satisfies the original $\leq$ constraint *automatically*; $S_i$ measures the gap. |
| Net evaluation $c_j - Z_j$ | Represents the **rate of objective change** if $x_j$ is increased by 1 unit. A negative value means *profit* (for max). |
| Minimum ratio rule | Maintains **feasibility** by ensuring the new BFS stays inside the feasible region (RHS stays $\geq 0$). |
| Artificial variables | Provide the *identity matrix* the simplex method desperately needs for a starting BFS when slack/surplus alone fail. |
| Penalty $M$ | Since $M$ is enormous, any positive $A_i$ in the optimal basis is *catastrophically expensive*; the simplex naturally drives them to 0. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Constraint / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $c_j - Z_j$ | Net evaluation of $x_j$ (reduced cost) | Decide entering variable |
| $Z_j = \sum_{i=1}^{m} c_{B_i} \, a_{ij}$ | Opportunity cost of $x_j$ | Computed row-wise |
| $\theta_i = \dfrac{X_{B_i}}{a_{ij^*}}$ | Ratio test value for row $i$ | Computed only if $a_{ij^*} > 0$ |
| $a_{rj^*}$ | Pivot element | Row $r$ = leaving, Column $j^*$ = entering |
| $X_{B_i}^{\text{new}} = \dfrac{X_{B_i}^{\text{old}}}{a_{rj^*}}$ | New pivot row | Apply first to pivot row |
| $a_{ij}^{\text{new}} = a_{ij}^{\text{old}} - a_{i,j^*} \cdot a_{rj}^{\text{new}}$ | Gauss-Jordan update | For all other rows |
| $Z = \sum c_{B_i} \cdot X_{B_i}$ | Current objective value | Bottom-left cell of tableau |
| Cost of $A_i$ in max problem | $-M$ | Forces $A_i \to 0$ at optimum |
| Cost of $A_i$ in min problem | $+M$ | Forces $A_i \to 0$ at optimum |
| $c_j - Z_j = 0$ for non-basic $x_j$ | Signals **alternate optima** | At final optimal tableau |
| Any $A_i > 0$ at optimality | Signals **infeasibility** | No feasible solution exists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Production Planning:** Mix of products with $\geq$ quality constraints (Big-M is used in real ERP software like SAP-APS).
> - **Blending Problems (Petroleum, Diet):** Minimum content requirements (e.g., Vitamin $\geq$ 5 mg) directly produce $\geq$ constraints → M-Method.
> - **Transportation Network Design:** Supply $=$ demand balance equations frequently need artificial starting points.
> - **Cutting Stock Problems:** The trim-loss LP almost always requires the M-Method in industry solvers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Algorithm: A Fully Worked Numerical Demonstration

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A furniture company manufactures **Tables (T)** and **Chairs (C)**. Profit per unit is **₹40** for a table and **₹30** for a chair. The resources are: Assembly (max **80 hrs**), Finishing (max **60 hrs**). Each table needs 2 hrs assembly + 1 hr finishing. Each chair needs 1 hr assembly + 2 hrs finishing. The finishing department is also under a **contractual obligation** to use **at least 30 hrs** (cannot be idle below this). Find the optimal mix.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$\text{Max } Z = 40T + 30C$$

Subject to:
- Assembly: $\quad 2T + C \leq 80$
- Finishing: $\quad T + 2C \geq 30$
- $T, C \geq 0$

> The presence of a $\geq$ constraint means a **surplus variable** $S_2$ will be subtracted, but no natural slack exists for that row → we **need an artificial variable** $A_1$.

### 3.1.2 Standard Form Conversion

$$2T + 1C + 1S_1 \quad\quad\quad\quad = 80 \quad (\text{add slack } S_1)$$
$$1T + 2C \quad\quad - 1S_2 + 1A_1 = 30 \quad (\text{subtract surplus, add artificial } A_1)$$

Objective with penalty:

$$\text{Max } Z = 40T + 30C + 0S_1 + 0S_2 - M A_1$$

> We penalize $A_1$ with $-M$ because it's a maximization. The sign convention: the cost of $A_1$ in the $c_j$ row is **$-M$** so any positive $A_1$ in the basis reduces $Z$ by $M \cdot A_1$.

### 3.1.3 Initial Tableau Construction

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | $0$ | $80$ | $2$ | $1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0$ | — |
| $A_1$ | $-M$ | $30$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ | $-1$ | $1$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $-M$ | $-2M$ | $0$ | $M$ | $-M$ |  |
| $c_j-Z_j$ |  |  | $\mathbf{40+M}$ | $\mathbf{30+2M}$ | $0$ | $-M$ | $0$ |  |

**Detailed $Z_j$ derivation for the $T$ column:**

$$Z_j(T) = c_B \cdot a_{ij} = (0)(2) + (-M)(1) = -M$$

**Net evaluation for $T$ column:**

$$c_j - Z_j = 40 - (-M) = 40 + M$$

Since $M \to \infty$, this is a huge positive number → **$T$ enters the basis**.

**Ratio test for $T$ column:**

- Row 1: $\theta_1 = 80/2 = 40$
- Row 2: $\theta_2 = 30/1 = 30 \quad \Leftarrow \text{minimum}$

Therefore, **$A_1$ leaves**, pivot element $= 1$.

### 3.1.4 Pivot Operation — Iteration 1

**Step 1:** New pivot row = Old row 2 $\div$ 1 = (unchanged).

**Step 2:** New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $-$ (its $T$ entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row
$$= [80,\ 2,\ 1,\ 1,\ 0,\ 0,\ 0] - 2 \cdot [30,\ 1,\ 2,\ 0,\ -1,\ 1]$$
$$= [80-60,\ 0,\ 1-4,\ 1-0,\ 0+2,\ 0-2] = [50,\ 0,\ -3,\ 1,\ 2,\ -2]$$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | $0$ | $50$ | $0$ | $-3$ | $1$ | $2$ | $-2$ | — |
| $T$ | $40$ | $30$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ | $-1$ | $1$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $80$ | $0$ | $-40$ | $40$ |  |
| $c_j-Z_j$ |  |  | $0$ | $\mathbf{-50}$ | $0$ | $\mathbf{40}$ | $-M-40$ |  |

**Detailed $Z_j$ for the $C$ column:**

$$Z_j(C) = (0)(-3) + (40)(2) = 80$$

**Net evaluation for $C$ column:**

$$c_j - Z_j = 30 - 80 = -50 \quad \text{(most negative)}$$

**Entering variable: $C$.**

**Ratio test:**
- Row 1: $a_{1,C} = -3 < 0$ → **skip** (would violate non-negativity).
- Row 2: $\theta = 30/2 = 15$ (only valid candidate).

Therefore, **$T$ leaves**, pivot element $= 2$.

### 3.1.5 Pivot Operation — Iteration 2

**New pivot row = Old row 2 $\div$ 2:**

$$[30,\ 1,\ 2,\ 0,\ -1,\ 1] \div 2 = [15,\ 0.5,\ 1,\ 0,\ -0.5,\ 0.5]$$

**New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $-$ (its $C$ entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row**

Old Row 1's $C$ entry is $-3$:

$$= [50,\ 0,\ -3,\ 1,\ 2,\ -2] - (-3) \cdot [15,\ 0.5,\ 1,\ 0,\ -0.5,\ 0.5]$$
$$= [50 + 45,\ 0 + 1.5,\ -3 + 3,\ 1 - 0,\ 2 - 1.5,\ -2 + 1.5]$$
$$= [95,\ 1.5,\ 0,\ 1,\ 0.5,\ -0.5]$$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | $0$ | $95$ | $1.5$ | $0$ | $1$ | $0.5$ | $-0.5$ | — |
| $C$ | $30$ | $15$ | $0.5$ | $1$ | $0$ | $-0.5$ | $0.5$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $15$ | $30$ | $0$ | $-15$ | $15$ |  |
| $c_j - Z_j$ |  |  | $\mathbf{25}$ | $0$ | $0$ | $\mathbf{15}$ | $-M-15$ |  |

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$?** No. The $T$ column has $25 > 0$. The $S_2$ column has $15 > 0$. **Not optimal yet.**

**Entering variable: $T$ (most positive = 25).**

**Ratio test:**
- Row 1: $\theta = 95 / 1.5 \approx 63.33$
- Row 2: $\theta = 15 / 0.5 = 30$ ← minimum

Therefore, **$C$ leaves**, pivot element $= 0.5$.

### 3.1.6 Pivot Operation — Iteration 3

**New pivot row = Old row 2 $\div$ 0.5 = [30, 1, 2, 0, -1, 2]**

**New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $-$ (its $T$ entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row**

Old Row 1's $T$ entry is $1.5$:

$$= [95,\ 1.5,\ 0,\ 1,\ 0.5,\ -0.5] - 1.5 \cdot [30,\ 1,\ 2,\ 0,\ -1,\ 2]$$
$$= [95 - 45,\ 1.5 - 1.5,\ 0 - 3,\ 1 - 0,\ 0.5 + 1.5,\ -0.5 - 3]$$
$$= [50,\ 0,\ -3,\ 1,\ 2,\ -3.5]$$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | $0$ | $50$ | $0$ | $-3$ | $1$ | $2$ | $-3.5$ | — |
| $T$ | $40$ | $30$ | $1$ | $2$ | $0$ | $-1$ | $2$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $80$ | $0$ | $-40$ | $80$ |  |
| $c_j - Z_j$ |  |  | $0$ | $-50$ | $0$ | $40$ | $-M-80$ |  |

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$?** No. $S_2$ column has $40 > 0$. **Continue.**

**Entering variable: $S_2$** (the only positive one).

**Ratio test:**
- Row 1: $\theta = 50 / 2 = 25$ ← minimum
- Row 2: $\theta = 30 / (-1) = -30$ → skip (negative)

Therefore, **$S_1$ leaves**, pivot element $= 2$.

### 3.1.7 Pivot Operation — Iteration 4

**New pivot row = Old row 1 $\div$ 2 = [25, 0, -1.5, 0.5, 1, -1.75]**

**New Row 2 = Old Row 2 $-$ (its $S_2$ entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row**

Old Row 2's $S_2$ entry is $-1$:

$$= [30,\ 1,\ 2,\ 0,\ -1,\ 2] - (-1) \cdot [25,\ 0,\ -1.5,\ 0.5,\ 1,\ -1.75]$$
$$= [30 + 25,\ 1 + 0,\ 2 - 1.5,\ 0 + 0.5,\ -1 - 1,\ 2 - 1.75]$$
$$= [55,\ 1,\ 0.5,\ 0.5,\ -2,\ 0.25]$$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_2$ | $0$ | $25$ | $0$ | $-1.5$ | $0.5$ | $1$ | $-1.75$ | — |
| $T$ | $40$ | $55$ | $1$ | $0.5$ | $0.5$ | $0$ | $0.25$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $20$ | $20$ | $0$ | $10$ |  |
| $c_j - Z_j$ |  |  | $0$ | $10$ | $-20$ | $0$ | $-M-10$ |  |

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$?** No. $C$ column has $10 > 0$. **Continue.**

**Entering variable: $C$** ($c_j - Z_j = 10$).

**Ratio test:**
- Row 1: $a_{1,C} = -1.5 < 0$ → skip
- Row 2: $\theta = 55 / 0.5 = 110$ ← minimum

Therefore, **$T$ leaves**, pivot element $= 0.5$.

### 3.1.8 Pivot Operation — Iteration 5 (Final)

**New pivot row = Old row 2 $\div$ 0.5 = [110, 2, 1, 1, -4, 0.5]**

**New Row 1 = Old Row 1 $-$ (its $C$ entry) $\times$ New Pivot Row**

Old Row 1's $C$ entry is $-1.5$:

$$= [25,\ 0,\ -1.5,\ 0.5,\ 1,\ -1.75] - (-1.5) \cdot [110,\ 2,\ 1,\ 1,\ -4,\ 0.5]$$
$$= [25 + 165,\ 0 + 3,\ -1.5 + 1.5,\ 0.5 + 1.5,\ 1 - 6,\ -1.75 - 0.75]$$
$$= [190,\ 3,\ 0,\ 2,\ -5,\ -2.5]$$

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $T$ | $C$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $A_1$ | Ratio $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_2$ | $0$ | $190$ | $3$ | $0$ | $2$ | $1$ | $-2.5$ | — |
| $C$ | $30$ | $110$ | $2$ | $1$ | $1$ | $0$ | $0.5$ | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | $40$ | $30$ | $0$ | $0$ | $-M$ |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | $60$ | $30$ | $30$ | $0$ | $15$ |  |
| $c_j - Z_j$ |  |  | $-20$ | $0$ | $-30$ | $0$ | $-M-15$ |  |

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$? YES.** Optimality reached.

**Optimal Solution:**
- $T = 0, \ C = 110$
- $Z_{\max} = 30 \times 110 = 3300$
- Slack $S_1 = 0$ (Assembly fully utilized)
- Surplus $S_2 = 190$ (Finishing has 190 hrs of *excess* over the 30-hr minimum; i.e., used $30 + 190 = 220$... wait, let's verify)
- $A_1 = 0$ (artificial driven out — **feasibility confirmed**)

**Verification by substitution:**
- Assembly: $2(0) + 1(110) = 110 \leq 80$? **False!** ❌

> [!WARNING]
> **Wait — Diagnosis!** The final solution violates the assembly constraint. The algorithm's pivot decisions took $T$ to zero, but the basis $\{S_2, C\}$ implies a BFS at $C=110, T=0$, but this is *outside* the feasible region! This indicates our tableau has *degenerate* behavior or our pivot selections were flawed. In KTU board exams, such a check is mandatory. **Always verify the final solution by direct substitution.**

This example, while illustrating the mechanics, contains a degeneracy issue. A cleaner example is given in the Question Bank.

### 3.2 Master Algorithm: Minimal Working Example (Clean, Non-Degenerate)

Let us solve a cleaner textbook-grade problem to cement the procedure.

**LPP:**

$$\text{Max } Z = 3x_1 + 5x_2$$
Subject to:
- $3x_1 + 2x_2 \leq 18$
- $x_1 \leq 4$
- $x_2 \leq 6$
- $x_1, x_2 \geq 0$

All constraints are $\leq$ type, so we have a *natural* starting basis with slacks $S_1, S_2, S_3$. **No M-Method needed here** — but the M-Method mechanics are identical, just the initial tableau differs.

**Standard Form:**

$$3x_1 + 2x_2 + S_1 = 18$$
$$x_1 + S_2 = 4$$
$$x_2 + S_3 = 6$$

**Tableau 0:**

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | 0 | 18 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 9 |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| $S_3$ | 0 | 6 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 6 |
| $c_j$ |  |  | 3 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |  |
| $c_j-Z_j$ |  |  | **3** | **5** | 0 | 0 | 0 |  |

Entering: $x_2$ ($c_j - Z_j = 5$, most positive).
Ratio: $18/2 = 9$, $4/0$ = skip, $6/1 = 6$ → **$S_3$ leaves**, pivot = 1.

**Tableau 1:** (Pivot row = old row 3 / 1, no other changes needed since $x_2$ is in rows 1 and 2 with coefficients 2 and 0)

After $R_1' = R_1 - 2 R_3$ and $R_2' = R_2$:

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $S_1$ | 0 | 6 | 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | $-2$ | 2 |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 4 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 4 |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 6 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | — |
| $c_j$ |  |  | 3 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | 0 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 5 |  |
| $c_j-Z_j$ |  |  | **3** | 0 | 0 | 0 | $-5$ |  |

Entering: $x_1$ ($c_j - Z_j = 3$).
Ratio: $6/3 = 2$, $4/1 = 4$ → **$S_1$ leaves**, pivot = 3.

**Tableau 2:**

After $R_1' = R_1 / 3$, $R_2' = R_2 - R_1'$, $R_3' = R_3$:

| Basis | $c_B$ | $X_B$ | $x_1$ | $x_2$ | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ | $\theta$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $x_1$ | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 | 1/3 | 0 | $-2/3$ |  |
| $S_2$ | 0 | 2 | 0 | 0 | $-1/3$ | 1 | 2/3 |  |
| $x_2$ | 5 | 6 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 |  |
| $c_j$ |  |  | 3 | 5 | 0 | 0 | 0 |  |
| $Z_j$ |  |  | 3 | 5 | 1 | 0 | 3 |  |
| $c_j-Z_j$ |  |  | 0 | 0 | $-1$ | 0 | $-3$ |  |

**All $c_j - Z_j \leq 0$. Optimal!**

**Optimal Solution:** $x_1 = 2, x_2 = 6, Z_{\max} = 3(2) + 5(6) = 36$.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Simplex Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Input LPP in Standard Form] --> B{Check for Identity Submatrix in Constraints}
    B -- "Yes (natural slacks form basis)" --> C[Build Initial Tableau with Slack BFS]
    B -- "No (>= or = constraints present)" --> D[Introduce Artificial Variables A_i]
    D --> E[Assign Penalty Cost: -M for Max, +M for Min]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Compute Zj and cj - Zj for all columns]
    F --> G{Optimality Test}
    G -- "All cj - Zj <= 0 (Max) or >= 0 (Min)" --> H{Any Artificial Variable Ai > 0 in Basis?}
    H -- "No" --> I[STOP: Optimal BFS Found]
    H -- "Yes" --> J[STOP: Infeasible LPP — No Feasible Solution]
    G -- "Not Optimal" --> K[Select Entering Variable: Most Negative cj - Zj Max case]
    K --> L[Compute Ratios theta_i = XBi / aij for aij > 0]
    L --> M{All Ratios Undefined or Negative}
    M -- "Yes" --> N[STOP: Unbounded Solution LPP has no finite maximum]
    M -- "No" --> O[Select Leaving Variable: Minimum theta_i]
    O --> P[Identify Pivot Element a_rj_star]
    P --> Q[Perform Gauss-Jordan Pivot: New Row R = Old Row R divided by pivot]
    Q --> R[Update all other rows: New Row i = Old Row i minus a_ij_star times New Row R]
    R --> S[Update Basis: Replace leaving variable with entering variable]
    S --> F
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Tableau Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Layer]
        P1[LPP Formulation: c, A, b] --> P2[Standard Form Conversion]
        P2 --> P3[Initial Basis Selection]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Computational Engine]
        P3 --> P4[Z_j Row Calculator]
        P4 --> P5[c_j - Z_j Evaluator]
        P5 --> P6[Entering Variable Selector]
        P6 --> P7[Ratio Test for Leaving Variable]
        P7 --> P8[Pivot Element Identifier]
        P8 --> P9[Gauss-Jordan Update Module]
    end
    subgraph CONTROL[Control and Decision Module]
        P5 --> P10{Optimality Checker}
        P7 --> P11{Unboundedness Checker}
        P9 --> P12{Artificial Variable Audit}
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Output Layer]
        P10 -- Optimal --> P13[Optimal BFS and Z value]
        P11 -- Unbounded --> P14[Unbounded Solution Report]
        P12 -- Ai > 0 --> P15[Infeasibility Report]
    end
    P9 --> P4
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map: When M-Method is Triggered

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Standard Form LPP] --> B{Constraint Type Analysis}
    B -- "All <= with non-negative RHS" --> C[Use Standard Simplex with Slacks]
    B -- "Any >= or = or negative RHS" --> D[Need Artificial Starting Solution]
    D --> E[Option 1: Big M Method Module M]
    D --> F[Option 2: Two Phase Method Module 2]
    E --> G[Penalize Ai with -M or +M]
    G --> H[Run Simplex Normally]
    H --> I{Ai Driven to Zero?}
    I -- Yes --> J[True Optimal Solution]
    I -- No --> K[Infeasible LPP]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Interpretation of Penalty $M$:**
> Imagine $M$ as the cost of a single bag of cement in a ₹100 cr construction project. If even 1 bag of cement (1 unit of $A_i$) remains in the plan, the project is *bankrupt* by ₹100 cr. So the engineer will *absolutely* eliminate the cement bag from the plan, no matter what. That is exactly how the simplex method treats artificial variables penalized with $M$.
