# Decision Making Under Risk — Section 5: KTU Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set B]**
**Distinguish between decision making under risk and decision making under uncertainty. Give one real-world example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Under Risk | Under Uncertainty |
|---|---|---|
| State probabilities | Known and quantified | Not known / not assignable |
| Information | Probabilistic data available | Only structural data available |
| Criterion used | EMV, EOL, EVPI | MaxiMin, MaxiMax, Hurwicz, Laplace, Savage |
| Example | Insurance premium for known accident rate | Launching a brand-new product in an untested market |

**(1 mark for definition of risk, 1 mark for uncertainty definition, 1 mark for correct real-world examples.)**

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set A]**
**State the formula for Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI) and explain its significance in two lines.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

$$\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \text{EVwoPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \left[ \max_{i} V_{ij} \right] \cdot P(S_j) - \max_{i} \left[ \sum_{j=1}^{n} V_{ij} \cdot P(S_j) \right]$$

**Significance:**
1. EVPI represents the **maximum amount a decision-maker should be willing to pay** for obtaining perfect (100% accurate) information about which state of nature will occur.
2. It also equals $\min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)$, providing a dual interpretation as the minimum expected regret the DM must bear.

**(1 mark for correct formula, 1 mark for ceiling-of-information interpretation, 1 mark for EOL equivalence.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each. Internal choice means students answer EITHER Option A OR Option B in full.

---

### **Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4, Question 2(a)]**

A construction company in Kochi must decide whether to build a small, medium, or large commercial complex. Demand projections give the following profit table (₹ in crores):

| Complex \ Demand | High ($P=0.5$) | Medium ($P=0.3$) | Low ($P=0.2$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small | 50 | 30 | 20 |
| Medium | 80 | 50 | 0 |
| Large | 120 | 40 | -30 |

**(a)** Determine the optimal decision using the **EMV criterion** and compute the corresponding expected profit.

**(b)** Construct the **regret table**, determine the optimal decision using the **EOL criterion**, and compute the **EVPI**. Verify your EVPI by the alternative EOL-based method.

#### **Model Solution (Question A):**

**Part (a) — EMV Analysis (7 marks):**

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Small}) = (50)(0.5) + (30)(0.3) + (20)(0.2) = 25 + 9 + 4 = 38 \text{ crores}$$

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Medium}) = (80)(0.5) + (50)(0.3) + (0)(0.2) = 40 + 15 + 0 = 55 \text{ crores}$$

$$\text{EMV}(\text{Large}) = (120)(0.5) + (40)(0.3) + (-30)(0.2) = 60 + 12 - 6 = 66 \text{ crores}$$

Comparing: $\max\{38, 55, 66\} = 66$.

$$\boxed{\text{Optimal Decision: Build LARGE complex; Expected Profit} = \text{₹} 66 \text{ crores}}$$

**(7 marks: 2 for setup, 3 for three EMV calculations, 2 for optimal selection with boxed answer.)**

**Part (b) — Regret Table, EOL, and EVPI (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Build the Regret Table.**

Column-wise maxima of the original payoff table:
- $S_1$ (High): $\max = 120$ (Large)
- $S_2$ (Medium): $\max = 50$ (Medium)
- $S_3$ (Low): $\max = 20$ (Small)

Compute regrets $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij}$:

| Complex \ Demand | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Small | $120-50=70$ | $50-30=20$ | $20-20=0$ |
| Medium | $120-80=40$ | $50-50=0$ | $20-0=20$ |
| Large | $120-120=0$ | $50-40=10$ | $20-(-30)=50$ |

**Step 2 — Compute EOLs.**

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Small}) = (70)(0.5) + (20)(0.3) + (0)(0.2) = 35 + 6 + 0 = 41$$

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Medium}) = (40)(0.5) + (0)(0.3) + (20)(0.2) = 20 + 0 + 4 = 24$$

$$\text{EOL}(\text{Large}) = (0)(0.5) + (10)(0.3) + (50)(0.2) = 0 + 3 + 10 = 13$$

**Step 3 — Optimal by EOL.**

$$\min\{41, 24, 13\} = 13 \implies A^* = \text{Large complex}$$

**Step 4 — EVPI via the EOL identity:**

$$\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i) = 13 \text{ crores}$$

**Step 5 — Verification via EVwPI − EVwoPI:**

$$\text{EVwPI} = (120)(0.5) + (50)(0.3) + (20)(0.2) = 60 + 15 + 4 = 79$$

$$\text{EVwoPI} = \max \text{EMV} = 66$$

$$\text{EVPI} = 79 - 66 = 13 \text{ crores}\ \checkmark$$

**Interpretation:** The construction company should not pay more than **₹13 crores** for any market research that guarantees 100% accurate demand forecasting.

**(7 marks: 2 for regret table, 2 for EOL calculations, 1 for optimal EOL, 1 for EVPI, 1 for verification.)**

---

### **Question B — Option 2 (Internal Choice) (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Module 4, Question 2(b)]**

A pharmaceutical firm is deciding between two R&D approaches, $A_1$ and $A_2$, for a new drug. The drug can be either a *success* (state $S_1$), a *moderate success* (state $S_2$), or a *failure* (state $S_3$). Prior probabilities are $P(S_1) = 0.3$, $P(S_2) = 0.5$, $P(S_3) = 0.2$. Payoffs (₹ in lakhs):

| Approach \ Outcome | $S_1$ | $S_2$ | $S_3$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| $A_1$ | 200 | 80 | -50 |
| $A_2$ | 150 | 100 | 20 |

**(a)** Compute the optimal decision by EMV. If the firm can conduct a pre-clinical trial $E$ with likelihoods $P(E \mid S_1) = 0.8$, $P(E \mid S_2) = 0.5$, $P(E \mid S_3) = 0.1$, use **Bayes' theorem** to compute the posterior probabilities of the states given a positive trial result $E$.

**(b)** Using these posterior probabilities, recompute the optimal EMV-based decision. Comment on whether the trial is worthwhile if it costs **₹25 lakhs**.

#### **Model Solution (Question B):**

**Part (a) — Prior EMV and Posterior Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Prior EMV (without trial):**

$$\text{EMV}(A_1) = (200)(0.3) + (80)(0.5) + (-50)(0.2) = 60 + 40 - 10 = 90$$

$$\text{EMV}(A_2) = (150)(0.3) + (100)(0.5) + (20)(0.2) = 45 + 50 + 4 = 99$$

$$\boxed{A^* = A_2 \text{ (Prior EMV} = 99 \text{ lakhs)}}$$

**Step 2 — Marginal probability of positive trial $P(E)$ via Total Probability:**

$$P(E) = P(E \vert S_1) P(S_1) + P(E \vert S_2) P(S_2) + P(E \vert S_3) P(S_3)$$

$$P(E) = (0.8)(0.3) + (0.5)(0.5) + (0.1)(0.2)$$

$$P(E) = 0.24 + 0.25 + 0.02 = 0.51$$

**Step 3 — Apply Bayes' Theorem for each state:**

$$P(S_1 \vert E) = \frac{(0.8)(0.3)}{0.51} = \frac{0.24}{0.51} \approx 0.4706$$

$$P(S_2 \vert E) = \frac{(0.5)(0.5)}{0.51} = \frac{0.25}{0.51} \approx 0.4902$$

$$P(S_3 \vert E) = \frac{(0.1)(0.2)}{0.51} = \frac{0.02}{0.51} \approx 0.0392$$

**Verification of Posterior Sum:**

$$0.4706 + 0.4902 + 0.0392 = 1.0000\ \checkmark$$

**(7 marks: 2 for prior EMV, 2 for marginal $P(E)$, 3 for posterior computation.)**

**Part (b) — Posterior EMV and Trial Valuation (7 marks):**

**Step 4 — Recompute EMVs using posterior probabilities:**

$$\text{EMV}(A_1 \vert E) = (200)(0.4706) + (80)(0.4902) + (-50)(0.0392)$$

$$= 94.12 + 39.216 - 1.96 \approx 131.376 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\text{EMV}(A_2 \vert E) = (150)(0.4706) + (100)(0.4902) + (20)(0.0392)$$

$$= 70.59 + 49.02 + 0.784 \approx 120.394 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Step 5 — Optimal Posterior Decision:**

$$\boxed{A^* \vert E = A_1 \text{ with expected payoff} \approx 131.38 \text{ lakhs}}$$

**Step 6 — Value of Sample Information (EVSI) and Trial Decision:**

**Note:** A complete EVSI calculation requires evaluating the trial for *both* a positive outcome $E$ *and* a negative outcome $\bar{E}$. For simplicity, assuming the firm follows a decision rule: "If $E$ positive, choose $A_1$; if $E$ negative, choose $A_2$," and using $P(\bar{E}) = 1 - 0.51 = 0.49$:

Posterior probabilities given $\bar{E}$:

$$P(S_1 \vert \bar{E}) = \frac{(0.2)(0.3)}{0.49} = 0.1224$$

$$P(S_2 \vert \bar{E}) = \frac{(0.5)(0.5)}{0.49} = 0.5102$$

$$P(S_3 \vert \bar{E}) = \frac{(0.9)(0.2)}{0.49} = 0.3673$$

Posterior EMV given $\bar{E}$:

$$\text{EMV}(A_1 \vert \bar{E}) = (200)(0.1224) + (80)(0.5102) + (-50)(0.3673) \approx 24.49 + 40.82 - 18.37 = 46.94$$

$$\text{EMV}(A_2 \vert \bar{E}) = (150)(0.1224) + (100)(0.5102) + (20)(0.3673) \approx 18.36 + 51.02 + 7.35 = 76.73$$

So if $\bar{E}$ occurs, the firm chooses $A_2$ with EMV $\approx 76.73$ lakhs.

**Overall expected payoff with trial (before deducting cost):**

$$\text{EVwSI} = (131.38)(0.51) + (76.73)(0.49) = 67.00 + 37.60 \approx 104.60 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\text{EVSI} = \text{EVwSI} - \text{EVwoPI} = 104.60 - 99 = 5.60 \text{ lakhs}$$

**Decision on Trial:**

$$\text{Net Gain} = \text{EVSI} - \text{Trial Cost} = 5.60 - 25 = -19.40 \text{ lakhs}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Since Net Gain} < 0, \text{ the firm should NOT conduct the trial.}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify posterior sum = 1** after Bayes' computation — KTU deducts 1 mark explicitly for this.
> 2. **Mixing up the prior and posterior in EMV** — students often use the prior $P(S_j)$ with the wrong alternative's payoffs, swapping the indices. Always check that $V_{ij}$ aligns with $P(S_j)$.
> 3. **Trial cost is deducted from EVSI, NOT from EVwoPI** — confusion here loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Decision tree should show BOTH branches of the trial** ($E$ and $\bar{E}$). A common mistake is drawing only the positive-test branch and ignoring the negative-test branch.
> 5. **Rounding too early** in Bayes' computation introduces large errors. Carry at least 4 decimal places until the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4: Decision Making Under Risk**

- [x] **Risk ≠ Uncertainty.** Risk = probabilities known; Uncertainty = probabilities unknown.
- [x] **Four elements** of every decision problem: Alternatives, States, Payoffs, Probabilities.
- [x] **EMV Formula:** $\text{EMV}(A_i) = \sum_{j} V_{ij} P(S_j)$. Maximize for profit; minimize for cost.
- [x] **Regret Formula (profit):** $R_{ij} = \max_k V_{kj} - V_{ij}$. For cost, reverse the sign.
- [x] **EOL Formula:** $\text{EOL}(A_i) = \sum_j R_{ij} P(S_j)$. Minimize.
- [x] **EVPI Formula:** $\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \text{EVwoPI}$.
- [x] **Identity to memorize:** $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(A_i)$. This is a 2-mark favorite.
- [x] **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(S_j \vert E) = \dfrac{P(E \vert S_j) P(S_j)}{\sum_k P(E \vert S_k) P(S_k)}$.
- [x] **Posterior probabilities replace priors** in subsequent EMV/EOL computations.
- [x] **Decision tree conventions:** $\square$ = decision, $\bigcirc$ = chance, $\triangle$ = payoff.
- [x] **Roll-back (folding-back):** Always start from the *rightmost* chance node and work leftward.
- [x] **EVPI interpretation:** Upper ceiling price for perfect information; value of *foresight*, not of the *decision*.
- [x] **EVSI ≤ EVPI** always — sample information is never more valuable than perfect information.
- [x] **Equivalence theorem:** EMV-optimal and EOL-optimal decisions are *always the same*. Use EOL as a *cross-check*.
- [x] **Sensitivity Analysis:** KTU often asks "what if the probability of $S_j$ changes by $\pm 0.1$?" — be ready to recompute EMVs and identify the **crossover point** where the optimal alternative switches.
- [x] **Utility vs. Money:** For stakes > ₹10 crores or highly risk-averse DMs, use **expected utility** instead of EMV. The utility function $U(M)$ is concave for risk-averse, linear for risk-neutral, and convex for risk-seeking.

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule for KTU Valuation:** Always show *all* intermediate calculations, label every table clearly (Alternative $\times$ State), and box your final answer. Half the marks are awarded for *process*, not just the final number.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decision Tree-Based Expected Value Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Decision Tree-Based Expected Value Criterion # Decision Tree-Based Expected Value Criterion ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Decision Tree-Based Expected Value Criterion** is a quantitative decision-making technique under conditions of **risk** (where probabilities of outcomes are known or estimable). It involves representing all possible decision alternatives, chance events, and their payoffs in a **tree-like diagram**, then computing the **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** at each chance node by summing the products of payoffs and their associated probabilities. The alternative yielding the maximum (or minimum for cost problems) EMV is selected as the optimal decision. In KTU 2024 Scheme Operations Research terminology, this technique belongs to **Decision Theory** under **Decision-Making Under Risk**, and is a direct application of the **Bayesian Decision Framework**. **Standard Metrics Used:** - **EMV (Expected Monetary Value)** — measured in monetary units (₹, $, etc.) - **Probability** — dimensionless quantity, $0 \le p \le 1$ - **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** — monetary units - **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** — monetary units ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Weather and Wedding Dilemma"** > > Imagine you are a wedding planner deciding whether to host an outdoor ceremony in Kerala during **June (monsoon season)**. You have three alternatives: (1) Book an outdoor venue, (2) Book an indoor venue, (3) Postpone and decide later. The "states of nature" are *Heavy Rain*, *Light Drizzle*, and *Sunny*. You can estimate probabilities from IMD historical data, and you know the **profit/loss** for each combination of decision and weather. > > A **decision tree** is simply a visual map of this dilemma. You start at a **decision point (square)**, branch out to your alternatives, and at the end of each alternative, attach a **chance node (circle)** that branches further into the possible weather outcomes. The "expected value criterion" is the rule that says: *calculate the average payoff you would get if you could repeat this decision infinitely many times under identical conditions, and pick the alternative with the best long-run average.* ## 1.3 Core Symbols and Notations | Symbol | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | | $D$ | Decision alternative | | $\theta_j$ | $j$-th state of nature | | $P(\theta_j)$ | Probability of state $\theta_j$ | | $V_{ij}$ | Payoff from decision $D_i$ under state $\theta_j$ | | $E_i$ | Expected value of decision $D_i$ | | $d^*$ | Optimal decision | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis ## 2.1 Conceptual Building Blocks A decision tree is built from **three distinct graphical elements**: 1. **Decision Node (Square □):** Represents a point where the decision-maker has control. Branches emerging are **decision alternatives**. 2. **Chance Node (Circle ○):** Represents a point of uncertainty. Branches emerging are **states of nature**, each annotated with a probability. 3. **Endpoint (Triangle △ or tip):** Represents the terminal **payoff** (profit, cost, or utility) for a complete path. > [!NOTE] > **The "Roll-Back" Procedure:** After all EMVs are computed at chance nodes, the analyst moves from right to left. At each decision node, the branch with the most favorable EMV is **selected** (and the others pruned/crossed out). The selected EMV replaces the decision node's value, and the process continues until the root decision node is reached. ## 2.2 Why Use Decision Trees? - They **expose the structure** of sequential decisions and uncertainties visually. - They naturally incorporate **prior and posterior probabilities** (via Bayes' theorem) in multi-stage problems. - They support the calculation of **EVPI**, **EOL**, and other risk-adjusted metrics. - They scale well to **multi-stage (sequential) decision problems**. ## 2.3 The Expected Value Formula For a single-stage decision problem, given decision $D_i$ and mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive states $\{\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n\}$: $$E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \cdot V_{ij}$$ The optimal decision $d^*$ under the **maximization** objective is: $$d^* = \arg\max_{i} \, E_i$$ For a **minimization** (cost) problem: $$d^* = \arg\min_{i} \, E_i$$ ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Meaning | Units | | :-- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 1 | $E_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \cdot V_{ij}$ | Expected value of decision $D_i$ | Currency | | 2 | $d^* = \arg\max E_i$ | Maximization rule | — | | 3 | $R_j = \max_i V_{ij}$ | Regret of state $\theta_j$ | Currency | | 4 | $\text{EOL}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \cdot R_{ij}$ | Expected opportunity loss of $D_i$ | Currency | | 5 | $d^* = \arg\min \text{EOL}_i$ | Minimax regret rule | — | | 6 | $P(\theta_j \mid E) = \dfrac{P(E \mid \theta_j) P(\theta_j)}{\sum_k P(E \mid \theta_k) P(\theta_k)}$ | Bayes' posterior probability | Dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EOL}^* = \min_i \text{EOL}_i$ | Value of perfect information | Currency | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Tip:** Always state whether you are using the **EMV criterion (max)** or the **EOL criterion (min)**. Both yield the same optimal decision because $\min \text{EOL} \iff \max \text{EMV}$ for the same payoff matrix. Examiners award marks for stating the criterion explicitly. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Construction Engineering:** Choosing between foundation types given uncertain soil conditions. - **Manufacturing:** Make-or-buy decisions under uncertain demand. - **Energy Systems:** Investment decisions for solar/wind under uncertain weather and tariff. - **Inventory & Supply Chain:** Lot-sizing under stochastic demand. - **Software Project Management:** Build, buy, or delay decisions under uncertain user adoption. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example — The Complete Decision-Tree Calculation ### Problem Statement A KTU-affiliated engineering consultancy in Trivandrum must decide whether to: - **$D_1$:** Submit a small tender (cost ₹2,00,000). - **$D_2$:** Submit a large tender (cost ₹5,00,000). The market can be in one of three states: - **$\theta_1$:** Favourable ($P = 0.4$) — Small yields net profit ₹3,00,000; Large yields ₹8,00,000. - **$\theta_2$:** Moderate ($P = 0.4$) — Small yields ₹1,50,000; Large yields ₹3,00,000. - **$\theta_3$:** Unfavourable ($P = 0.2$) — Small yields ₹–50,000; Large yields ₹–4,00,000. Determine the optimal decision using the EMV criterion. ### Step 1 — Construct the Payoff Matrix (in ₹) | Decision | $\theta_1$ ($p=0.4$) | $\theta_2$ ($p=0.4$) | $\theta_3$ ($p=0.2$) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $D_1$: Small tender | $3,00,000$ | $1,50,000$ | $-50,000$ | | $D_2$: Large tender | $8,00,000$ | $3,00,000$ | $-4,00,000$ | > [!NOTE] > A **negative payoff** represents a **net loss** (i.e., the cost is not fully recovered). ### Step 2 — Compute EMV for Decision $D_1$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1) &= P(\theta_1) V_{11} + P(\theta_2) V_{12} + P(\theta_3) V_{13} \\[4pt] &= (0.4)(3,00,000) + (0.4)(1,50,000) + (0.2)(-50,000) \\[4pt] &= 1,20,000 + 60,000 - 10,000 \\[4pt] &= 1,70,000 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Compute EMV for Decision $D_2$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_2) &= P(\theta_1) V_{21} + P(\theta_2) V_{22} + P(\theta_3) V_{23} \\[4pt] &= (0.4)(8,00,000) + (0.4)(3,00,000) + (0.2)(-4,00,000) \\[4pt] &= 3,20,000 + 1,20,000 - 80,000 \\[4pt] &= 3,60,000 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Apply the Maximization Rule $$ d^* = \arg\max\{\text{EMV}(D_1),\, \text{EMV}(D_2)\} = \arg\max\{1,70,000,\; 3,60,000\} = D_2 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conclusion:** The optimal decision is to submit the **Large Tender ($D_2$)**, with an expected net profit of **₹3,60,000**. --- ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Multi-Stage Tree with Bayes' Update Consider the same problem, but the consultancy can conduct a **market survey** (cost ₹30,000) before deciding. The survey returns either *Favourable* (F) or *Unfavourable* (U), with these conditional probabilities: | Survey | $P(\cdot \mid \theta_1)$ | $P(\cdot \mid \theta_2)$ | $P(\cdot \mid \theta_3)$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Favourable (F) | $0.7$ | $0.3$ | $0.1$ | | Unfavourable (U) | $0.3$ | $0.7$ | $0.9$ | ### Step 5 — Compute Marginal Survey Probabilities $$ \begin{aligned} P(F) &= \sum_{j} P(F \mid \theta_j) P(\theta_j) \\ &= (0.7)(0.4) + (0.3)(0.4) + (0.1)(0.2) \\ &= 0.28 + 0.12 + 0.02 = 0.42 \\[6pt] P(U) &= 1 - P(F) = 0.58 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Apply Bayes' Theorem (Posterior Probabilities) $$ \begin{aligned} P(\theta_1 \mid F) &= \frac{P(F \mid \theta_1) P(\theta_1)}{P(F)} = \frac{0.7 \times 0.4}{0.42} = \frac{0.28}{0.42} = 0.6667 \\[4pt] P(\theta_2 \mid F) &= \frac{0.3 \times 0.4}{0.42} = \frac{0.12}{0.42} = 0.2857 \\[4pt] P(\theta_3 \mid F) &= \frac{0.1 \times 0.2}{0.42} = \frac{0.02}{0.42} = 0.0476 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} P(\theta_1 \mid U) &= \frac{0.3 \times 0.4}{0.58} = \frac{0.12}{0.58} = 0.2069 \\[4pt] P(\theta_2 \mid U) &= \frac{0.7 \times 0.4}{0.58} = \frac{0.28}{0.58} = 0.4828 \\[4pt] P(\theta_3 \mid U) &= \frac{0.9 \times 0.2}{0.58} = \frac{0.18}{0.58} = 0.3103 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Compute Posterior EMVs **If Survey = Favourable:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1 \mid F) &= (0.6667)(3,00,000) + (0.2857)(1,50,000) + (0.0476)(-50,000) \\ &= 2,00,010 + 42,855 - 2,380 = 2,40,485 \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_2 \mid F) &= (0.6667)(8,00,000) + (0.2857)(3,00,000) + (0.0476)(-4,00,000) \\ &= 5,33,360 + 85,710 - 19,040 = 6,00,030 \end{aligned} $$ **If Survey = Unfavourable:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1 \mid U) &= (0.2069)(3,00,000) + (0.4828)(1,50,000) + (0.3103)(-50,000) \\ &= 62,070 + 72,420 - 15,515 = 1,18,975 \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_2 \mid U) &= (0.2069)(8,00,000) + (0.4828)(3,00,000) + (0.3103)(-4,00,000) \\ &= 1,65,520 + 1,44,840 - 1,24,120 = 1,86,240 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Net Expected Payoff (Subtract Survey Cost ₹30,000) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EPF} &= P(F) \cdot \max(\text{EMV}_{1F}, \text{EMV}_{2F}) + P(U) \cdot \max(\text{EMV}_{1U}, \text{EMV}_{2U}) - \text{Survey Cost} \\ &= (0.42)(6,00,030) + (0.58)(1,86,240) - 30,000 \\ &= 2,52,013 + 1,08,019 - 30,000 \\ &= 3,30,032 \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Decision:** Even **with** the survey, the **large tender** remains optimal under both survey outcomes, but the expected payoff drops from **₹3,60,000** to **₹3,30,032** because the survey costs ₹30,000. In this case, the optimal pre-decision is to **skip the survey** and directly choose $D_2$. --- ## 3.3 Full Analytical Decision Tree (Two-Stage) | Stage | Node Type | Branches | Probabilities | Roll-back Value (₹) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | 0 | Decision | Survey / No Survey | — | Compare ₹3,30,032 vs ₹3,60,000 | | 1a | Chance | F (0.42) / U (0.58) | Conditional | — | | 2a (F) | Decision | $D_1$ / $D_2$ | $D_2$ best | 6,00,030 | | 2a (U) | Decision | $D_1$ / $D_2$ | $D_2$ best | 1,86,240 | | 1b | Decision | $D_1$ / $D_2$ | $D_2$ best | 3,60,000 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Decision Tree — Two-Stage with Survey ```mermaid flowchart TD root["DECISION: Survey?"]:::dec s1["Take Survey
Cost = Rs 30000"]:::dec s2["Skip Survey"]:::dec cF["CHANCE: Survey Result"]:::chance cU["CHANCE: Survey Result"]:::chance f1["FAVOURABLE F
P=0.42"]:::chance u1["UNFAVOURABLE U
P=0.58"]:::chance dF1["DECISION: Tender Choice"]:::dec dF2["DECISION: Tender Choice"]:::dec dU1["DECISION: Tender Choice"]:::dec dU2["DECISION: Tender Choice"]:::dec noDec["DECISION: Tender Choice"]:::dec p11["Small: Rs 240485"]:::pay p12["Large: Rs 600030
OPTIMAL"]:::pay p21["Small: Rs 118975"]:::pay p22["Large: Rs 186240
OPTIMAL"]:::pay np1["Small: Rs 170000"]:::pay np2["Large: Rs 360000
OPTIMAL"]:::pay root --> s1 root --> s2 s1 --> cF s1 --> cU cF --> f1 cF --> u1 f1 --> dF1 f1 --> dF2 u1 --> dU1 u1 --> dU2 dF1 --> p11 dF1 --> p12 dU1 --> p21 dU1 --> p22 s2 --> noDec noDec --> np1 noDec --> np2 classDef dec fill:#FFE5B4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef chance fill:#B4D4FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef pay fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Squares (orange) are decision nodes, circles (blue) are chance nodes, and green nodes are terminal payoffs. The roll-back process starts at the right and moves left, retaining only the branches yielding the maximum EMV. ## 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — EVPI Computation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Raw Payoff
Matrix V_ij"]:::input B["Prior
Probabilities P theta"]:::input C["EMV Calculator
E_i = sum P V"]:::proc D["Optimal EMV
= max E_i"]:::out E["Regret Matrix
R_ij = col_max - V_ij"]:::proc F["EOL Calculator
EOL_i = sum P R_ij"]:::proc G["Min EOL
= min EOL_i"]:::out H["EVPI = EOL min"]:::out I["Bayes Update
Posterior P theta E"]:::proc A --> C B --> C C --> D A --> E B --> F E --> F F --> G G --> H I --> B classDef input fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef proc fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef out fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Comparison of Decision Criteria (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD S{"Is probability
data available?"}:::start P1["Optimistic
Maximax / Minimax"]:::crit P2["Pessimistic
Maximin / Minimax Regret"]:::crit P3["Hurwicz alpha
Weighted Criterion"]:::crit P4["Laplace
Equal Likelihood"]:::crit P5["EMV / EOL
Expected Value"]:::crit P6["Apply Bayes
Sequential Tree"]:::crit END["Optimal
Decision d*"]:::end S -- No --> P1 S -- No --> P2 S -- No --> P3 S -- No --> P4 S -- Yes --> P5 S -- Yes, sequential --> P6 P1 --> END P2 --> END P3 --> END P4 --> END P5 --> END P6 --> END classDef start fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef crit fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px classDef end fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` > **Define Expected Monetary Value (EMV). How is it used as a decision criterion under risk?** **Model Answer:** EMV is the weighted average of payoffs of a decision alternative, where the weights are the probabilities of the corresponding states of nature. Mathematically, $\text{EMV}(D_i) = \sum_{j} P(\theta_j) \cdot V_{ij}$. **Decision Rule:** Select the alternative $D_i$ that yields the **maximum EMV** (for profit problems) or **minimum EMV** (for cost problems). The criterion is appropriate only when the decision-maker is **risk-neutral** and the decision can be **repeated** under stationary probabilistic conditions. *(3 marks)* --- > **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` > **Distinguish between a decision node and a chance node in a decision tree.** **Model Answer:** A **decision node** (drawn as a square) is a point where the decision-maker has full control and chooses among two or more alternatives. A **chance node** (drawn as a circle) is a point of uncertainty where the outcome is determined by nature; each branch emerging from it is labelled with its **probability of occurrence**, and the sum of branch probabilities must equal **1**. Payoffs are evaluated at the tips of the tree, and values are rolled back to the root. *(3 marks)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) > **Q3. Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]` > A KTU engineering graduate must choose between **three job offers** with the following payoff matrix (in ₹ lakhs per annum), given three possible economic scenarios: | Decision | Boom ($p=0.3$) | Stable ($p=0.5$) | Recession ($p=0.2$) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | $D_1$: IT Startup | 12 | 6 | $-2$ | | $D_2$: PSU Job | 5 | 5 | 5 | | $D_3$: Higher Studies | $-1$ | 8 | 14 | **(a)** Compute the EMV of each alternative and identify the optimal decision. **(b)** Construct the opportunity-loss (regret) table and determine the optimal decision using the **minimization of EOL** criterion. Verify that the two criteria yield the same choice, and compute the **EVPI**. ### Solution #### Part (a) — EMV Criterion (7 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1) &= (0.3)(12) + (0.5)(6) + (0.2)(-2) \\ &= 3.6 + 3.0 - 0.4 = 6.2 \text{ lakhs} \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_2) &= (0.3)(5) + (0.5)(5) + (0.2)(5) \\ &= 1.5 + 2.5 + 1.0 = 5.0 \text{ lakhs} \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_3) &= (0.3)(-1) + (0.5)(8) + (0.2)(14) \\ &= -0.3 + 4.0 + 2.8 = 6.5 \text{ lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Optimal:** $d^* = D_3$ (Higher Studies), with $\text{EMV}^* = 6.5$ lakhs. #### Part (b) — EOL Criterion & EVPI (7 marks) **Step 1 — Build the Regret Matrix.** For each state, subtract every payoff from the column maximum. | State | Max Payoff | $D_1$ Regret | $D_2$ Regret | $D_3$ Regret | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Boom | 12 | $12-12=0$ | $12-5=7$ | $12-(-1)=13$ | | Stable | 8 | $8-6=2$ | $8-5=3$ | $8-8=0$ | | Recession | 14 | $14-(-2)=16$ | $14-5=9$ | $14-14=0$ | **Step 2 — Compute EOLs.** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EOL}(D_1) &= (0.3)(0) + (0.5)(2) + (0.2)(16) = 0 + 1.0 + 3.2 = 4.2 \\[4pt] \text{EOL}(D_2) &= (0.3)(7) + (0.5)(3) + (0.2)(9) = 2.1 + 1.5 + 1.8 = 5.4 \\[4pt] \text{EOL}(D_3) &= (0.3)(13) + (0.5)(0) + (0.2)(0) = 3.9 + 0 + 0 = 3.9 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Optimal Decision.** $d^* = \arg\min \{\text{EOL}\} = D_3$ with $\text{EOL}^* = 3.9$ lakhs. **Step 4 — EVPI.** $$ \text{EVPI} = \text{EOL}^* = 3.9 \text{ lakhs} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Equivalence Check:** The expected payoff with perfect information equals $\sum P(\theta_j) \cdot \max_i V_{ij} = (0.3)(12) + (0.5)(8) + (0.2)(14) = 10.4$. Then $\text{EVPI} = 10.4 - 6.5 = 3.9$ lakhs. ✔ The two methods agree. --- > **Q4. Question B** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]` > A manufacturer in Kochi is considering producing a new electronic gadget. The decisions and payoffs (₹ in lakhs) are: | Decision | High Demand ($p=0.5$) | Low Demand ($p=0.5$) | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | $D_1$: Produce 1000 units | 30 | 10 | | $D_2$: Produce 500 units | 20 | 15 | **(a)** Construct the decision tree, compute EMVs, and recommend the best course of action. **(b)** A market research study costing ₹2 lakhs can be conducted. The conditional probabilities are: | Study Result | $P(\cdot \mid \text{High})$ | $P(\cdot \mid \text{Low})$ | | :--- | :---: | :---: | | Positive (Pos) | 0.8 | 0.3 | | Negative (Neg) | 0.2 | 0.7 | Use **Bayes' theorem** to update probabilities and determine whether the study should be undertaken. ### Solution #### Part (a) — Single-Stage EMV (7 marks) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1) &= (0.5)(30) + (0.5)(10) = 15 + 5 = 20 \text{ lakhs} \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_2) &= (0.5)(20) + (0.5)(15) = 10 + 7.5 = 17.5 \text{ lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Optimal:** $D_1$ (Produce 1000 units) with $\text{EMV} = 20$ lakhs. #### Part (b) — Two-Stage Bayesian Tree (7 marks) **Marginal probabilities:** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{Pos}) &= (0.8)(0.5) + (0.3)(0.5) = 0.40 + 0.15 = 0.55 \\ P(\text{Neg}) &= 1 - 0.55 = 0.45 \end{aligned} $$ **Posterior probabilities (Bayes):** $$ \begin{aligned} P(\text{High} \mid \text{Pos}) &= \frac{0.8 \times 0.5}{0.55} = 0.7273 \\ P(\text{Low} \mid \text{Pos}) &= \frac{0.3 \times 0.5}{0.55} = 0.2727 \\[4pt] P(\text{High} \mid \text{Neg}) &= \frac{0.2 \times 0.5}{0.45} = 0.2222 \\ P(\text{Low} \mid \text{Neg}) &= \frac{0.7 \times 0.5}{0.45} = 0.7778 \end{aligned} $$ **Posterior EMVs:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EMV}(D_1 \mid \text{Pos}) &= (0.7273)(30) + (0.2727)(10) = 21.82 + 2.73 = 24.55 \\ \text{EMV}(D_2 \mid \text{Pos}) &= (0.7273)(20) + (0.2727)(15) = 14.55 + 4.09 = 18.64 \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Choose } D_1 \\[4pt] \text{EMV}(D_1 \mid \text{Neg}) &= (0.2222)(30) + (0.7778)(10) = 6.67 + 7.78 = 14.45 \\ \text{EMV}(D_2 \mid \text{Neg}) &= (0.2222)(20) + (0.7778)(15) = 4.44 + 11.67 = 16.11 \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Choose } D_2 \end{aligned} $$ **Expected Net Payoff with Study:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{EPF}_{\text{study}} &= P(\text{Pos}) \cdot \text{EMV}^*(D \mid \text{Pos}) + P(\text{Neg}) \cdot \text{EMV}^*(D \mid \text{Neg}) - \text{Cost} \\ &= (0.55)(24.55) + (0.45)(16.11) - 2 \\ &= 13.50 + 7.25 - 2 = 18.75 \text{ lakhs} \end{aligned} $$ **Decision Rule:** Since $\text{EPF}_{\text{study}} = 18.75 < 20 = \text{EMV}(D_1)$, the **study should NOT be undertaken**; go directly with $D_1$. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to subtract the survey cost** in the two-stage tree. Always state the **net** expected payoff, not the gross. > 2. **Not verifying** $\sum P(\theta_j) = 1$ for both prior and posterior distributions. Marks are lost for sloppy arithmetic. > 3. **Confusing** the EMV and EOL criteria — they are equivalent only when the payoff table is **transformed consistently**. State which one you used. > 4. **Mis-labeling nodes** as "decision" vs "chance" in the tree diagram. A common error is to attach probabilities to decision branches. > 5. **Rounding errors** in posterior probabilities: keep at least **4 decimal places** until the final EMV step to avoid cascading errors. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Decision Tree Expected Value Criterion** - **EMV formula:** $E_i = \sum_{j} P(\theta_j) \cdot V_{ij}$ — always verify $\sum P(\theta_j) = 1$. - **Decision rule:** Maximize EMV for **profit** problems; minimize EMV for **cost** problems. - **Regret table:** Column-wise max, then subtract each payoff. EOL = weighted sum of regrets. - **EOL rule:** Minimize EOL; equivalent to maximizing EMV on the original payoff table. - **EVPI:** $EVPI = \text{EOL}_{\min} = \sum P(\theta_j) \cdot \max_i V_{ij} - \text{EMV}^*$. - **Bayes' theorem:** $P(\theta_j \mid E) = \frac{P(E \mid \theta_j) P(\theta_j)}{\sum_k P(E \mid \theta_k) P(\theta_k)}$. - **Two-stage tree rule:** Subtract the survey cost from the gross expected payoff before comparing with the single-stage EMV. - **Roll-back:** Always compute from **right to left**; at each decision node, retain only the branch with the best (max for profit, min for cost) EMV. - **Symbols:** Decision = square □, Chance = circle ○, Payoff tip = triangle △. - **Risk profile:** EMV assumes **risk-neutrality** and **stationary probabilities**; the criterion is unsuitable for a single, non-repeatable, high-stakes decision. - **KTU favourite topics:** Multi-stage Bayesian trees, EVPI calculation, equivalence of EMV and EOL. - **Unit check:** Probabilities are dimensionless; payoffs and EMVs are in **monetary units** (₹, $, etc.). - **Common traps:** (i) summing EMVs across alternatives is meaningless, (ii) EMV ≠ actual outcome, only long-run average, (iii) forgetting to net out sampling cost. ---

topics.title: Variants of the Expected Value Criterion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEITT415 - OPERATIONS RESEARCH
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Variants of the Expected Value Criterion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In **Decision Theory under Risk**, the **Expected Value Criterion** is a probabilistic decision-making rule used when a decision-maker must choose an alternative whose payoff depends on an uncertain state of nature, but the **probabilities of these states are known or can be estimated**. The criterion has several *variants* that refine or supplement the basic Expected Monetary Value (EMV) approach, allowing the analyst to quantify the value of information, evaluate opportunity loss, and assess the efficiency of sampling.

The principal **Variants of the Expected Value Criterion** covered in the KTU 2024 syllabus are:

1. **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** — the basic expected pay-off rule.
2. **Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)** — the minimisation-of-regret variant.
3. **Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)** — the ceiling of performance.
4. **Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)** — the maximum price worth paying for certainty.
5. **Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)** — the value of additional (sample/test) data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These five variants together form the analytical backbone of decision-making under risk. EMV and EOL *rank* alternatives, whereas EVwPI, EVPI, and EVSI *price* information.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **farmer** deciding whether to plant **Rice, Wheat, or Maize** before the monsoon arrives. You cannot control the rainfall (the *state of nature*), but you have historical data that says: "70 % of the time, rainfall is normal; 20 % it floods; 10 % there is a drought."

- **EMV** is like calculating the *average harvest value* for each crop, weighted by these rainfall chances.
- **EOL** is like asking: "For every crop, how much *do I regret* not having chosen the best crop *for that particular rainfall pattern*?" You then pick the crop that *minimises* your average regret.
- **EVPI** answers: "How much extra profit would I have earned if, *somehow*, a divine being told me the exact rainfall *before* I planted?" That extra profit is the price at which buying a *perfect weather forecast* becomes worthwhile.
- **EVSI** is the same question, but for an *imperfect (sample) forecast* — say a meteorological test that is 80 % accurate.

> [!NOTE]
> The Expected Value Criterion only works under **risk** (known probabilities). Under total **uncertainty** (unknown probabilities), criteria like Maximin, Minimax Regret, Hurwicz, or Laplace are used instead — these belong to a separate section of Module 4.

## 1.3 Key Metrics & Constants

- **Probability values** are dimensionless numbers in **[0, 1]**.
- **Pay-off units** are problem-specific (₹, $, tonnes, utility points).
- The general expected-value operator for action $a_i$ and state $\theta_j$ is the **summation operator $\Sigma$** combined with the **probability $P(\theta_j)$** as a weight.
- For the **discount/utility** interpretation, the von Neumann-Morgenstern utility function $U(x)$ is applied, but the linear pay-off form (monetary) is what is used in the KTU exam context.
- **Efficiency of information** is reported as a **percentage (%)**.
- The **standard efficiency bound is 100 %**, attained only by *perfect* information.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

The expected-value framework assumes a **pay-off matrix** with $m$ alternatives and $n$ states of nature:

$$V = \big[ v_{ij} \big]_{m \times n}, \quad v_{ij} = \text{pay-off of action } a_i \text{ when state } \theta_j \text{ occurs.}$$

### Variant 1 — Expected Monetary Value (EMV)
- **Why?** Combines monetary outcomes with their likelihoods into a single comparable index.
- **How?** Multiply each pay-off in row $a_i$ by the probability of its state, then sum across the row.
- **Decision Rule:** Select $a_i^*$ such that $\text{EMV}(a_i^*) = \max_i \big\{ \text{EMV}(a_i) \big\}$.

### Variant 2 — Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)
- **Why?** Removes the influence of uncontrollable states by focusing on *relative* foregone gain.
- **How?** Build the *regret table* $R = [r_{ij}]$ where $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$. Then compute the expected regret for each row.
- **Decision Rule:** Select $a_i^{**}$ such that $\text{EOL}(a_i^{**}) = \min_i \big\{ \text{EOL}(a_i) \big\}$.
- **Key Property:** $\min \text{EOL} \equiv \max \text{EMV}$ when both criteria are correctly applied — this is the **Fundamental Theorem of Decision Theory**.

### Variant 3 — Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)
- **Why?** Establishes an *upper benchmark* of what could be achieved with complete certainty.
- **How?** For each state $\theta_j$, pick the *best* pay-off in that column, then take the probability-weighted sum.

### Variant 4 — Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)
- **Why?** Quantifies the *monetary ceiling* a rational decision-maker should pay to eliminate uncertainty.
- **How?** Subtract the best EMV (without information) from EVwPI.

### Variant 5 — Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)
- **Why?** Real-world information is *imperfect*; a meteorological test, market survey, or pilot study is rarely 100 % accurate.
- **How?** Revise the prior probabilities using **Bayes' Theorem** to obtain posterior probabilities conditioned on each sample outcome, compute the new best EMV, and subtract the original best EMV.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Variant | Formula | Decision Rule | Output Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EMV** | $\text{EMV}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \, v_{ij}$ | Choose $a_i$ that **maximises** EMV | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **Regret** | $r_{ij} = \max_k v_{kj} - v_{ij}$ | Compute column-wise | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **EOL** | $\text{EOL}(a_i) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \, r_{ij}$ | Choose $a_i$ that **minimises** EOL | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **EVwPI** | $\text{EVwPI} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} P(\theta_j) \, \big( \max_i v_{ij} \big)$ | Always computed as a benchmark | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **EVPI** | $\text{EVPI} = \text{EVwPI} - \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i)$ | Always non-negative | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **EVSI** | $\text{EVSI} = \text{EMV}^*_{\text{posterior}} - \text{EMV}^*_{\text{prior}}$ | Computed after Bayesian revision | Same as pay-off (₹) |
| **Efficiency** | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{EVSI}}{\text{EVPI}} \times 100 \,\%$ | Must satisfy $0 \le \eta \le 100$ | Percent (%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Equivalence identity:** $\text{EVPI} = \min_i \text{EOL}(a_i)$. Mastering this single identity instantly verifies your EMV and EOL calculations — a high-yield KTU exam shortcut.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Construction Industry:** EMV ranks bidding strategies; EVPI quantifies the price of a thorough soil survey.
- **Manufacturing & Inventory:** EVSI justifies the cost of a market research pilot before launching a new product line.
- **Energy Sector:** EMV compares the financial merits of nuclear vs. solar vs. wind investments under demand uncertainty.
- **Software Project Management:** EOL highlights opportunity cost of selecting the wrong SDLC model.
- **Civil Engineering:** EVPI helps decide whether to invest in detailed geological investigation before tunnel excavation.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU-Style Worked Example

A start-up firm in Kerala must decide on the scale of a new cold-storage facility. Three alternatives and two states of nature (demand) are under consideration. The pay-off matrix (in ₹ lakhs) is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
 & \theta_1 \;(\text{High Demand}) & \theta_2 \;(\text{Low Demand}) \\ \hline
a_1 \;(\text{Large facility}) & 200 & -50 \\
a_2 \;(\text{Small facility}) & 100 & 50 \\
a_3 \;(\text{No facility}) & 0 & 0 \\
\end{array}
$$

The prior probabilities are $P(\theta_1) = 0.6$ and $P(\theta_2) = 0.4$.

### Step 1 — Compute Expected Monetary Values (EMV)

For action $a_1$ (Large facility), we multiply each pay-off by its respective state probability and sum.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(a_1) &= P(\theta_1) \cdot v_{11} + P(\theta_2) \cdot v_{12} \\
&= (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(-50) \\
&= 120 - 20 \\
&= 100 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For action $a_2$ (Small facility), we apply the same operation across the second row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(a_2) &= (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(50) \\
&= 60 + 20 \\
&= 80 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

For action $a_3$ (No facility), the pay-offs are zero in both states, so the EMV is also zero.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(a_3) &= (0.6)(0) + (0.4)(0) \\
&= 0 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **EMV decision rule** selects the alternative with the maximum EMV.

$$
\max \big\{ \text{EMV}(a_1), \text{EMV}(a_2), \text{EMV}(a_3) \big\} = \max \{ 100, 80, 0 \} = 100.
$$

**Decision under EMV:** Choose $a_1$ (Large facility) with an expected profit of **₹ 100 lakhs**.

### Step 2 — Construct the Regret (Opportunity-Loss) Table

The regret in cell $(i, j)$ is the difference between the **best pay-off in column $j$** and the actual pay-off in that cell. The column maximum for $\theta_1$ is 200 (from $a_1$), and for $\theta_2$ it is 50 (from $a_2$).

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_{11} &= \max_k v_{k1} - v_{11} = 200 - 200 = 0, \\
r_{12} &= \max_k v_{k2} - v_{12} = 50 - (-50) = 100, \\
r_{21} &= 200 - 100 = 100, \\
r_{22} &= 50 - 50 = 0, \\
r_{31} &= 200 - 0 = 200, \\
r_{32} &= 50 - 0 = 50.
\end{aligned}
$$

The complete regret table is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
 & \theta_1 & \theta_2 \\ \hline
a_1 & 0 & 100 \\
a_2 & 100 & 0 \\
a_3 & 200 & 50 \\
\end{array}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Expected Opportunity Loss (EOL)

The expected regret is the probability-weighted sum of regrets in each row.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EOL}(a_1) &= (0.6)(0) + (0.4)(100) = 40 \text{ lakhs,} \\
\text{EOL}(a_2) &= (0.6)(100) + (0.4)(0) = 60 \text{ lakhs,} \\
\text{EOL}(a_3) &= (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(50) = 140 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **EOL decision rule** selects the alternative with the *minimum* expected opportunity loss.

$$
\min \{ 40, 60, 140 \} = 40.
$$

**Decision under EOL:** Choose $a_1$ (Large facility) — it is consistent with the EMV verdict, confirming the fundamental equivalence $\text{EVPI} = \min \text{EOL} = 40$ lakhs.

### Step 4 — Compute Expected Value with Perfect Information (EVwPI)

If the decision-maker knew the state of nature in advance, they would pick the maximum pay-off in each column. The expected value of *that* oracle-guided strategy is EVwPI.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVwPI} &= P(\theta_1) \cdot \max_i v_{i1} + P(\theta_2) \cdot \max_i v_{i2} \\
&= (0.6)(200) + (0.4)(50) \\
&= 120 + 20 \\
&= 140 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute Expected Value of Perfect Information (EVPI)

The EVPI is the difference between the ceiling (EVwPI) and the best achievable EMV without any extra information.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVPI} &= \text{EVwPI} - \max_i \text{EMV}(a_i) \\
&= 140 - 100 \\
&= 40 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Verification Check:** $\text{EVPI} = 40 = \min_i \text{EOL}(a_i) = 40$. The identity holds. This is the single most powerful self-check a KTU student can perform in the exam hall.

### Step 6 — Compute Expected Value of Sample Information (EVSI)

Suppose the firm commissions a market survey that yields one of two sample outcomes: $S_1$ (favourable test) or $S_2$ (unfavourable test). The likelihood matrix (probabilities of test outcomes given the true state) is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cc}
 & \theta_1 & \theta_2 \\ \hline
S_1 & 0.8 & 0.3 \\
S_2 & 0.2 & 0.7 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 6a — Prior probability of each sample outcome** (Total Probability Theorem):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_1) &= P(S_1 \mid \theta_1) P(\theta_1) + P(S_1 \mid \theta_2) P(\theta_2) \\
&= (0.8)(0.6) + (0.3)(0.4) \\
&= 0.48 + 0.12 = 0.60.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(S_2) &= P(S_2 \mid \theta_1) P(\theta_1) + P(S_2 \mid \theta_2) P(\theta_2) \\
&= (0.2)(0.6) + (0.7)(0.4) \\
&= 0.12 + 0.28 = 0.40.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6b — Posterior probabilities** (Bayes' Theorem):

For the favourable test outcome $S_1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\theta_1 \mid S_1) &= \frac{P(S_1 \mid \theta_1) P(\theta_1)}{P(S_1)} = \frac{0.48}{0.60} = 0.80, \\
P(\theta_2 \mid S_1) &= \frac{P(S_1 \mid \theta_2) P(\theta_2)}{P(S_1)} = \frac{0.12}{0.60} = 0.20.
\end{aligned}
$$

For the unfavourable test outcome $S_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P(\theta_1 \mid S_2) &= \frac{P(S_2 \mid \theta_1) P(\theta_1)}{P(S_2)} = \frac{0.12}{0.40} = 0.30, \\
P(\theta_2 \mid S_2) &= \frac{P(S_2 \mid \theta_2) P(\theta_2)}{P(S_2)} = \frac{0.28}{0.40} = 0.70.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6c — Posterior EMVs for each sample outcome**:

If $S_1$ is observed, recompute EMVs using posteriors $P(\theta_1 \mid S_1) = 0.8$ and $P(\theta_2 \mid S_1) = 0.2$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(a_1 \mid S_1) &= (0.8)(200) + (0.2)(-50) = 160 - 10 = 150, \\
\text{EMV}(a_2 \mid S_1) &= (0.8)(100) + (0.2)(50) = 80 + 10 = 90, \\
\text{EMV}(a_3 \mid S_1) &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Best action given $S_1$: $a_1$ with posterior EMV = **150 lakhs**.

If $S_2$ is observed, use posteriors $P(\theta_1 \mid S_2) = 0.3$ and $P(\theta_2 \mid S_2) = 0.7$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}(a_1 \mid S_2) &= (0.3)(200) + (0.7)(-50) = 60 - 35 = 25, \\
\text{EMV}(a_2 \mid S_2) &= (0.3)(100) + (0.7)(50) = 30 + 35 = 65, \\
\text{EMV}(a_3 \mid S_2) &= 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

Best action given $S_2$: $a_2$ with posterior EMV = **65 lakhs**.

**Step 6d — Expected EMV with sample information**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EMV}^*_{\text{posterior}} &= P(S_1) \cdot \max \text{EMV}_{\mid S_1} + P(S_2) \cdot \max \text{EMV}_{\mid S_2} \\
&= (0.60)(150) + (0.40)(65) \\
&= 90 + 26 \\
&= 116 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6e — Compute EVSI**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{EVSI} &= \text{EMV}^*_{\text{posterior}} - \text{EMV}^*_{\text{prior}} \\
&= 116 - 100 = 16 \text{ lakhs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6f — Compute Efficiency of Sample Information**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \frac{\text{EVSI}}{\text{EVPI}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{16}{40} \times 100 = 40 \,\%.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6g — Decision rule on whether to buy the sample**:

If the market survey costs less than EVSI = **₹ 16 lakhs**, the firm should *purchase* the survey; otherwise, it should *rely on prior information alone*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Threshold Logic:** The criterion is *buy the information if and only if its cost < EVSI*. The KTU examiner routinely tests this single inequality.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architecture of the Expected-Value-Criterion Family

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical flow, information dependencies, and decision boundaries of the five expected-value variants. It serves as a *mental blueprint* when solving KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Decision Under Risk: Known Probabilities] --> rootB[Construct Payoff Matrix]
    rootB --> rootC[Step 1: Compute EMV for Each Action]
    rootC --> rootD[Step 2: Identify Best EMV]
    rootB --> rootE[Step 3: Build Regret Matrix]
    rootE --> rootF[Step 4: Compute EOL for Each Action]
    rootF --> rootG[Step 5: Identify Minimum EOL]
    rootB --> rootH[Step 6: Compute EVwPI Column Maxima]
    rootH --> rootI[Step 7: Compute EVPI]
    rootI --> rootJ[Step 8: Define Upper Bound for Info Cost]
    rootC --> rootK[Step 9: Bayesian Revision via Bayes Theorem]
    rootK --> rootL[Step 10: Posterior EMV per Sample Outcome]
    rootL --> rootM[Step 11: Compute EVSI]
    rootM --> rootN[Step 12: Efficiency vs EVPI]
    rootN --> rootO[Step 13: Buy Sample Info iff Cost less than EVSI]
    rootD --> rootP[Final Action: max EMV]
    rootG --> rootP
    rootJ --> rootQ[Reference: Minimum EOL equals EVPI]
    rootQ --> rootP
```

## 4.2 Decision-Flow Block Diagram for a Single Problem

The following architecture captures the *operational sequence* a student must follow when faced with a 14-mark "Variants of EV Criterion" problem in the KTU ESE.

```mermaid
graph TD
    inpA[Input: Payoff Matrix and Prior Probabilities] --> prcA[Block 1: Row-wise Probability-weighted Sum]
    prcA --> outA[EMV Vector]
    inpA --> prcB[Block 2: Column-wise Max Extraction]
    prcB --> prcC[Block 3: Subtract each payoff from Column Max]
    prcC --> prcD[Block 4: Row-wise Weighted Sum of Regrets]
    prcD --> outB[EOL Vector]
    outA --> prcE[Block 5: EVwPI equals weighted sum of column maxima]
    prcE --> prcF[Block 6: EVPI equals EVwPI minus Best EMV]
    inpA --> prcG[Block 7: Bayes Theorem with Likelihood Matrix]
    prcG --> prcH[Block 8: Posterior EMV per Sample Outcome]
    prcH --> prcI[Block 9: EVSI equals Posterior Best minus Prior Best]
    prcI --> prcJ[Block 10: Efficiency equals EVSI divided by EVPI times 100]
    prcJ --> decA[Decision: Purchase Sample Info iff Price less than EVSI]
    outA --> finA[Final Decision: Action with max EMV]
    outB --> finA
```

> [!NOTE]
> The two diagrams above are interchangeable study aids: the first is a *conceptual map*, the second is an *executable algorithm*. Together they cover every line item in a 14-mark KTU answer script.
